title: Flip Distance of Triangulations of Convex Polygons / Rotation Distance of Binary Trees is NP-complete
summary:
Flips in triangulations of convex polygons arise in many different settings. They are isomorphic to rotations in binary trees, define edges in the 1-skeleton of the Associahedron and cover relations in the Tamari Lattice.
  The complexity of determining the minimum number of flips that transform one triangulation of a convex point set into another remained a tantalizing open question for many decades. We settle this question by proving that computing shortest flip sequences between triangulations of convex polygons, and therefore also computing the rotation distance of binary trees, is NP-hard.
  For our proof we develop techniques for flip sequences of triangulations whose counterparts were introduced for the study of flip sequences of non-crossing spanning trees by Bjerkevik, Kleist, Ueckerdt, and Vogtenhuber~[SODA25] and Bjerkevik, Dorfer, Kleist, Ueckerdt, and Vogtenhuber~[SoCG26].

AI.tldr: NP-hardness of computing shortest flip sequences between triangulations of convex polygons; also NP-hard for rotation distance of binary trees; advances via flip-sequence techniques inspired by recent non-crossing spanning tree work and connects to Associahedron/Tamari Lattice.
AI.motivation: Resolve a longstanding open question about the computational complexity of flip distances in convex polygon triangulations and their relation to binary-tree rotations; understanding the inherent difficulty of these combinatorial distances.
AI.method: Develop and adapt flip-sequence techniques for triangulations, building on recent work on flip sequences for non-crossing spanning trees; provide reductions to show NP-hardness and extend the technique to triangulations of convex polygons to establish hardness of the corresponding distances.
AI.result: Proves that computing the minimum number of flips to transform one triangulation of a convex polygon into another is NP-hard; equivalently, computing the rotation distance of binary trees is NP-hard.
AI.conclusion: Hardness of flip-distance problems is established, suggesting limits on exact algorithms and motivating study of approximations or fixed-parameter tractable approaches; the approach connects triangulation flips to broader notions in combinatorics (Associahedron, Tamari Lattice) and leverages recent developments in non-crossing spanning-tree flip sequences.

title: FLIGHT: Fibonacci Lattice-based Inference for Geometric Heading in real-Time
summary: Estimating camera motion from monocular video is a fundamental problem in computer vision, central to tasks such as SLAM, visual odometry, and structure-from-motion. Existing methods that recover the camera's heading under known rotation, whether from an IMU or an optimization algorithm, tend to perform well in low-noise, low-outlier conditions, but often decrease in accuracy or become computationally expensive as noise and outlier levels increase. To address these limitations, we propose a novel generalization of the Hough transform on the unit sphere (S(2)) to estimate the camera's heading. First, the method extracts correspondences between two frames and generates a great circle of directions compatible with each pair of correspondences. Then, by discretizing the unit sphere using a Fibonacci lattice as bin centers, each great circle casts votes for a range of directions, ensuring that features unaffected by noise or dynamic objects vote consistently for the correct motion direction. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate that the proposed method is on the Pareto frontier of accuracy versus efficiency. Additionally, experiments on SLAM show that the proposed method reduces RMSE by correcting the heading during camera pose initialization.

AI.tldr: Robust monocular heading estimation via a spherical Hough transform; great-circle voting on a Fibonacci-discretized S^2 yields accurate, efficient heading estimates even with noise and outliers, improving SLAM initialization.
AI.motivation: Heading estimation in monocular VO/SfM is sensitive to noise and outliers; existing methods under known rotation degrade with higher noise; robust heading estimation can improve downstream SLAM robustness and performance.
AI.method: From frame pairs, extract correspondences and derive a great circle of directions compatible with each correspondence pair. Discretize the unit sphere S^2 using a Fibonacci lattice as bin centers. Each great circle casts votes across a range of directions, so features unaffected by noise or dynamic objects vote for the correct motion direction. The heading is estimated from the resulting vote distribution.
AI.result: Evaluated on three datasets, the method sits on the Pareto frontier of accuracy vs efficiency. In SLAM experiments, correcting the heading during camera pose initialization reduces RMSE.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a robust, efficient heading estimator for monocular motion based on a generalized Hough transform on S^2; demonstrates robustness to noise/outliers and practical benefit for SLAM initialization.

title: Enabling clinical use of foundation models in histopathology
summary: Foundation models in histopathology are expected to facilitate the development of high-performing and generalisable deep learning systems. However, current models capture not only biologically relevant features, but also pre-analytic and scanner-specific variation that bias the predictions of task-specific models trained from the foundation model features. Here we show that introducing novel robustness losses during training of downstream task-specific models reduces sensitivity to technical variability. A purpose-designed comprehensive experimentation setup with 27,042 WSIs from 6155 patients is used to train thousands of models from the features of eight popular foundation models for computational pathology. In addition to a substantial improvement in robustness, we observe that prediction accuracy improves by focusing on biologically relevant features. Our approach successfully mitigates robustness issues of foundation models for computational pathology without retraining the foundation models themselves, enabling development of robust computational pathology models applicable to real-world data in routine clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Introducing robustness losses during downstream training of features from foundation models in histopathology improves robustness to technical variability and shifts predictions toward biologically relevant features, without retraining foundation models.
AI.motivation: Combat the bias from pre-analytic and scanner-related variation embedded in foundation-model features that harm task-specific predictions.
AI.method: Train thousands of downstream models using eight foundation-model features with novel robustness losses; evaluate on a large-scale dataset of 27,042 WSIs from 6,155 patients; comprehensive experimentation across combinations.
AI.result: Notable robustness gains; accuracy improves as models focus on biologically relevant features; approach eliminates need to retrain foundation models, enabling robust models for real-world clinical practice.
AI.conclusion: Robustness issues of foundation models in computational pathology can be mitigated via targeted robustness losses during downstream training, enabling robust, reusable models without modifying foundation models.

title: SODA-CitrON: Static Object Data Association by Clustering Multi-Modal Sensor Detections Online
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures; Submitted to the 2026 International Conference on Information Fusion (FUSION 2026). Under review
summary: The online fusion and tracking of static objects from heterogeneous sensor detections is a fundamental problem in robotics, autonomous systems, and environmental mapping. Although classical data association approaches such as JPDA are well suited for dynamic targets, they are less effective for static objects observed intermittently and with heterogeneous uncertainties, where motion models provide minimal discriminative with respect to clutter. In this paper, we propose a novel method for static object data association by clustering multi-modal sensor detections online (SODA-CitrON), while simultaneously estimating positions and maintaining persistent tracks for an unknown number of objects. The proposed unsupervised machine learning approach operates in a fully online manner and handles temporally uncorrelated and multi-sensor measurements. Additionally, it has a worst-case loglinear complexity in the number of sensor detections while providing full output explainability. We evaluate the proposed approach in different Monte Carlo simulation scenarios and compare it against state-of-the-art methods, including Bayesian filtering, DBSTREAM clustering, and JPDA. The results demonstrate that SODA-CitrON consistently outperforms the compared methods in terms of F1 score, position RMSE, MOTP, and MOTA in the static object mapping scenarios studied.

AI.tldr: Introduces SODA-CitrON, an online unsupervised clustering method for static object data association using multi-modal sensor detections. It estimates positions, maintains persistent tracks for an unknown object count, runs online with log-linear worst-case complexity, and provides explainability. It outperforms JPDA, DBSTREAM, and Bayesian filtering in static mapping tasks across F1, position RMSE, MOTP, and MOTA.
AI.motivation: Static objects observed intermittently with heterogeneous uncertainties challenge traditional data association (e.g., JPDA) which rely on motion models and dynamic targets. There is a need for online, unsupervised clustering-based tracking that can handle multiple sensors, unknown object counts, and clutter without strong dynamics assumptions.
AI.method: Online, unsupervised clustering approach (SODA-CitrON) that clusters detections from multiple sensors to jointly estimate object positions and maintain persistent tracks. Operates fully online on temporally uncorrelated, multi-sensor data, with worst-case log-linear complexity in the number of detections and explicit output explainability.
AI.result: Through Monte Carlo simulations, SODA-CitrON consistently outperforms Bayesian filtering, DBSTREAM, and JPDA in static object mapping scenarios, achieving higher F1 scores, lower position RMSE, and better MOTP and MOTA.
AI.conclusion: SODA-CitrON offers a scalable, explainable online solution for static object data association under heterogeneous sensing, suitable for environments where object count is unknown and observations are intermittent.

title: To Deceive is to Teach? Forging Perceptual Robustness via Adversarial Reinforcement Learning
summary: Despite their impressive capabilities, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit perceptual fragility when confronted with visually complex scenes. This weakness stems from a reliance on finite training datasets, which are prohibitively expensive to scale and impose a ceiling on model robustness. We introduce \textbf{AOT-SFT}, a large-scale adversarial dataset for bootstrapping MLLM robustness. Building on this, we propose \textbf{AOT (Adversarial Opponent Training)}, a self-play framework that forges MLLM robustness by creating its own training data. Our method orchestrates a co-evolution between an image-editing Attacker and a Defender MLLM, where the Attacker generates a diverse and dynamic curriculum of image manipulations, forcing the Defender to adapt and improve. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AOT enhances the Defender's perceptual robustness and reduces hallucinations, establishing a scalable paradigm for training more reliable MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Proposes AOT-SFT adversarial dataset and AOT self-play framework to improve MLLM robustness; an Attacker creates dynamic image manipulations to train a Defender, yielding enhanced perceptual robustness and fewer hallucinations.
AI.motivation: MLLMs show perceptual fragility in complex scenes due to dependence on finite, costly-to-scale training data; a scalable method is needed to boost robustness and reduce hallucinations without prohibitive data collection.
AI.method: Introduce AOT-SFT, a large-scale adversarial dataset for bootstrapping MLLM robustness. Propose AOT (Adversarial Opponent Training) : a self-play framework where an image-editing Attacker and a Defender MLLM co-evolve. The Attacker generates a diverse curriculum of image manipulations, forcing the Defender to adapt; iterative self-play refines both models.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that AOT enhances the Defender’s perceptual robustness and reduces hallucinations, demonstrating a scalable paradigm for training more reliable MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: AOT provides a scalable, self-play adversarial training paradigm for improving MLLM robustness against perceptual perturbations and hallucinations; the approach could generalize to broader multimodal robustness challenges and data-efficient training.

title: Graph Your Way to Inspiration: Integrating Co-Author Graphs with Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Model Based Scientific Idea Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate potential in the field of scientific idea generation. However, the generated results often lack controllable academic context and traceable inspiration pathways. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a scientific idea generation system called GYWI, which combines author knowledge graphs with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to form an external knowledge base to provide controllable context and trace of inspiration path for LLMs to generate new scientific ideas. We first propose an author-centered knowledge graph construction method and inspiration source sampling algorithms to construct external knowledge base. Then, we propose a hybrid retrieval mechanism that is composed of both RAG and GraphRAG to retrieve content with both depth and breadth knowledge. It forms a hybrid context. Thirdly, we propose a Prompt optimization strategy incorporating reinforcement learning principles to automatically guide LLMs optimizing the results based on the hybrid context. To evaluate the proposed approaches, we constructed an evaluation dataset based on arXiv (2018-2023). This paper also develops a comprehensive evaluation method including empirical automatic assessment in multiple-choice question task, LLM-based scoring, human evaluation, and semantic space visualization analysis. The generated ideas are evaluated from the following five dimensions: novelty, feasibility, clarity, relevance, and significance. We conducted experiments on different LLMs including GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, and Gemini 2.5. Experimental results show that GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in multiple metrics such as novelty, reliability, and relevance.

AI.tldr: GYWI is an author-knowledge-graph–driven, hybrid RAG framework with prompt optimization that aims to provide controllable context and traceable inspiration for scientific idea generation, showing improvements over several LLMs on an arXiv-based dataset.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of controllable academic context and traceable inspiration in LLM-generated scientific ideas. Proposes external knowledge base built from author knowledge graphs and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to guide LLMs and reveal inspiration pathways.
AI.method: 1) Build author-centered knowledge graphs; 2) develop inspiration source sampling; 3) implement a hybrid retrieval mechanism combining RAG and GraphRAG for deep and broad content; 4) propose a prompt optimization strategy with reinforcement-learning–like guidance; 5) evaluate on arXiv (2018–2023) using automatic MCQ, LLM scoring, human evaluation, and semantic space visualization; assess novelty, feasibility, clarity, relevance, and significance; test across GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-8B, Gemini 2.5.
AI.result: GYWI significantly outperforms mainstream LLMs in metrics of novelty, reliability, and relevance on the constructed evaluation tasks and datasets.
AI.conclusion: External knowledge base with hybrid retrieval and prompt optimization enables controllable context and traceable inspiration for LLM-based scientific idea generation, yielding higher novelty and relevance and better alignment with scholarly context.

title: Optimizing Neural Network Architecture for Medical Image Segmentation Using Monte Carlo Tree Search
summary: This paper proposes a novel medical image segmentation framework, MNAS-Unet, which combines Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and Neural Architecture Search (NAS). MNAS-Unet dynamically explores promising network architectures through MCTS, significantly enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of architecture search. It also optimizes the DownSC and UpSC unit structures, enabling fast and precise model adjustments. Experimental results demonstrate that MNAS-Unet outperforms NAS-Unet and other state-of-the-art models in segmentation accuracy on several medical image datasets, including PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS. Furthermore, compared with NAS-Unet, MNAS-Unet reduces the architecture search budget by 54% (early stopping at 139 epochs versus 300 epochs under the same search setting), while achieving a lightweight model with only 0.6M parameters and lower GPU memory consumption, which further improves its practical applicability. These results suggest that MNAS-Unet can improve search efficiency while maintaining competitive segmentation accuracy under practical resource constraints.

AI.tldr: MNAS-Unet uses MCTS-guided NAS to design efficient medical image segmentation networks, achieving higher accuracy with reduced search budget and a compact model, by optimizing DownSC/UpSC units.
AI.motivation: To accelerate neural architecture search for medical image segmentation while producing compact models suitable for practical deployment under limited resources.
AI.method: Integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search with neural architecture search to explore architectures; jointly optimizes DownSC and UpSC units within a U-Net-like framework; evaluates on PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS datasets.
AI.result: Outperforms NAS-Unet and other state-of-the-art models in segmentation accuracy; reduces NAS-Unet's search budget by ~54% (139 vs 300 epochs); yields a lightweight model with 0.6M parameters and lower GPU memory usage.
AI.conclusion: MNAS-Unet improves search efficiency and maintains competitive segmentation accuracy under practical resource constraints; suitable for deployment in resource-limited clinical settings.

title: Detection and Recognition: A Pairwise Interaction Framework for Mobile Service Robots
summary: Autonomous mobile service robots, like lawnmowers or cleaning robots, operating in human-populated environments need to reason about local human-human interactions to support safe and socially aware navigation while fulfilling their tasks. For such robots, interaction understanding is not primarily a fine-grained recognition problem, but a perception problem under limited sensing quality and computational resources. Many existing approaches focus on holistic group activity recognition, which often requires complex and large models which may not be necessary for mobile service robots. Others use pairwise interaction methods which commonly rely on skeletal representations but their use in outdoor environments remains challenging. In this work, we argue that pairwise human interaction constitute a minimal yet sufficient perceptual unit for robot-centric social understanding. We study the problem of identifying interacting person pairs and classifying coarse-grained interaction behaviors sufficient for downstream group-level reasoning and service robot decision-making. To this end, we adopt a two-stage framework in which candidate interacting pairs are first identified based on lightweight geometric and motion cues, and interaction types are subsequently classified using a relation network. We evaluate the proposed approach on the JRDB dataset, where it achieves sufficient accuracy with reduced computational cost and model size compared to appearance-based methods. Additional experiments on the Collective Activity Dataset and zero shot test on a lawnmower-collected dataset further illustrate the generality of the proposed framework. These results suggest that pairwise geometric and motion cues provide a practical basis for interaction perception on mobile service robot providing a promising method for integration into mobile robot navigation stacks in future work. Code will be released soon

AI.tldr: Lightweight pairwise interaction perception framework for mobile service robots; identify interacting person pairs via geometric/motion cues and classify coarse interactions with a relation network; efficient and generalizable across datasets.
AI.motivation: To enable safe, socially aware navigation under limited sensing/computation by treating pairwise interactions as the minimal perceptual unit for robot-centric social understanding.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) candidate interacting pairs generated from lightweight geometric and motion cues; (2) interaction type classification using a relation network; evaluated on JRDB, with additional experiments on the Collective Activity Dataset and a zero-shot lawnmower-collected dataset.
AI.result: Achieves sufficient accuracy with reduced computational cost and smaller model size compared to appearance-based methods; demonstrates generalization across datasets and settings.
AI.conclusion: Pairwise geometric and motion cues provide a practical basis for interaction perception in mobile robots; framework is suitable for integration into navigation stacks; code forthcoming.

title: Patient-Centered, Graph-Augmented Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Passive Surveillance for Early Stroke Risk Detection in High-Risk Individuals
summary: Stroke affected millions annually, yet poor symptom recognition often delayed care-seeking. To address risk recognition gap, we developed a passive surveillance system for early stroke risk detection using patient-reported symptoms among individuals with diabetes. Constructing a symptom taxonomy grounded in patients own language and a dual machine learning pipeline (heterogeneous GNN and EN/LASSO), we identified symptom patterns associated with subsequent stroke. We translated findings into a hybrid risk screening system integrating symptom relevance and temporal proximity, evaluated across 3-90 day windows through EHR-based simulations. Under conservative thresholds, intentionally designed to minimize false alerts, the screening system achieved high specificity (1.00) and prevalence-adjusted positive predictive value (1.00), with good sensitivity (0.72), an expected trade-off prioritizing precision, that was highest in 90-day window. Patient-reported language alone supported high-precision, low-burden early stroke risk detection, that could offer a valuable time window for clinical evaluation and intervention for high-risk individuals.

AI.tldr: Passive surveillance using patient-reported symptoms and ML to detect imminent stroke risk in diabetics; achieves high precision and specificity in a 90-day window with reasonable sensitivity.
AI.motivation: Address poor symptom recognition and delay in care for stroke by leveraging patient language and a passive surveillance approach.
AI.method: Construct a symptom taxonomy from patient language; employ a dual ML pipeline (heterogeneous Graph Neural Network + Elastic Net/LASSO); create a hybrid risk screening integrating symptom relevance and temporal proximity; assess performance across 3–90 day windows using EHR-based simulations.
AI.result: Under conservative thresholds, specificity = 1.00; prevalence-adjusted PPV = 1.00; sensitivity = 0.72; optimal performance observed in the 90-day window; high-precision, low-burden early risk detection.
AI.conclusion: Patient-reported language supports high-precision early stroke risk detection and could provide a clinically actionable window for evaluation and intervention in high-risk individuals.

title: FIRE: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Financial Intelligence and Reasoning Evaluation
summary: We introduce FIRE, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate both the theoretical financial knowledge of LLMs and their ability to handle practical business scenarios. For theoretical assessment, we curate a diverse set of examination questions drawn from widely recognized financial qualification exams, enabling evaluation of LLMs deep understanding and application of financial knowledge. In addition, to assess the practical value of LLMs in real-world financial tasks, we propose a systematic evaluation matrix that categorizes complex financial domains and ensures coverage of essential subdomains and business activities. Based on this evaluation matrix, we collect 3,000 financial scenario questions, consisting of closed-form decision questions with reference answers and open-ended questions evaluated by predefined rubrics. We conduct comprehensive evaluations of state-of-the-art LLMs on the FIRE benchmark, including XuanYuan 4.0, our latest financial-domain model, as a strong in-domain baseline. These results enable a systematic analysis of the capability boundaries of current LLMs in financial applications. We publicly release the benchmark questions and evaluation code to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: FIRE is a benchmark to assess both theoretical financial knowledge and practical business scenario handling in LLMs, using exam-style questions and a 3,000-question evaluation matrix; it benchmarks state-of-the-art models (including XuanYuan 4.0) and releases data/code for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between theoretical finance understanding and practical decision-making in LLMs by providing a comprehensive, standardized evaluation framework that covers diverse subdomains and real-world tasks.
AI.method: 1) Compile theory questions from widely recognized financial qualification exams. 2) Develop an evaluation matrix to cover practical financial domains and essential business activities. 3) Assemble 3,000 financial-scenario questions (closed-form with answers and open-ended with rubrics). 4) Evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs (including XuanYuan 4.0) using the benchmark. 5) Publicly release benchmark, questions, and evaluation code.
AI.result: A reproducible benchmark and evaluation workflow that enables systematic analysis of LLMs’ capabilities and limits in finance, with both closed-form and open-ended assessments, and baseline results for comparison.
AI.conclusion: FIRE provides a public, comprehensive platform for assessing financial knowledge and real-world task handling in LLMs, facilitating future research and fair comparisons across models.

title: AeroDGS: Physically Consistent Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Single-Sequence Aerial 4D Reconstruction
summary: Recent advances in 4D scene reconstruction have significantly improved dynamic modeling across various domains. However, existing approaches remain limited under aerial conditions with single-view capture, wide spatial range, and dynamic objects of limited spatial footprint and large motion disparity. These challenges cause severe depth ambiguity and unstable motion estimation, making monocular aerial reconstruction inherently ill-posed. To this end, we present AeroDGS, a physics-guided 4D Gaussian splatting framework for monocular UAV videos. AeroDGS introduces a Monocular Geometry Lifting module that reconstructs reliable static and dynamic geometry from a single aerial sequence, providing a robust basis for dynamic estimation. To further resolve monocular ambiguity, we propose a Physics-Guided Optimization module that incorporates differentiable ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors, transforming ambiguous image cues into physically consistent motion. The framework jointly refines static backgrounds and dynamic entities with stable geometry and coherent temporal evolution. We additionally build a real-world UAV dataset that spans various altitudes and motion conditions to evaluate dynamic aerial reconstruction. Experiments on synthetic and real UAV scenes demonstrate that AeroDGS outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior reconstruction fidelity in dynamic aerial environments.

AI.tldr: AeroDGS presents a monocular UAV 4D reconstruction method using physics-guided Gaussian splatting, introducing Monocular Geometry Lifting and Physics-Guided Optimization to jointly recover static and dynamic geometry under challenging aerial single-view conditions.
AI.motivation: Monocular aerial dynamic reconstruction suffers from depth ambiguity, large motion disparity, and instability, especially with single-view capture, wide spatial range, and objects with limited footprints. A physics-informed framework is needed to produce physically plausible, coherent 4D scenes.
AI.method: A 4D Gaussian splatting framework (AeroDGS) with (1) Monocular Geometry Lifting to recover reliable static and dynamic geometry from a single aerial sequence, and (2) Physics-Guided Optimization incorporating differentiable ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors to enforce physical plausibility while jointly refining static and dynamic elements.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real UAV scenes demonstrate superior reconstruction fidelity compared to state-of-the-art methods, validating the approach and its robustness across dynamic aerial environments.
AI.conclusion: AeroDGS provides a physically informed, monocular 4D reconstruction framework for UAV videos, achieving stable geometry and coherent temporal evolution through explicit priors and joint optimization, supported by a real-world UAV dataset spanning diverse altitudes and motion conditions.

title: Hierarchical Trajectory Planning of Floating-Base Multi-Link Robot for Maneuvering in Confined Environments
comment: Accepted to IEEE T-ASE; DOI pending
summary: Floating-base multi-link robots can change their shape during flight, making them well-suited for applications in confined environments such as autonomous inspection and search and rescue. However, trajectory planning for such systems remains an open challenge because the problem lies in a high-dimensional, constraint-rich space where collision avoidance must be addressed together with kinematic limits and dynamic feasibility. This work introduces a hierarchical trajectory planning framework that integrates global guidance with configuration-aware local optimization. First, we exploit the dual nature of these robots - the root link as a rigid body for guidance and the articulated joints for flexibility - to generate global anchor states that decompose the planning problem into tractable segments. Second, we design a local trajectory planner that optimizes each segment in parallel with differentiable objectives and constraints, systematically enforcing kinematic feasibility and maintaining dynamic feasibility by avoiding control singularities. Third, we implement a complete system that directly processes point-cloud data, eliminating the need for handcrafted obstacle models. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments confirm that this framework enables an articulated aerial robot to exploit its morphology for maneuvering that rigid robots cannot achieve. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first planning framework for floating-base multi-link robots that has been demonstrated on a real robot to generate continuous, collision-free, and dynamically feasible trajectories directly from raw point-cloud inputs, without relying on handcrafted obstacle models.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical planning framework for floating-base multi-link robots that uses global anchor states from the root link and parallel differentiable local optimization, enabling direct-from-point-cloud, collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectories.
AI.motivation: Floating-base articulated aerial robots operate in confined or cluttered environments where high-dimensional, constraint-rich planning must address collision avoidance, kinematic limits, and dynamic feasibility. Existing methods struggle with scalability and reliance on handcrafted obstacle models, motivating a framework that leverages morphology and hierarchical decomposition.
AI.method: A two-tier approach: (1) global guidance uses the root link as a rigid body to generate global anchor states that decompose planning into tractable segments; (2) a local trajectory planner optimizes each segment in parallel with differentiable objectives and constraints, enforcing kinematic feasibility and avoiding control singularities to maintain dynamic feasibility; (3) a complete system that processes raw point-cloud data directly, eliminating handcrafted obstacle models.
AI.result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments show the articulated aerial robot can exploit its morphology to maneuver beyond what rigid robots can achieve. The work claims to be the first planning framework for floating-base multi-link robots demonstrated on a real robot, capable of generating continuous, collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectories directly from raw point-cloud inputs without handcrafted obstacle models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical planning framework delivers end-to-end capability for collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectory generation for floating-base multi-link robots from unstructured sensor data, enabling advanced maneuvers in confined environments and illustrating the practical viability of morphology-aware planning.

title: Improving Spatial Allocation for Energy System Coupling with Graph Neural Networks
summary: In energy system analysis, coupling models with mismatched spatial resolutions is a significant challenge. A common solution is assigning weights to high-resolution geographic units for aggregation, but traditional models are limited by using only a single geospatial attribute. This paper presents an innovative method employing a self-supervised Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network to address this issue. This method models high-resolution geographic units as graph nodes, integrating various geographical features to generate physically meaningful weights for each grid point. These weights enhance the conventional Voronoi-based allocation method, allowing it to go beyond simply geographic proximity by incorporating essential geographic information.In addition, the self-supervised learning paradigm overcomes the lack of accurate ground-truth data. Experimental results demonstrate that applying weights generated by this method to cluster-based Voronoi Diagrams significantly enhances scalability, accuracy, and physical plausibility, while increasing precision compared to traditional methods.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised heterogeneous graph neural network learns physically meaningful weights for high-resolution geographic units to improve Voronoi-based aggregation in energy system models with mismatched spatial resolutions.
AI.motivation: Coupling energy system models across spatial scales is hindered by the need to aggregate high-resolution geographic data. Relying on a single geospatial attribute limits fidelity. A method to learn weights from multiple geographic features can enhance aggregation beyond simple proximity.
AI.method: Model high-resolution units as nodes in a heterogeneous graph with multiple geographic features. Use self-supervised learning to generate weights for each grid point. Integrate these weights into a cluster-based Voronoi diagram to improve allocation beyond pure geographical proximity.
AI.result: Weights generated by the method improve scalability, accuracy, and physical plausibility of Voronoi-based allocations. Experimental results show increased precision over traditional, proximity-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The self-supervised heterogeneous GNN method provides a promising way to address multi-resolution coupling in energy systems, improving allocation fidelity. Further benchmarking and ablation studies are suggested to validate robustness and generalizability.

title: Multi-Level Causal Embeddings
summary: Abstractions of causal models allow for the coarsening of models such that relations of cause and effect are preserved. Whereas abstractions focus on the relation between two models, in this paper we study a framework for causal embeddings which enable multiple detailed models to be mapped into sub-systems of a coarser causal model. We define causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction, and present a generalized notion of consistency. By defining a multi-resolution marginal problem, we showcase the relevance of causal embeddings for both the statistical marginal problem and the causal marginal problem; furthermore, we illustrate its practical use in merging datasets coming from models with different representations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction for mapping multiple detailed causal models into sub-systems of a coarser causal model, defines a generalized consistency notion, and develops a multi-resolution marginal framework with applications to statistical and causal marginal problems and to merging datasets from differently represented models.
AI.motivation: Preserve causal relationships when coarsening models and enable integration of multiple detailed models by embedding them into a common coarse structure; address statistical and causal marginal problems and facilitate merging data across representations.
AI.method: Formalize causal embeddings as a generalization of abstraction, define a generalized consistency criterion, formulate a multi-resolution marginal problem, and illustrate the framework's relevance to marginal problems and data merging across disparate representations.
AI.result: A theoretical framework linking causal embeddings to marginal problems, with demonstrated potential for merging datasets from models with different representations and for understanding how detailed models can be embedded into coarse causal systems.
AI.conclusion: Causal embeddings extend abstraction to a multi-resolution setting, providing a unifying approach for preserving causal relations across models and enabling data fusion from heterogeneous representations in both statistical and causal marginal contexts.

title: Enhancing Renal Tumor Malignancy Prediction: Deep Learning with Automatic 3D CT Organ Focused Attention
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, Accepted at IEEE ISBI 2026
summary: Accurate prediction of malignancy in renal tumors is crucial for informing clinical decisions and optimizing treatment strategies. However, existing imaging modalities lack the necessary accuracy to reliably predict malignancy before surgical intervention. While deep learning has shown promise in malignancy prediction using 3D CT images, traditional approaches often rely on manual segmentation to isolate the tumor region and reduce noise, which enhances predictive performance. Manual segmentation, however, is labor-intensive, costly, and dependent on expert knowledge. In this study, a deep learning framework was developed utilizing an Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss function to modify the attention of image patches so that organ patches attend only to other organ patches. Hence, no segmentation of 3D renal CT images is required at deployment time for malignancy prediction. The proposed framework achieved an AUC of 0.685 and an F1-score of 0.872 on a private dataset from the UF Integrated Data Repository (IDR), and an AUC of 0.760 and an F1-score of 0.852 on the publicly available KiTS21 dataset. These results surpass the performance of conventional models that rely on segmentation-based cropping for noise reduction, demonstrating the frameworks ability to enhance predictive accuracy without explicit segmentation input. The findings suggest that this approach offers a more efficient and reliable method for malignancy prediction, thereby enhancing clinical decision-making in renal cancer diagnosis.

AI.tldr: OFA-based 3D renal tumor malignancy predictor eliminates segmentation at deployment, achieving competitive AUC/F1 on private and KiTS21 datasets and outperforming segmentation-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on manual segmentation for noise reduction in 3D CT malignancy prediction; cut labor, cost, and expert-dependency while enabling end-to-end inference.
AI.method: Introduce Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss to constrain image patch attention to organ patches, enabling end-to-end malignancy prediction on 3D renal CT without segmentation; evaluate on UF IDR private data and KiTS21 dataset, compare to segmentation-cropping approaches.
AI.result: On UF IDR: AUC 0.685, F1 0.872. On KiTS21: AUC 0.760, F1 0.852. Outperforms segmentation-based cropping baselines.
AI.conclusion: OFA-based framework enables efficient, segmentation-free malignancy prediction from 3D renal CT, potentially improving clinical decision-making in renal cancer diagnosis.

title: EgoAVFlow: Robot Policy Learning with Active Vision from Human Egocentric Videos via 3D Flow
summary: Egocentric human videos provide a scalable source of manipulation demonstrations; however, deploying them on robots requires active viewpoint control to maintain task-critical visibility, which human viewpoint imitation often fails to provide due to human-specific priors. We propose EgoAVFlow, which learns manipulation and active vision from egocentric videos through a shared 3D flow representation that supports geometric visibility reasoning and transfers without robot demonstrations. EgoAVFlow uses diffusion models to predict robot actions, future 3D flow, and camera trajectories, and refines viewpoints at test time with reward-maximizing denoising under a visibility-aware reward computed from predicted motion and scene geometry. Real-world experiments under actively changing viewpoints show that EgoAVFlow consistently outperforms prior human-demo-based baselines, demonstrating effective visibility maintenance and robust manipulation without robot demonstrations.

AI.tldr: EgoAVFlow learns manipulation and active vision from egocentric videos using a shared 3D flow representation and diffusion models to predict actions, 3D flow, and camera trajectories, with test-time visibility optimization; it transfers to robots without robot demonstrations and outperforms human-demo baselines under changing viewpoints.
AI.motivation: Human-viewpoint imitation from egocentric data often fails to provide robust visibility due to human priors; robots require active viewpoint control for task-critical visibility and scalable transfer from passive egocentric demonstrations.
AI.method: Introduce a shared 3D flow representation that encodes scene motion and visibility; use diffusion models to jointly predict robot actions, future 3D flow, and camera trajectories; at test time, refine viewpoints via reward-maximizing denoising using a visibility-aware reward derived from predicted motion and scene geometry; training relies on egocentric videos without robot demonstrations.
AI.result: Real-world experiments with actively changing viewpoints show EgoAVFlow consistently outperforms prior baselines based on human demonstrations, achieving better visibility maintenance and robust manipulation without robot demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that manipulation and active vision can be learned from egocentric videos and transferred to robots without robot demonstrations, enabling robust, visibility-aware manipulation under dynamic viewpoints.

title: Zatom-1: A Multimodal Flow Foundation Model for 3D Molecules and Materials
summary: General-purpose 3D chemical modeling encompasses molecules and materials, requiring both generative and predictive capabilities. However, most existing AI approaches are optimized for a single domain (molecules or materials) and a single task (generation or prediction), which limits representation sharing and transfer. We introduce Zatom-1, the first foundation model that unifies generative and predictive learning of 3D molecules and materials. Zatom-1 is a Transformer trained with a multimodal flow matching objective that jointly models discrete atom types and continuous 3D geometries. This approach supports scalable pretraining with predictable gains as model capacity increases, while enabling fast and stable sampling. We use joint generative pretraining as a universal initialization for downstream multi-task prediction of properties, energies, and forces. Empirically, Zatom-1 matches or outperforms specialized baselines on both generative and predictive benchmarks, while reducing the generative inference time by more than an order of magnitude. Our experiments demonstrate positive predictive transfer between chemical domains from joint generative pretraining: modeling materials during pretraining improves molecular property prediction accuracy.

AI.tldr: Unified foundation model Zatom-1 for 3D molecules/materials that jointly learns generation and prediction via multimodal flow matching; scalable, fast sampling, and cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: Address domain-specific, single-task AI models in 3D chemistry/materials literature; enable shared representations and cross-domain transfer.
AI.method: Transformer architecture trained with multimodal flow matching to model discrete atom types and continuous 3D coordinates; scalable pretraining; joint generative pretraining to initialize downstream prediction of properties, energies, forces; fast sampling.
AI.result: Matches or surpasses specialized baselines on generative and predictive tasks; generative inference speed up by >10x; predictive transfer observed across materials and molecules.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a unified 3D chemistry foundation model with cross-domain transfer and scalable gains; joint pretraining provides universal initialization for multi-task objectives.

title: Agent Behavioral Contracts: Formal Specification and Runtime Enforcement for Reliable Autonomous AI Agents
comment: 71 pages, 7 figures, 14 tables. Patent pending. Also available on Zenodo: DOI 10.5281/zenodo.18775393
summary: Traditional software relies on contracts -- APIs, type systems, assertions -- to specify and enforce correct behavior. AI agents, by contrast, operate on prompts and natural language instructions with no formal behavioral specification. This gap is the root cause of drift, governance failures, and frequent project failures in agentic AI deployments. We introduce Agent Behavioral Contracts (ABC), a formal framework that brings Design-by-Contract principles to autonomous AI agents. An ABC contract C = (P, I, G, R) specifies Preconditions, Invariants, Governance policies, and Recovery mechanisms as first-class, runtime-enforceable components. We define (p, delta, k)-satisfaction -- a probabilistic notion of contract compliance that accounts for LLM non-determinism and recovery -- and prove a Drift Bounds Theorem showing that contracts with recovery rate gamma > alpha (the natural drift rate) bound behavioral drift to D* = alpha/gamma in expectation, with Gaussian concentration in the stochastic setting. We establish sufficient conditions for safe contract composition in multi-agent chains and derive probabilistic degradation bounds. We implement ABC in AgentAssert, a runtime enforcement library, and evaluate on AgentContract-Bench, a benchmark of 200 scenarios across 7 models from 6 vendors. Results across 1,980 sessions show that contracted agents detect 5.2-6.8 soft violations per session that uncontracted baselines miss entirely (p < 0.0001, Cohen's d = 6.7-33.8), achieve 88-100% hard constraint compliance, and bound behavioral drift to D* < 0.27 across extended sessions, with 100% recovery for frontier models and 17-100% across all models, at overhead < 10 ms per action.

AI.tldr: Agent Behavioral Contracts (ABC) extend Design-by-Contract to autonomous AI agents by formalizing runtime-enforceable contracts with preconditions, invariants, governance policies, and recovery. They provide a probabilistic satisfaction notion under non-determinism and establish drift bounds, enabling safe composition and practical runtime enforcement with empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between formal software contracts and prompt-based agent behavior to prevent drift, governance failures, and project failures in agentic AI deployments.
AI.method: Define ABC as C=(P, I, G, R) with (p, delta, k)-satisfaction for probabilistic contract compliance; prove Drift Bounds Theorem for gamma>alpha; establish conditions for safe composition; implement AgentAssert runtime library; evaluate on AgentContract-Bench (200 scenarios, 7 models, 6 vendors) with 1,980 sessions.
AI.result: Contractions detect 5.2–6.8 soft violations per session missed by uncontracted baselines; 88–100% hard constraint compliance; drift bound D*<0.27; 100% recovery for frontier models and 17–100% across models; overhead <10 ms per action.
AI.conclusion: ABC provides a formal, enforceable contract framework for autonomous agents, combining theoretical drift guarantees with practical runtime enforcement and strong empirical validation; effective at reducing drift and improving compliance with minimal latency.

title: Vision Transformers Need More Than Registers
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs), when pre-trained on large-scale data, provide general-purpose representations for diverse downstream tasks. However, artifacts in ViTs are widely observed across different supervision paradigms and downstream tasks. Through systematic analysis of artifacts in ViTs, we find that their fundamental mechanisms have yet to be sufficiently elucidated. In this paper, through systematic analysis, we conclude that these artifacts originate from a lazy aggregation behavior: ViT uses semantically irrelevant background patches as shortcuts to represent global semantics, driven by global attention and Coarse-grained semantic supervision. Our solution selectively integrates patch features into the CLS token, reducing the influence of background-dominated shortcuts and consistently improving performance across 12 benchmarks under label-, text-, and self-supervision. We hope this work offers a new perspective on ViT behavior.

AI.tldr: ViTs exhibit background-dominated shortcuts due to lazy aggregation; a patch-feature integration into CLS token mitigates this and yields consistent gains across supervision paradigms.
AI.motivation: Understand why ViTs display artifacts across supervision and tasks and clarify their underlying mechanisms.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of artifacts in ViTs, identifying lazy aggregation where semantically irrelevant background patches serve as shortcuts; propose selective integration of patch features into the CLS token and validate on 12 benchmarks across label-, text-, and self-supervision.
AI.result: Reduced influence of background shortcuts; consistent performance improvements across diverse benchmarks and supervision types.
AI.conclusion: Offers a new perspective on ViT behavior by diagnosing background-driven shortcuts and providing a simple, effective aggregation adjustment to improve robustness.

title: When to Act, Ask, or Learn: Uncertainty-Aware Policy Steering
summary: Policy steering is an emerging way to adapt robot behaviors at deployment-time: a learned verifier analyzes low-level action samples proposed by a pre-trained policy (e.g., diffusion policy) and selects only those aligned with the task. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are promising general-purpose verifiers due to their reasoning capabilities, existing frameworks often assume these models are well-calibrated. In practice, the overconfident judgment from VLM can degrade the steering performance under both high-level semantic uncertainty in task specifications and low-level action uncertainty or incapability of the pre-trained policy. We propose uncertainty-aware policy steering (UPS), a framework that jointly reasons about semantic task uncertainty and low-level action feasibility, and selects an uncertainty resolution strategy: execute a high-confidence action, clarify task ambiguity via natural language queries, or ask for action interventions to correct the low-level policy when it is deemed incapable at the task. We leverage conformal prediction to calibrate the composition of the VLM and the pre-trained base policy, providing statistical assurances that the verifier selects the correct strategy. After collecting interventions during deployment, we employ residual learning to improve the capability of the pre-trained policy, enabling the system to learn continually but with minimal expensive human feedback. We demonstrate our framework through experiments in simulation and on hardware, showing that UPS can disentangle confident, ambiguous, and incapable scenarios and minimizes expensive user interventions compared to uncalibrated baselines and prior human- or robot-gated continual learning approaches. Videos can be found at https://jessie-yuan.github.io/ups/

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-Aware Policy Steering (UPS) calibrates a vision-language verifier with a pre-trained policy via conformal prediction to handle semantic task uncertainty and low-level action infeasibility, choosing among executing a confident action, querying for clarification, or requesting interventions, and using residual learning for continual improvement; validated in simulation and hardware with reduced interventions.
AI.motivation: VLMs offer broad, reasoning-based verification but are often overconfident, leading to degraded steering when task specs are semantically uncertain or the base policy cannot fulfill actions. A calibrated, uncertainty-aware framework is needed to reliably combine high-level task understanding with low-level action feasibility and to minimize costly human interventions.
AI.method: Integrate a Vision-Language Model with a pre-trained policy using conformal prediction to calibrate their composition. The policy selects among three strategies: execute a high-confidence action, ask natural-language clarifications to resolve task ambiguity, or request action interventions to correct the policy. After deployment, collect interventions and apply residual learning to improve the policy, enabling continual learning with minimal human feedback. Evaluate in simulation and on hardware, comparing against uncalibrated baselines and prior continual-learning approaches.
AI.result: UPS can disentangle guaranteed/confident, ambiguous, and incapable scenarios and reduces expensive user interventions relative to uncalibrated baselines and prior human- or robot-gated continual learning. Demonstrates effectiveness in both simulated and real-robot experiments; provides statistical assurances via conformal calibration.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware, calibrated policy steering via conformal prediction enables robust, interpretable, and continually improvable robot behavior under semantic and action-level uncertainty, with practical deployment benefits and reduced need for human input.

title: Causal Direction from Convergence Time: Faster Training in the True Causal Direction
summary: We introduce Causal Computational Asymmetry (CCA), a principle for causal direction identification based on optimization dynamics in which one neural network is trained to predict $Y$ from $X$ and another to predict $X$ from $Y$, and the direction that converges faster is inferred to be causal. Under the additive noise model $Y = f(X) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \perp X$ and $f$ nonlinear and injective, we establish a formal asymmetry: in the reverse direction, residuals remain statistically dependent on the input regardless of approximation quality, inducing a strictly higher irreducible loss floor and non-separable gradient noise in the optimization dynamics, so that the reverse model requires strictly more gradient steps in expectation to reach any fixed loss threshold; consequently, the forward (causal) direction converges in fewer expected optimization steps. CCA operates in optimization-time space, distinguishing it from methods such as RESIT, IGCI, and SkewScore that rely on statistical independence or distributional asymmetries, and proper z-scoring of both variables is required for valid comparison of convergence rates. On synthetic benchmarks, CCA achieves 26/30 correct causal identifications across six neural architectures, including 30/30 on sine and exponential data-generating processes. We further embed CCA into a broader framework termed Causal Compression Learning (CCL), which integrates graph structure learning, causal information compression, and policy optimization, with all theoretical guarantees formally proved and empirically validated on synthetic datasets.

AI.tldr: Optimization-dynamics-based causal direction identification via asymmetry in convergence speed of two regressors; forward direction converges faster under additive noise with nonlinear injective f; supports CCA and CCL; strong synthetic performance.
AI.motivation: Address causal direction identification without relying on statistical independence assumptions by exploiting optimization-time asymmetry; provide a principled, testable criterion based on gradient dynamics.
AI.method: Train two neural nets predicting Y from X and X from Y; compare convergence rates to a fixed loss; analyze residual dependence and irreducible loss floor; account for gradient-noise characteristics; require proper z-scoring for valid comparisons; compare to RESIT, IGCI, SkewScore; evaluate on synthetic data; embed the idea into a broader Causal Compression Learning (CCL) framework with guarantees.
AI.result: Formal asymmetry: in the reverse direction residuals remain statistically dependent on the input, inducing a strictly higher irreducible loss floor and non-separable gradient noise; as a result, the forward (causal) direction converges in fewer expected optimization steps to any fixed loss. Empirically, CCA achieves 26/30 correct causal identifications across six neural architectures, including 30/30 on sine and exponential data-generating processes.
AI.conclusion: CCA introduces an optimization-time criterion for causal discovery complementary to statistical-independence-based methods, requiring proper z-scoring; it scales to multiple architectures and data-generating processes; integrated into the CCL framework with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.

title: Vibe Researching as Wolf Coming: Can AI Agents with Skills Replace or Augment Social Scientists?
summary: AI agents -- systems that execute multi-step reasoning workflows with persistent state, tool access, and specialist skills -- represent a qualitative shift from prior automation technologies in social science. Unlike chatbots that respond to isolated queries, AI agents can now read files, run code, query databases, search the web, and invoke domain-specific skills to execute entire research pipelines autonomously. This paper introduces the concept of vibe researching -- the AI-era parallel to ``vibe coding'' (Karpathy, 2025) -- and uses scholar-skill, a 21-skill plugin for Claude Code covering the full research pipeline from idea to submission, as an illustrative case. I develop a cognitive task framework that classifies research activities along two dimensions -- codifiability and tacit knowledge requirement -- to identify a delegation boundary that is cognitive, not sequential: it cuts through every stage of the research pipeline, not between stages. I argue that AI agents excel at speed, coverage, and methodological scaffolding but struggle with theoretical originality and tacit field knowledge. The paper concludes with an analysis of three implications for the profession -- augmentation with fragile conditions, stratification risk, and a pedagogical crisis -- and proposes five principles for responsible vibe researching.

AI.tldr: Proposes 'vibe researching': AI agents perform full research pipelines using a two-dimensional cognitive framework (codifiability and tacit knowledge) to identify cognitive delegation boundaries across the pipeline; illustrated with a 21-skill plugin for Claude Code; discusses implications and offers five principles for responsible practice.
AI.motivation: To map how AI agents change the practice of research, outlining potential augmentation benefits and risks (fragility, stratification, pedagogical disruption).
AI.method: Constructs a two-axis cognitive task framework (codifiability vs tacit knowledge) to locate cognitive delegation boundaries that span the entire research pipeline. Uses 'scholar-skill,' a 21-skill plugin for Claude Code, as an illustrative case and analyzes implications, culminating in five proposed principles for responsible vibe researching.
AI.result: Finds that AI agents excel in speed, coverage, and scaffolding of methodological workflows, but struggle with theoretical originality and tacit field knowledge. Delegation boundary is cognitive and crosses all pipeline stages, not confined to stage transitions. Identifies three implications: augmentation under fragile conditions, risk of stratification, and a pedagogical crisis.
AI.conclusion: Calls for five principles to guide responsible vibe researching and positions the framework as a guide for integrating AI agents into research practice while acknowledging limitations in originality and tacit expertise.

title: CLIP Is Shortsighted: Paying Attention Beyond the First Sentence
comment: 19 pages, 13 figures, to be published in the CVPR 2026 proceedings
summary: CLIP models learn transferable multi-modal features via image-text contrastive learning on internet-scale data. They are widely used in zero-shot classification, multi-modal retrieval, text-to-image diffusion, and as image encoders in large vision-language models. However, CLIP's pretraining is dominated by images paired with short captions, biasing the model toward encoding simple descriptions of salient objects and leading to coarse alignment on complex scenes and dense descriptions. While recent work mitigates this by fine-tuning on small-scale long-caption datasets, we identify an important common bias: both human- and LLM-generated long captions typically begin with a one-sentence summary followed by a detailed description. We show that this acts as a shortcut during training, concentrating attention on the opening sentence and early tokens and weakening alignment over the rest of the caption. To resolve this, we introduce DeBias-CLIP, which removes the summary sentence during training and applies sentence sub-sampling and text token padding to distribute supervision across all token positions. DeBias-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art long-text retrieval, improves short-text retrieval, and is less sensitive to sentence order permutations. It is a drop-in replacement for Long-CLIP with no additional trainable parameters.

AI.tldr: DeBias-CLIP removes the leading summary sentence from long captions during CLIP pretraining and uses sentence sub-sampling with token padding to spread supervision across all token positions, achieving state-of-the-art long-text retrieval, better short-text retrieval, and robustness to sentence order; it is a drop-in, parameter-free replacement for Long-CLIP.
AI.motivation: CLIP pretraining on image-caption pairs is biased toward short, salient-object descriptions due to captions often being short and biased by a leading one-sentence summary; long descriptions suffer from a shortcut that concentrates attention on opening tokens, hindering alignment across the full caption. The goal is to mitigate this bias to improve alignment on long, complex scenes.
AI.method: During training, remove the leading summary sentence from long captions. Apply sentence sub-sampling and text token padding to distribute supervision across all token positions, avoiding overemphasis on early tokens. No additional trainable parameters; can be used as a drop-in replacement for Long-CLIP.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on long-text retrieval and improvements in short-text retrieval; demonstrates reduced sensitivity to sentence order permutations.
AI.conclusion: DeBias-CLIP provides a parameter-free, drop-in improvement over Long-CLIP by mitigating the summary-sentence bias in long captions, resulting in more uniform token-position supervision and stronger cross-modal alignment.

title: SignVLA: A Gloss-Free Vision-Language-Action Framework for Real-Time Sign Language-Guided Robotic Manipulation
summary:
We present, to our knowledge, the first sign language-driven Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework for intuitive and inclusive human-robot interaction. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on gloss annotations as intermediate supervision, the proposed system adopts a gloss-free paradigm and directly maps visual sign gestures to semantic instructions. This design reduces annotation cost and avoids the information loss introduced by gloss representations, enabling more natural and scalable multimodal interaction.
  In this work, we focus on a real-time alphabet-level finger-spelling interface that provides a robust and low-latency communication channel for robotic control. Compared with large-scale continuous sign language recognition, alphabet-level interaction offers improved reliability, interpretability, and deployment feasibility in safety-critical embodied environments. The proposed pipeline transforms continuous gesture streams into coherent language commands through geometric normalization, temporal smoothing, and lexical refinement, ensuring stable and consistent interaction.
  Furthermore, the framework is designed to support future integration of transformer-based gloss-free sign language models, enabling scalable word-level and sentence-level semantic understanding. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed system in grounding sign-derived instructions into precise robotic actions under diverse interaction scenarios. These results highlight the potential of the framework to advance accessible, scalable, and multimodal embodied intelligence.

AI.tldr: Gloss-free, sign-language–driven VLA framework that maps visual alphabet signs directly to robot commands, enabling real-time, low-latency control without gloss annotations and with scalable future word/sentence understanding.
AI.motivation: Overcome annotation cost and information loss from gloss representations in sign-language systems, enabling more natural, reliable, and scalable multimodal human–robot interaction in safety-critical, embodied contexts.
AI.method: A gloss-free pipeline for interpreting alphabet-level finger spelling, converting continuous gesture streams into coherent language commands via geometric normalization, temporal smoothing, and lexical refinement; designed for potential integration of transformer-based gloss-free SL models.
AI.result: Demonstrated effective grounding of sign-derived instructions into precise robotic actions across diverse interaction scenarios, with robust, low-latency performance and improved interpretability compared with gloss-based or continuous-sign approaches.
AI.conclusion: The framework advances accessible, scalable, multimodal embodied intelligence and lays groundwork for future word- and sentence-level understanding with gloss-free sign language models.

title: Deep Sequence Modeling with Quantum Dynamics: Language as a Wave Function
summary: We introduce a sequence modeling framework in which the latent state is a complex-valued wave function evolving on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space under a learned, time-dependent Hamiltonian. Unlike standard recurrent architectures that rely on gating mechanisms to suppress competing hypotheses, our framework utilizes quantum interference: the Hamiltonian steers the phases of complex amplitudes so that conflicting interpretations cancel while compatible ones reinforce. The dynamics are strictly unitary, ensuring that the state norm is preserved exactly at every time step via a Cayley (Crank--Nicolson) discretization. Token probabilities are extracted using the Born rule, a quadratic measurement operator that couples magnitudes and relative phases. Our primary theoretical contribution is a separation theorem characterizing the representational advantage of this readout: we define a family of disambiguation tasks that a complex unitary model of dimension $N$ solves exactly, but which requires a state dimension of $Ω(N^2)$ for any real-valued orthogonal model equipped with a standard affine-softmax readout. This quadratic gap arises because the Born rule implicitly lifts the $N$-dimensional state into the space of rank-one Hermitian matrices, accessing pairwise phase correlations that are inaccessible to linear projections. Finally, we derive a continuity equation for the latent probability mass, yielding conserved pairwise currents that serve as a built-in diagnostic for tracing information flow between dimensions.

AI.tldr: A complex-valued, unitary sequence model evolves under a learnable time-dependent Hamiltonian on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space; uses quantum interference for disambiguation, exact norm preservation via Cayley discretization, and Born-rule readout; proves a quadratic representational gap over real-valued models and introduces a continuity equation for latent currents.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of gating-based RNNs by modeling interference-driven discrimination in the latent state; exploit unitary evolution and phase information to capture dependencies and conflicts in sequences; provide theoretical guarantees and diagnostic tools.
AI.method: Define latent state as a complex vector; evolve with learned Hamiltonian H(t) (time-dependent) generating unitary dynamics; discretize with Cayley/Crank–Nicolson to preserve norm; extract token probabilities via Born rule; compare with real-valued affine-softmax; derive a separation theorem; derive continuity equation showing conserved pairwise currents.
AI.result: Proven exact solvability of a disambiguation task family with dimension N using complex model, requiring Ω(N^2) for real-valued models; the Born rule lifts to rank-one Hermitian matrices, enabling access to pairwise phase correlations not present in linear projections; continuity equation provides diagnostic currents.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a fundamental representational advantage of complex unitary sequence models in tasks requiring disambiguation; provides principled readout separation and diagnostic tools; suggests new directions for sequence modeling with quantum-inspired dynamics.

title: Towards Autonomous Memory Agents
summary: Recent memory agents improve LLMs by extracting experiences and conversation history into an external storage. This enables low-overhead context assembly and online memory update without expensive LLM training. However, existing solutions remain passive and reactive; memory growth is bounded by information that happens to be available, while memory agents seldom seek external inputs in uncertainties. We propose autonomous memory agents that actively acquire, validate, and curate knowledge at a minimum cost. U-Mem materializes this idea via (i) a cost-aware knowledge-extraction cascade that escalates from cheap self/teacher signals to tool-verified research and, only when needed, expert feedback, and (ii) semantic-aware Thompson sampling to balance exploration and exploitation over memories and mitigate cold-start bias. On both verifiable and non-verifiable benchmarks, U-Mem consistently beats prior memory baselines and can surpass RL-based optimization, improving HotpotQA (Qwen2.5-7B) by 14.6 points and AIME25 (Gemini-2.5-flash) by 7.33 points.

AI.tldr: Autonomous memory agents (U-Mem) actively acquire, verify, and curate external memories using a cost-aware extraction cascade and semantic-aware Thompson sampling, achieving strong improvements on memory-augmented QA benchmarks and surpassing prior baselines and some RL methods.
AI.motivation: Current memory-augmented LLMs are passive and memory growth is limited to what is observed; proactive, low-cost memory acquisition is needed to handle uncertainties and reduce training overhead.
AI.method: Introduce a cost-aware knowledge-extraction cascade (self/teacher signals -> tool-verified research -> expert feedback) and semantic-aware Thompson sampling to balance exploration over memories and mitigate cold-start bias.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms prior memory baselines on verifiable and non-verifiable benchmarks; improves HotpotQA (Qwen2.5-7B) by 14.6 points and AIME25 (Gemini-2.5-flash) by 7.33 points; in some cases surpasses RL-based optimization.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous, cost-conscious memory agents outperform passive approaches and RL optimization in memory-augmented LLMs, enabling proactive, scalable memory management with strong empirical gains.

title: SimpleOCR: Rendering Visualized Questions to Teach MLLMs to Read
summary: Despite the rapid advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), a critical question regarding their visual grounding mechanism remains unanswered: do these models genuinely ``read'' text embedded in images, or do they merely rely on parametric shortcuts in the text prompt? In this work, we diagnose this issue by introducing the Visualized-Question (VQ) setting, where text queries are rendered directly onto images to structurally mandate visual engagement. Our diagnostic experiments on Qwen2.5-VL reveal a startling capability-utilization gap: despite possessing strong OCR capabilities, models suffer a performance degradation of up to 12.7% in the VQ setting, exposing a deep-seated ``modality laziness.'' To bridge this gap, we propose SimpleOCR, a plug-and-play training strategy that imposes a structural constraint on the learning process. By transforming training samples into the VQ format with randomized styles, SimpleOCR effectively invalidates text-based shortcuts, compelling the model to activate and optimize its visual text extraction pathways. Empirically, SimpleOCR yields robust gains without architectural modifications. On four representative OOD benchmarks, it surpasses the base model by 5.4% and GRPO based on original images by 2.7%, while exhibiting extreme data efficiency, achieving superior performance with 30x fewer samples (8.5K) than recent RL-based methods. Furthermore, its plug-and-play nature allows seamless integration with advanced RL strategies like NoisyRollout to yield complementary improvements. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleOCR.

AI.tldr: VQ reveals a modality laziness in MLLMs by forcing visual engagement; SimpleOCR is a plug-and-play training strategy that converts data to VQ format with randomized styles to compel visual text extraction, yielding data-efficient gains without architecture changes.
AI.motivation: Despite OCR ability, multimodal LLMs may rely on text prompts rather than truly reading in images. The Visualized-Question (VQ) setting structurally enforces visual engagement to diagnose grounding behavior. The paper questions whether current MLLMs perform genuine visual text reading or rely on prompt-based shortcuts.
AI.method: 1) Introduce Visualized-Question (VQ) setting by rendering text queries onto images. 2) Diagnose with Qwen2.5-VL, revealing a performance drop (modality laziness). 3) Propose SimpleOCR, a plug-and-play training strategy that converts training samples into VQ format with randomized styles to invalidate text-based shortcuts and enforce learning of visual text extraction pathways. 4) Evaluate with four OOD benchmarks and compare to base models and GRPO; examine compatibility with NoisyRollout RL strategy.
AI.result: SimpleOCR yields robust gains: +5.4% on four representative OOD benchmarks and +2.7% on GRPO using original images. It achieves these improvements with 8.5K samples, i.e., ~30x fewer than recent RL methods, highlighting extreme data efficiency. The method is plug-and-play and can complement advanced RL strategies like NoisyRollout.
AI.conclusion: SimpleOCR effectively bridges the gap between OCR capability and genuine visual grounding by forcing reliance on visual text extraction, delivering data-efficient, architecture-free improvements and compatible with RL enhancements.

title: Metamorphic Testing of Vision-Language Action-Enabled Robots
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are multimodal robotic task controllers that, given an instruction and visual inputs, produce a sequence of low-level control actions (or motor commands) enabling a robot to execute the requested task in the physical environment. These systems face the test oracle problem from multiple perspectives. On the one hand, a test oracle must be defined for each instruction prompt, which is a complex and non-generalizable approach. On the other hand, current state-of-the-art oracles typically capture symbolic representations of the world (e.g., robot and object states), enabling the correctness evaluation of a task, but fail to assess other critical aspects, such as the quality with which VLA-enabled robots perform a task. In this paper, we explore whether Metamorphic Testing (MT) can alleviate the test oracle problem in this context. To do so, we propose two metamorphic relation patterns and five metamorphic relations to assess whether changes to the test inputs impact the original trajectory of the VLA-enabled robots. An empirical study involving five VLA models, two simulated robots, and four robotic tasks shows that MT can effectively alleviate the test oracle problem by automatically detecting diverse types of failures, including, but not limited to, uncompleted tasks. More importantly, the proposed MRs are generalizable, making the proposed approach applicable across different VLA models, robots, and tasks, even in the absence of test oracles.

AI.tldr: Metamorphic Testing (MT) is proposed to tackle the test oracle problem in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robotic controllers by introducing two metamorphic relation patterns and five metamorphic relations; validated across multiple models/robots/tasks; MT detects diverse failures and is generalizable even without test oracles.
AI.motivation: Test oracles for VLA are complex and non-generalizable; existing state-of-the-art oracles rely on symbolic world states, which miss quality aspects; there is a need to assess task execution quality and robustness beyond correctness.
AI.method: Propose two metamorphic relation patterns and five metamorphic relations; apply MT to VLA models by modifying test inputs and observing impact on trajectories; empirical evaluation with five VLA models, two simulated robots, four tasks; evaluate detection of failures and generalizability.
AI.result: MT successfully alleviates the test oracle problem by automatically detecting failures, including incomplete tasks; metamorphic relations are generalizable across models/robots/tasks; effective even when explicit test oracles are unavailable.
AI.conclusion: MT provides a generalizable, practical approach to validate VLA robotic systems beyond symbolic correctness; applicable across diverse models and environments; demonstrates broader applicability of metamorphic testing in robotics.

title: Orthogonal Weight Modification Enhances Learning Scalability and Convergence Efficiency without Gradient Backpropagation
