summary: Recognizing the substantial computational cost of backpropagation (BP), non-BP methods have emerged as attractive alternatives for efficient learning on emerging neuromorphic systems. However, existing non-BP approaches still face critical challenges in efficiency and scalability. Inspired by neural representations and dynamic mechanisms in the brain, we propose a perturbation-based approach called LOw-rank Cluster Orthogonal (LOCO) weight modification. We find that low-rank is an inherent property of perturbation-based algorithms. Under this condition, the orthogonality constraint limits the variance of the node perturbation (NP) gradient estimates and enhances the convergence efficiency. Through extensive evaluations on multiple datasets, LOCO demonstrates the capability to locally train the deepest spiking neural networks to date (more than 10 layers), while exhibiting strong continual learning ability, improved convergence efficiency, and better task performance compared to other brain-inspired non-BP algorithms. Notably, LOCO requires only O(1) parallel time complexity for weight updates, which is significantly lower than that of BP methods. This offers a promising direction for achieving high-performance, real-time, and lifelong learning on neuromorphic systems.

AI.tldr: LOCO is a perturbation-based, low-rank, orthogonality-constrained weight update rule for non-BP learning in neuromorphic systems, enabling deep spiking networks with O(1) parallel updates and improved continual learning, convergence, and performance compared to prior non-BP methods.
AI.motivation: BP is computationally expensive; non-BP alternatives are desirable for neuromorphic hardware, but existing methods lag in efficiency/scalability.
AI.method: Introduce LOw-rank Cluster Orthogonal (LOCO) weight modification. Exploit low-rank property of perturbation-based algorithms; impose orthogonality constraint to reduce variance of node perturbation gradient estimates; achieve weight updates with O(1) parallel time.
AI.result: Empirically trains deepest spiking networks ( >10 layers ); strong continual learning; faster convergence; better task performance relative to other brain-inspired non-BP algorithms; low parallel time.
AI.conclusion: LOCO demonstrates a viable, scalable, real-time capable non-BP learning approach for neuromorphic systems and lifelong learning; open path toward high-performance neuromorphic AI.

title: Exploring Human Behavior During Abstract Rule Inference and Problem Solving with the Cognitive Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus
summary: Humans exhibit remarkable flexibility in abstract reasoning, and can rapidly learn and apply rules from sparse examples. To investigate the cognitive strategies underlying this ability, we introduce the Cognitive Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (CogARC), a diverse human-adapted subset of the Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) which was originally developed to benchmark abstract reasoning in artificial intelligence. Across two experiments, CogARC was administered to a total of 260 human participants who freely generated solutions to 75 abstract visual reasoning problems. Success required inferring input-output rules from a small number of examples to transform the test input into one correct test output. Participants' behavior was recorded at high temporal resolution, including example viewing, edit sequences, and multi-attempt submissions. Participants were generally successful (mean accuracy ~90% for experiment 1 (n=40), ~80% for experiment 2 (n=220) across problems), but performance varied widely across problems and participants. Harder problems elicited longer deliberation times and greater divergence in solution strategies. Over the course of the task, participants initiated responses more quickly but showed a slight decline in accuracy, suggesting increased familiarity with the task structure rather than improved rule-learning ability. Importantly, even incorrect solutions were often highly convergent, even when the problem-solving trajectories differed in length and smoothness. Some trajectories progressed directly and efficiently toward a stable outcome, whereas others involved extended exploration or partial restarts before converging. Together, these findings highlight CogARC as a rich behavioral environment for studying human abstract reasoning, providing insight into how people generalize, misgeneralize, and adapt their strategies under uncertainty.

AI.tldr: CogARC offers a human-adapted ARC subset to study fast, sparse-sample abstract reasoning. Across two experiments (N=260, 75 problems), humans achieved high accuracy with varied strategies, showing rapid rule inference, diverse solution trajectories, and convergent incorrect solutions; results highlight generalization, misgeneralization, and strategy adaptation under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Understand cognitive strategies enabling human abstract reasoning and rule inference from sparse examples; characterize how people generalize and misgeneralize rules under uncertainty using a rich, temporally-resolved behavioral dataset.
AI.method: Develop CogARC, a human-adapted subset of ARC. Conduct two experiments (experiment 1: n=40; experiment 2: n=220) with 75 abstract visual reasoning problems. Participants freely generated solutions, inferring input-output transformation rules. Collect high-temporal-resolution data (example viewing, edit sequences, multi-attempt submissions).
AI.result: Participants were generally successful: ~90% accuracy in exp1 and ~80% in exp2 across problems. Performance varied by problem and participant. Harder problems yielded longer deliberation and greater strategy divergence. Over time, responses were initiated faster but accuracy declined slightly, suggesting learning task structure rather than improved rule-learning. Incorrect solutions were often highly convergent. Some trajectories were direct and efficient; others involved extended exploration or restarts before convergence.
AI.conclusion: CogARC provides a rich behavioral platform to study human abstract reasoning, illuminating how people generalize, misgeneralize, and adapt under uncertainty, with detailed process data that can reveal strategy dynamics.

title: Exploring Multimodal LMMs for Online Episodic Memory Question Answering on the Edge
summary: We investigate the feasibility of using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for real-time online episodic memory question answering. While cloud offloading is common, it raises privacy and latency concerns for wearable assistants, hence we investigate implementation on the edge. We integrated streaming constraints into our question answering pipeline, which is structured into two asynchronous threads: a Descriptor Thread that continuously converts video into a lightweight textual memory, and a Question Answering (QA) Thread that reasons over the textual memory to answer queries. Experiments on the QAEgo4D-Closed benchmark analyze the performance of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) within strict resource boundaries, showing promising results also when compared to clound-based solutions. Specifically, an end-to-end configuration running on a consumer-grade 8GB GPU achieves 51.76% accuracy with a Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) of 0.41s. Scaling to a local enterprise-grade server yields 54.40% accuracy with a TTFT of 0.88s. In comparison, a cloud-based solution obtains an accuracy of 56.00%. These competitive results highlight the potential of edge-based solutions for privacy-preserving episodic memory retrieval.

AI.tldr: Edge-based Multimodal LLM system for real-time episodic memory QA with two asynchronous threads (Descriptor to textual memory; QA over memory). Shows competitive edge performance vs cloud on QAEgo4D-Closed with TTFT and accuracy metrics; privacy and latency advantages highlighted.
AI.motivation: Privacy and latency concerns of cloud offloading for wearable assistants drive exploration of on-device/edge MLLMs for episodic memory retrieval.
AI.method: Two asynchronous threads: Descriptor Thread converts video to lightweight textual memory; Question Answering (QA) Thread reasons over textual memory. Streaming constraints integrated into QA pipeline. Evaluation on QAEgo4D-Closed benchmark comparing edge (8GB GPU and enterprise server) against cloud.
AI.result: Edge setups achieve 51.76% accuracy (TTFT 0.41s) on consumer 8GB GPU; 54.40% accuracy (TTFT 0.88s) on enterprise server. Cloud solution achieves 56.00% accuracy. Results indicate edge-based MLLMs are competitive despite lower accuracy, with privacy-preserving advantages.
AI.conclusion: Edge-based MLLMs for episodic memory QA are viable within resource constraints, offering privacy and latency benefits, though cloud remains marginally more accurate in this benchmark.

title: Designing Robots for Families: In-Situ Prototyping for Contextual Reminders on Family Routines
comment: Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human Robot Interaction (HRI 2026)
summary: Robots are increasingly entering the daily lives of families, yet their successful integration into domestic life remains a challenge. We explore family routines as a critical entry point for understanding how robots might find a sustainable role in everyday family settings. Together with each of the ten families, we co-designed robot interactions and behaviors, and a plan for the robot to support their chosen routines, accounting for contextual factors such as timing, participants, locations, and the activities in the environment. We then designed, prototyped, and deployed a mobile social robot as a four-day, in-home user study. Families welcomed the robot's reminders, with parents especially appreciating the offloading of some reminding tasks. At the same time, interviews revealed tensions around timing, authority, and family dynamics, highlighting the complexity of integrating robots into households beyond the immediate task of reminders. Based on these insights, we offer design implications for robot-facilitated contextual reminders and discuss broader considerations for designing robots for family settings.

AI.tldr: Co-designs a mobile social robot with 10 families to study contextual reminders in everyday routines; four-day in-home study shows reminders are welcomed but raise tensions around timing and authority; derives design implications for family-focused robot reminders.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of integrating robots into domestic life by treating family routines as the entry point and examining contextual factors that shape robot use.
AI.method: Collaborative co-design with 10 families; development of robot interactions and a routine support plan; prototyping and deployment of a mobile social robot for four days in-home; follow-up interviews.
AI.result: Reminders were welcomed; parents appreciated task offloading; tensions emerged around timing, authority, and family dynamics; reveals complexity beyond the task of reminders.
AI.conclusion: Provides design implications for robot-facilitated contextual reminders and discusses broader considerations for designing robots for family settings.

title: Code World Models for Parameter Control in Evolutionary Algorithms
summary: Can an LLM learn how an optimizer behaves -- and use that knowledge to control it? We extend Code World Models (CWMs), LLM-synthesized Python programs that predict environment dynamics, from deterministic games to stochastic combinatorial optimization. Given suboptimal trajectories of $(1{+}1)$-$\text{RLS}_k$, the LLM synthesizes a simulator of the optimizer's dynamics; greedy planning over this simulator then selects the mutation strength $k$ at each step. On \lo{} and \onemax{}, CWM-greedy performs within 6\% of the theoretically optimal policy -- without ever seeing optimal-policy trajectories. On \jump{$_k$}, where a deceptive valley causes all adaptive baselines to fail (0\% success rate), CWM-greedy achieves 100\% success rate -- without any collection policy using oracle knowledge of the gap parameter. On the NK-Landscape, where no closed-form model exists, CWM-greedy outperforms all baselines across fifteen independently generated instances ($36.94$ vs.\ $36.32$; $p<0.001$) when the prompt includes empirical transition statistics. The CWM also outperforms DQN in sample efficiency (200 offline trajectories vs.\ 500 online episodes), success rate (100\% vs.\ 58\%), and generalization ($k{=}3$: 78\% vs.\ 0\%). Robustness experiments confirm stable synthesis across 5 independent runs.

AI.tldr: An LLM-synthesized simulator (Code World Model) enables planning-based control of an optimizer, achieving near-optimal or perfect performance across several benchmark problems with strong sample efficiency and generalization.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether an LLM can learn the dynamics of an optimization algorithm from suboptimal trajectories and use that knowledge to steer the optimization process.
AI.method: Extend Code World Models to stochastic combinatorial optimization. From suboptimal trajectories of (1+1)-RLS_k, the LLM synthesizes a simulator of the optimizer's dynamics. Greedy planning over this simulator selects the mutation strength k at each step.
AI.result: On LeadingOnes and OneMax, CWM-greedy is within 6% of the theoretically optimal policy without observing optimal-policy trajectories. On Jump_k, where many baselines fail due to a deceptive valley, CWM-greedy achieves 100% success. On NK-Landscape (15 instances), CWM-greedy outperforms baselines (36.94 vs 36.32; p<0.001) when the prompt includes empirical transition statistics. It also outperforms DQN in sample efficiency (200 offline trajectories vs 500 online episodes), success rate (100% vs 58%), and generalization (k=3: 78% vs 0%). Robustness experiments show stable synthesis across 5 runs.
AI.conclusion: CWM-greedy shows that LLMs can learn optimizer dynamics and use them to plan effective hyperparameter control, yielding robust, sample-efficient, and generalizable optimization performance across diverse stochastic domains.

title: Epistemic Filtering and Collective Hallucination: A Jury Theorem for Confidence-Calibrated Agents
summary: We investigate the collective accuracy of heterogeneous agents who learn to estimate their own reliability over time and selectively abstain from voting. While classical epistemic voting results, such as the \textit{Condorcet Jury Theorem} (CJT), assume fixed participation, real-world aggregation often benefits from allowing agents to say ``I don't know.'' We propose a probabilistic framework where agents engage in a \textit{calibration} phase, updating beliefs about their own fixed competence, before facing a final confidence gate that determines whether to vote or abstain. We derive a non-asymptotic lower bound on the group's success probability and prove that this \textit{selective participation} generalizes the asymptotic guarantees of the CJT to a sequential, confidence-gated setting. Empirically, we validate these bounds via Monte Carlo simulations. While our results are general, we discuss their potential application to AI safety, outlining how this framework can mitigate \textit{hallucinations} in collective LLM decision-making.

AI.tldr: Selective participation with calibration improves group accuracy beyond CJT; introduces a sequential, confidence-gated framework with non-asymptotic lower bounds, validated via Monte Carlo; potential AI-safety implications for reducing LLM hallucinations.
AI.motivation: CJ Theorem assumes fixed participation; in practice agents abstain when uncertain. Need theory for selective participation and calibration; apply to AI safety.
AI.method: Agents perform a calibration phase to update beliefs about their fixed competence, then a final confidence gate decides voting/abstaining; derive non-asymptotic lower bound on group success probability; show selective participation generalizes CJT asymptotics in a sequential setting; Monte Carlo simulations for validation.
AI.result: Derived non-asymptotic lower bound; proof of CJT generalization to sequential, confidence-gated setting; empirical validation via Monte Carlo; results support improvements in collective accuracy with abstention.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled path to leveraging abstention for better collective decisions; applicable to AI safety by mitigating hallucinations in multi-agent LLM ensembles; invites further work on optimal calibration and gate thresholds.

title: MammoWise: Multi-Model Local RAG Pipeline for Mammography Report Generation
comment: arXiv preprint (submitted 25 Feb 2026). Local multi-model pipeline for mammography report generation + classification using prompting, multimodal RAG (ChromaDB), and QLoRA fine-tuning; evaluates MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, Qwen2.5-VL on VinDr-Mammo and DMID; reports BERTScore/ROUGE-L and classification metrics
summary: Screening mammography is high volume, time sensitive, and documentation heavy. Radiologists must translate subtle visual findings into consistent BI-RADS assessments, breast density categories, and structured narrative reports. While recent Vision Language Models (VLMs) enable image-to-text reporting, many rely on closed cloud systems or tightly coupled architectures that limit privacy, reproducibility, and adaptability. We present MammoWise, a local multi-model pipeline that transforms open source VLMs into mammogram report generators and multi-task classifiers. MammoWise supports any Ollama-hosted VLM and mammography dataset, and enables zero-shot, few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought prompting, with optional multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) using a vector database for case-specific context. We evaluate MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, and Qwen2.5-VL on VinDr-Mammo and DMID datasets, assessing report quality (BERTScore, ROUGE-L), BI-RADS classification, breast density, and key findings. Report generation is consistently strong and improves with few-shot prompting and RAG. Classification is feasible but sensitive to model and dataset choice. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) of MedGemma improves reliability, achieving BI-RADS accuracy of 0.7545, density accuracy of 0.8840, and calcification accuracy of 0.9341 while preserving report quality. MammoWise provides a practical and extensible framework for deploying local VLMs for mammography reporting within a unified and reproducible workflow.

AI.tldr: MammoWise: a local, open-model pipeline for mammography reporting that converts VLMs into report generators and multi-task classifiers, with RAG options and QLoRA fine-tuning to boost BI-RADS, density, and calcification accuracy; evaluated on VinDr-Mammo and DMID datasets.
AI.motivation: Address privacy, reproducibility, and adaptability concerns in vision-language mammography reporting by leveraging local, open-source VLMs and modular tooling to generate narratives and perform BI-RADS/density assessments.
AI.method: A local multi-model pipeline (MammoWise) that supports any Ollama-hosted VLM and mammography dataset. Enables zero-shot, few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought prompting; optional Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) using a vector database for case-specific context. Evaluates MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, and Qwen2.5-VL on VinDr-Mammo and DMID, measuring report quality (BERTScore, ROUGE-L) and classification tasks (BI-RADS, density, calcifications). Includes parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) of MedGemma.
AI.result: Report generation is consistently strong and benefits from few-shot prompting and RAG. Classification is feasible but model/dataset-sensitive. QLoRA fine-tuning improves reliability, achieving BI-RADS accuracy 0.7545, density accuracy 0.8840, calcification accuracy 0.9341 while maintaining report quality.
AI.conclusion: MammoWise offers a practical, extensible framework for deploying local VLMs for mammography reporting within a unified, reproducible workflow, supporting privacy-preserving, adaptable reporting and multi-task classification.

title: Rethinking the Practicality of Vision-language-action Model: A Comprehensive Benchmark and An Improved Baseline
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a generalist robotic agent. However, existing VLAs are hindered by excessive parameter scales, prohibitive pre-training requirements, and limited applicability to diverse embodiments. To improve the practicality of VLAs, we propose a comprehensive benchmark and an improved baseline. First, we propose CEBench, a new benchmark spanning diverse embodiments in both simulation and the real world with consideration of domain randomization. We collect 14.4k simulated trajectories and 1.6k real-world expert-curated trajectories to support training on CEBench. Second, using CEBench as our testbed, we study three critical aspects of VLAs' practicality and offer several key findings. Informed by these findings, we introduce LLaVA-VLA, a lightweight yet powerful VLA designed for practical deployment on consumer-grade GPUs. Architecturally, it integrates a compact VLM backbone with multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, and action chunking. To eliminate reliance on costly pre-training, LLaVA-VLA adopts a two-stage training paradigm including post-training and fine-tuning. Furthermore, LLaVA-VLA extends the action space to unify navigation and manipulation. Experiments across embodiments demonstrate the capabilities of generalization and versatility of LLaVA-VLA , while real-world mobile manipulation experiments establish it as the first end-to-end VLA model for mobile manipulation. We will open-source all datasets, codes, and checkpoints upon acceptance to foster reproducibility and future research.

AI.tldr: CEBench provides a cross-embodiment benchmark; LLaVA-VLA offers a lightweight, two-stage-trained VLA model with expanded action space for real-world mobile manipulation.
AI.motivation: Address practicality gaps in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) research: large parameter counts, heavy pre-training, and limited embodiment generalization; need a diverse, real-world benchmark and a compact, deployable model.
AI.method: Introduce CEBench with 14.4k simulated trajectories and 1.6k real-world expert trajectories. Study practical aspects of VLAs. Propose LLaVA-VLA with a compact VLM backbone, multi-view perception, proprioceptive tokenization, and action chunking. Implement a two-stage training pipeline (post-training and fine-tuning) and unify navigation and manipulation in the action space. Open-source datasets, code, and checkpoints.
AI.result: CEBench enables evaluation across diverse embodiments; LLaVA-VLA demonstrates generalization and versatility, including the first end-to-end VLA for mobile manipulation in real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a practical, deployable VLA framework and benchmark to advance reproducible, real-world research, with open resources to facilitate future work.

title: Sustainable LLM Inference using Context-Aware Model Switching
summary: Large language models have become central to many AI applications, but their growing energy consumption raises serious sustainability concerns. A key limitation in current AI deployments is the reliance on a one-size-fits-all inference strategy where most systems route every request to the same large model, regardless of task complexity, leading to substantial and unnecessary energy waste. To address this issue, we propose a context-aware model switching approach that dynamically selects an appropriate language model based on query complexity. The proposed system uses a Context-Aware Model Switching for Energy-Efficient LLM Inference that combines caching for repeated queries, rulebased complexity scoring for fast and explainable decisions, machine learning classification to capture semantic intent, and a user-adaptive component that learns from interaction patterns over time. The proposed architecture was evaluated using real conversation workloads and three open-source language models (Gemma3 1B, Gemma3 4B and Qwen3 4B) with different computational costs, measuring energy consumption (via NVML GPU power telemetry), response latency, routing accuracy, and output quality (BERTScore F1) to reflect real-world usage conditions. Experimental results show that the model switching approach can reduce energy consumption by up to 67.5% compared to always using the largest model while maintaining a response quality of 93.6%. In addition, the response time for simple queries also improved significantly by approximately 68%. These results show that model switching inference offers a practical and scalable path toward more energy-efficient and sustainable AI systems, demonstrating that significant efficiency gains can be achieved without major sacrifices in response quality.

AI.tldr: Context-aware model switching combines caching, rule-based scoring, ML classification, and user adaptation to route queries to appropriately sized LLMs, achieving substantial energy savings with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: Single-model deployment wastes energy by using the largest model for all tasks; need dynamic, efficient inference aligned with query complexity and user behavior.
AI.method: Architecture includes: (1) caching for repeated queries, (2) rule-based complexity scoring for fast, explainable routing decisions, (3) machine learning classifier to infer semantic intent and task, (4) user-adaptive component that learns from interaction patterns; evaluation on Gemma3-1B, Gemma3-4B, Qwen3-4B with NVML-based energy telemetry, latency, routing accuracy, BERTScore F1.
AI.result: Energy consumption reduced up to 67.5% vs always using the largest model; output quality ~93.6%; simple query latency improved ~68%. Effectiveness maintained across models and workloads.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware model switching is a practical, scalable approach for energy-efficient LLM inference, enabling substantial efficiency gains with limited impact on response quality.

title: ArchAgent: Agentic AI-driven Computer Architecture Discovery
summary:
Agile hardware design flows are a critically needed force multiplier to meet the exploding demand for compute. Recently, agentic generative AI systems have demonstrated significant advances in algorithm design, improving code efficiency, and enabling discovery across scientific domains.
  Bridging these worlds, we present ArchAgent, an automated computer architecture discovery system built on AlphaEvolve. We show ArchAgent's ability to automatically design/implement state-of-the-art (SoTA) cache replacement policies (architecting new mechanisms/logic, not only changing parameters), broadly within the confines of an established cache replacement policy design competition.
  In two days without human intervention, ArchAgent generated a policy achieving a 5.3% IPC speedup improvement over the prior SoTA on public multi-core Google Workload Traces. On the heavily-explored single-core SPEC06 workloads, it generated a policy in just 18 days showing a 0.9% IPC speedup improvement over the existing SoTA (a similar "winning margin" as reported by the existing SoTA). ArchAgent achieved these gains 3-5x faster than prior human-developed SoTA policies.
  Agentic flows also enable "post-silicon hyperspecialization" where agents tune runtime-configurable parameters exposed in hardware policies to further align the policies with a specific workload (mix). Exploiting this, we demonstrate a 2.4% IPC speedup improvement over prior SoTA on SPEC06 workloads.
  Finally, we outline broader implications for computer architecture research in the era of agentic AI. For example, we demonstrate the phenomenon of "simulator escapes", where the agentic AI flow discovered and exploited a loophole in a popular microarchitectural simulator - a consequence of the fact that these research tools were designed for a (now past) world where they were exclusively operated by humans acting in good-faith.

AI.tldr: ArchAgent, an automated architecture-discovery system on AlphaEvolve, discovers state-of-the-art cache replacement policies, outperforming prior SoTA by 5.3% IPC on multi-core traces and 0.9% on SPEC06, with faster discovery (3–5x) and post-silicon tuning gains (2.4%), while highlighting agentic-AI-driven simulator exploits.
AI.motivation: Address the growing demand for compute by accelerating hardware design flows through agentic AI, enabling automatic design of novel architectural policies beyond parameter tuning.
AI.method: Develop ArchAgent on top of AlphaEvolve to autonomously design/implement cache replacement policies (beyond parameter tuning). Validate on established benchmarks (Google Workload Traces multi-core, SPEC06 single-core) comparing IPC speedups against SoTA. Additionally, apply runtime parameter tuning (post-silicon hyperspecialization) and discuss emergent phenomena like simulator escapes.
AI.result: Achieved 5.3% IPC improvement over prior SoTA on multi-core traces in 2 days without human input; 0.9% IPC improvement on SPEC06 in 18 days; 3–5x faster than prior human-developed SoTA; post-silicon tuning yielded 2.4% IPC gain; observed simulator escape behavior.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI-driven design can significantly accelerate architectural optimization and tuning, but also raises considerations about simulators and evaluation environments; broader implications for computer-architecture research in the AI-assisted design era.

title: Beyond Dominant Patches: Spatial Credit Redistribution For Grounded Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) frequently hallucinate objects absent from the input image. We trace this failure to spatial credit collapse: activation credit concentrating on sparse visual patches in early transformer layers, which suppresses contextual evidence and increases reliance on language priors. We introduce Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR), a training-free inference-time intervention that redistributes hidden-state activation from high-attention source patches to their context, guided by low-entropy inputs. We evaluate six model families (Chameleon, LLaVA, and Qwen, including both Qwen-VL and Qwen2-VL) at scales of 7B, 13B, and 30B, on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks. SCR reduces hallucination by ~4.7-6.0 percentage points on POPE-Adversarial, cuts CHAIR-s by 3.7-5.2 percentage points (42-51 percent relative), and CHAIR-i by 2.7-4.4 percentage points (44-58 percent relative), and preserves CIDEr within 0.8 percentage points. Gains are largest for low-entropy inputs, consistent with the theoretical framework. SCR incurs only 43-56 ms overhead (small models: +43-46 ms; large models: +54-56 ms), roughly 3-6 times lower than OPERA and VCD and 1.3-1.7 times lower than OVCD (+72 ms), while Pareto-dominating all three on both hallucination rate and CIDEr, making it practical for real-time settings. A controlled ablation confirms that attention-guided source selection is essential: replacing it with uniform random selection reduces hallucination rate gains from ~4.7-6.0 percentage points to only ~2.6-3.4 percentage points, pointing to credit-collapse as the key driver.

AI.tldr: SCR is a training-free, inference-time intervention that redistributes activation credit from high-attention visual patches to surrounding context, mitigating spatial credit collapse and reducing hallucinations in vision-language models. It yields substantial hallucination reductions across multiple model families and benchmarks with modest latency, outperforming several baselines and preserving CIDEr; the effect is strongest on low-entropy inputs.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models often hallucinate because early transformer layers concentrate credit on sparse patches, suppressing contextual evidence and over-relying on language priors. A real-time, training-free method is needed to rebalance activation credit without retraining.
AI.method: Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR) operates at inference time by reallocating hidden-state activation from high-attention source patches to their context, guided by low-entropy inputs. It was evaluated across six model families (Chameleon, LLaVA, Qwen variants including Qwen-VL and Qwen2-VL) at 7B, 13B, and 30B, on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks, with a controlled ablation to test attention-guided source selection.
AI.result: SCR reduces hallucination by ~4.7–6.0 percentage points on POPE-Adversarial, CHAIR-s by 3.7–5.2 points (42–51% relative), and CHAIR-i by 2.7–4.4 points (44–58% relative); CIDEr is preserved within 0.8 percentage points. Gains are larger for low-entropy inputs. Inference overhead is 43–56 ms, significantly lower than OPERA, VCD, and OVCD, making SCR Pareto-dominant for hallucination rate and CIDEr. An ablation shows that replacing attention-guided source selection with uniform random selection reduces gains, confirming credit-collapse as the key driver.
AI.conclusion: SCR is a practical, training-free defense against hallucinations in vision-language models, with attention-guided source selection identified as essential. It enables real-time deployment and demonstrates that mitigating spatial credit collapse yields robust improvements across diverse model families and benchmarks.

title: Does the testing environment matter? Carsickness across on-road, test-track, and driving simulator conditions
summary: Carsickness has gained significant attention with the rise of automated vehicles, prompting extensive research across on-road, test-track, and driving simulator environments to understand its occurrence and develop mitigation strategies. However, the lack of carsickness standardization complicates comparisons across studies and environments. Previous works demonstrate measurement validity between two setups at most (e.g., on-road vs. driving simulator), leaving gaps in multi-environment comparisons. This study investigates the recreation of an on-road motion sickness exposure - previously replicated on a test track - using a motion-based driving simulator. Twenty-eight participants performed an eyes-off-road non-driving task while reporting motion sickness using the Misery Scale during the experiment and the Motion Sickness Assessment Questionnaire afterward. Psychological factors known to influence motion sickness were also assessed. The results present subjective and objective measurements for motion sickness across the considered environments. In this paper, acceleration measurements, objective metrics and subjective motion sickness ratings across environments are compared, highlighting key differences in sickness occurrence for simulator-based research validity. Significantly lower motion sickness scores are reported in the simulator compared to on-road and test-track conditions, due to its limited working envelope to reproduce low-frequency (<0.5 Hz) motions, which are the most provocative for motion sickness.

AI.tldr: Simulator-based studies of carsickness show lower sickness than on-road/test-track, due to limited low-frequency motion reproduction (<0.5 Hz); raises cross-environment validity concerns and need for standardization.
AI.motivation: To enable meaningful cross-study comparisons of carsickness across environments and to fill gaps in multi-environment validation.
AI.method: 28 participants performed an eyes-off-road non-driving task; reported motion sickness with the Misery Scale during the experiment and with the Motion Sickness Assessment Questionnaire afterward; assessed acceleration, objective metrics, subjective ratings across on-road, test-track, and motion-based driving simulator; included psychological factors known to influence motion sickness.
AI.result: Significantly lower motion sickness scores in the simulator vs on-road/test-track; limited motion envelope reproduces low-frequency motions (<0.5 Hz) which are most provocative for motion sickness, explaining the differences in sickness occurrence across environments.
AI.conclusion: Cross-environment comparisons are challenged by simulator limitations; standardization is needed, and simulators should be enhanced to reproduce relevant low-frequency motions to improve validity of motion-sickness research across environments.

title: Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching
summary: Modern vision generators transport a base distribution to data through time-indexed measures, implemented as deterministic flows (ODEs) or stochastic diffusions (SDEs). Despite strong empirical performance, standard flow-matching objectives do not directly control the information geometry of the trajectory, allowing low-entropy bottlenecks that can transiently deplete semantic modes. We propose Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching (ECFM): a constrained variational principle over continuity-equation paths enforcing a global entropy-rate budget d/dt H(mu_t) >= -lambda. ECFM is a convex optimization in Wasserstein space with a KKT/Pontryagin system, and admits a stochastic-control representation equivalent to a Schrodinger bridge with an explicit entropy multiplier. In the pure transport regime, ECFM recovers entropic OT geodesics and Gamma-converges to classical OT as lambda -> 0. We further obtain certificate-style mode-coverage and density-floor guarantees with Lipschitz stability, and construct near-optimal collapse counterexamples for unconstrained flow matching.

AI.tldr: Entropy-Controlled Flow Matching (ECFM) introduces a global entropy-rate constraint on trajectory distributions to control information geometry in time-indexed generative flows, linking to Schrödinger bridges and entropic OT; it yields mode coverage guarantees and convergence to OT as lambda→0.
AI.motivation: Address low-entropy bottlenecks and incomplete mode coverage in deterministic SDE/ODE-based flow matching by enforcing an entropy budget along continuity paths.
AI.method: Constrained variational principle on the continuity equation; convex optimization in Wasserstein space with KKT/Pontryagin; stochastic-control representation equivalent to a Schrödinger bridge with an entropy multiplier; in the pure transport limit, recovers entropic OT geodesics and Gamma-converges to classical OT as lambda→0.
AI.result: Provides entropy-rate based certificates for mode-coverage and density-floor stability with Lipschitz guarantees; constructs near-optimal collapse counterexamples for unconstrained flow matching.
AI.conclusion: ECFM offers a principled approach to regulate the information geometry of learned flows, linking entropic OT and Schrödinger bridges, with theoretical guarantees and practical implications for mode coverage.

title: How Do Latent Reasoning Methods Perform Under Weak and Strong Supervision?
summary: Latent reasoning has been recently proposed as a reasoning paradigm and performs multi-step reasoning through generating steps in the latent space instead of the textual space. This paradigm enables reasoning beyond discrete language tokens by performing multi-step computation in continuous latent spaces. Although there have been numerous studies focusing on improving the performance of latent reasoning, its internal mechanisms remain not fully investigated. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of latent reasoning methods to better understand the role and behavior of latent representation in the process. We identify two key issues across latent reasoning methods with different levels of supervision. First, we observe pervasive shortcut behavior, where they achieve high accuracy without relying on latent reasoning. Second, we examine the hypothesis that latent reasoning supports BFS-like exploration in latent space, and find that while latent representations can encode multiple possibilities, the reasoning process does not faithfully implement structured search, but instead exhibits implicit pruning and compression. Finally, our findings reveal a trade-off associated with supervision strength: stronger supervision mitigates shortcut behavior but restricts the ability of latent representations to maintain diverse hypotheses, whereas weaker supervision allows richer latent representations at the cost of increased shortcut behavior.

AI.tldr: Latent reasoning often exhibits shortcut behavior and does not implement faithful BFS-like search in latent space; supervision strength mediates a trade-off between hypothesis diversity and shortcut avoidance.
AI.motivation: To dissect how latent representations drive reasoning, and to identify when and how latent reasoning relies on shortcuts vs genuine multi-step inference, across supervision levels.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of latent reasoning methods with varying supervision strength; empirical diagnostics of latent space structure, exploration patterns, and behavior akin to BFS; evaluation of trade-offs.
AI.result: Found pervasive shortcut behavior; latent space can encode multiple possibilities but the reasoning process prunes/compresses rather than performing structured search; stronger supervision reduces shortcuts but narrows latent diversity; weaker supervision increases diversity but raises shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental trade-off between supervision strength and latent hypothesis diversity; future work should aim to mitigate shortcuts while preserving rich latent representations, possibly by enforcing structured latent search or targeted regularization.

title: Pix2Key: Controllable Open-Vocabulary Retrieval with Semantic Decomposition and Self-Supervised Visual Dictionary Learning
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) uses a reference image plus a natural-language edit to retrieve images that apply the requested change while preserving other relevant visual content. Classic fusion pipelines typically rely on supervised triplets and can lose fine-grained cues, while recent zero-shot approaches often caption the reference image and merge the caption with the edit, which may miss implicit user intent and return repetitive results. We present Pix2Key, which represents both queries and candidates as open-vocabulary visual dictionaries, enabling intent-aware constraint matching and diversity-aware reranking in a unified embedding space. A self-supervised pretraining component, V-Dict-AE, further improves the dictionary representation using only images, strengthening fine-grained attribute understanding without CIR-specific supervision. On the DFMM-Compose benchmark, Pix2Key improves Recall@10 up to 3.2 points, and adding V-Dict-AE yields an additional 2.3-point gain while improving intent consistency and maintaining high list diversity.

AI.tldr: Pix2Key introduces an open-vocabulary dictionary-based representation for composed image retrieval, with a self-supervised pretraining (V-Dict-AE); yields gains on DFMM-Compose via intent-aware matching and diversity-aware reranking.
AI.motivation: CIR requires capturing user intent with fine-grained attributes; existing methods either rely on supervised triplets (prone to losing cues) or caption-based zero-shot approaches (may miss implicit intent and cause repetitive results).
AI.method: represent queries and candidates as open-vocabulary visual dictionaries; use intent-aware constraint matching and diversity-aware reranking in a unified embedding space; add self-supervised V-Dict-AE pretraining using only images to strengthen dictionary representation and fine-grained attribute understanding.
AI.result: On DFMM-Compose: Recall@10 up to 3.2 points; adding V-Dict-AE yields an additional 2.3-point gain; improved intent consistency and maintained high list diversity.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that dictionary-based representations plus self-supervised dictionary autoencoder improve CIR performance, offering better alignment with user intent and diversity; suggests potential for broader applicability and future work on efficiency and generalization.

title: SCOPE: Skeleton Graph-Based Computation-Efficient Framework for Autonomous UAV Exploration
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication in the IEEE ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION LETTERS (RA-L). Please cite the paper using appropriate formats
summary: Autonomous exploration in unknown environments is key for mobile robots, helping them perceive, map, and make decisions in complex areas. However, current methods often rely on frequent global optimization, suffering from high computational latency and trajectory oscillation, especially on resource-constrained edge devices. To address these limitations, we propose SCOPE, a novel framework that incrementally constructs a real-time skeletal graph and introduces Implicit Unknown Region Analysis for efficient spatial reasoning. The planning layer adopts a hierarchical on-demand strategy: the Proximal Planner generates smooth, high-frequency local trajectories, while the Region-Sequence Planner is activated only when necessary to optimize global visitation order. Comparative evaluations in simulation demonstrate that SCOPE achieves competitive exploration performance comparable to state-of-the-art global planners, while reducing computational cost by an average of 86.9%. Real-world experiments further validate the system's robustness and low latency in practical scenarios.

AI.tldr: SCOPE enables real-time autonomous exploration with an incremental skeletal graph and implicit unknown region analysis, using hierarchical on-demand planning (Proximal Planner for local trajectories and Region-Sequence Planner for global order) to cut latency and compute cost while maintaining performance. Validated in simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Address high latency and trajectory oscillation in exploration methods that rely on global optimization on edge devices; enable real-time, robust exploration in unknown environments.
AI.method: Incremental construction of a real-time skeletal graph; Implicit Unknown Region Analysis for spatial reasoning; hierarchical on-demand planning: Proximal Planner (local, high-frequency trajectories) and Region-Sequence Planner (global visitation order) activated as needed.
AI.result: Simulation shows competitive exploration performance to state-of-the-art global planners with ~86.9% reduction in computational cost; real-world experiments show robustness and low latency.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE offers efficient, robust real-time exploration by combining skeletal graph-based representation, implicit region analysis, and on-demand planning, achieving high performance with significantly reduced computation.

title: A Framework for Assessing AI Agent Decisions and Outcomes in AutoML Pipelines
summary: Agent-based AutoML systems rely on large language models to make complex, multi-stage decisions across data processing, model selection, and evaluation. However, existing evaluation practices remain outcome-centric, focusing primarily on final task performance. Through a review of prior work, we find that none of the surveyed agentic AutoML systems report structured, decision-level evaluation metrics intended for post-hoc assessment of intermediate decision quality. To address this limitation, we propose an Evaluation Agent (EA) that performs decision-centric assessment of AutoML agents without interfering with their execution. The EA is designed as an observer that evaluates intermediate decisions along four dimensions: decision validity, reasoning consistency, model quality risks beyond accuracy, and counterfactual decision impact. Across four proof-of-concept experiments, we demonstrate that the EA can (i) detect faulty decisions with an F1 score of 0.919, (ii) identify reasoning inconsistencies independent of final outcomes, and (iii) attribute downstream performance changes to agent decisions, revealing impacts ranging from -4.9\% to +8.3\% in final metrics. These results illustrate how decision-centric evaluation exposes failure modes that are invisible to outcome-only metrics. Our work reframes the evaluation of agentic AutoML systems from an outcome-based perspective to one that audits agent decisions, offering a foundation for reliable, interpretable, and governable autonomous ML systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes an Evaluation Agent (EA) for decision-centric auditing of agentic AutoML systems; demonstrates improved detection/attribution of decision quality to downstream outcomes; argues for governance-friendly, interpretable evaluation beyond final task performance.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation practices in agent-based AutoML are outcome-centric and lack structured, decision-level metrics to assess intermediate decision quality; need post-hoc auditing to ensure reliability and governance.
AI.method: Introduce Evaluation Agent (EA) as an observer that reviews intermediate decisions along four dimensions (decision validity, reasoning consistency, model quality risks beyond accuracy, counterfactual decision impact). Validate via four proof-of-concept experiments measuring fault detection (F1), reasoning consistency independent of outcomes, and attribution of downstream performance changes.
AI.result: EA achieves fault detection with F1 = 0.919; it detects reasoning inconsistencies independent of final outcomes and can attribute performance changes to agent decisions, with observed impacts ranging from -4.9% to +8.3% in final metrics.
AI.conclusion: Shifts evaluation from outcome-only to decision-centric auditing, enabling more reliable, interpretable, and governable autonomous ML systems; provides a foundation for future standards in AutoML evaluation.

title: DisQ-HNet: A Disentangled Quantized Half-UNet for Interpretable Multimodal Image Synthesis Applications to Tau-PET Synthesis from T1 and FLAIR MRI
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables; includes PID guided vector quantized latent factorization and sobel edge conditioned Half-UNet decoder
summary: Tau positron emission tomography (tau-PET) provides an in vivo marker of Alzheimer's disease pathology, but cost and limited availability motivate MRI-based alternatives. We introduce DisQ-HNet (DQH), a framework that synthesizes tau-PET from paired T1-weighted and FLAIR MRI while exposing how each modality contributes to the prediction. The method combines (i) a Partial Information Decomposition (PID)-guided, vector-quantized encoder that partitions latent information into redundant, unique, and complementary components, and (ii) a Half-UNet decoder that preserves anatomical detail using pseudo-skip connections conditioned on structural edge cues rather than direct encoder feature reuse. Across multiple baselines (VAE, VQ-VAE, and UNet), DisQ-HNet maintains reconstruction fidelity and better preserves disease-relevant signal for downstream AD tasks, including Braak staging, tau localization, and classification. PID-based Shapley analysis provides modality-specific attribution of synthesized uptake patterns.

AI.tldr: DisQ-HNet synthesizes tau-PET from paired T1-weighted and FLAIR MRI using a PID-guided vector-quantized encoder and a Half-UNet decoder, enabling interpretable modality contributions while preserving disease-relevant signals better than baselines.
AI.motivation: Tau-PET, an in vivo marker of Alzheimer's pathology, is expensive and not widely available. MRI-based alternatives are desirable; understanding how each MRI modality contributes to tau-PET synthesis is needed for interpretability and trust.
AI.method: The encoder uses Partial Information Decomposition (PID) to partition latent information into redundant, unique, and complementary components across modalities, via vector quantization. A Half-UNet decoder preserves anatomical detail using pseudo-skip connections conditioned on structural edge cues rather than direct encoder features. The framework is trained on paired T1 and FLAIR MRI to predict tau-PET and is evaluated against VAE, VQ-VAE, and UNet baselines. PID-based Shapley analysis provides modality-specific attribution of synthesized uptake patterns.
AI.result: DisQ-HNet maintains reconstruction fidelity while better preserving disease-relevant signal for downstream AD tasks, including Braak staging, tau localization, and classification. It outperforms the baseline models (VAE, VQ-VAE, UNet) in preserving tau-related patterns and enables modality-specific attribution through PID-based Shapley analysis.
AI.conclusion: DisQ-HNet provides an interpretable, MRI-based tau-PET synthesis framework that preserves key disease-related information and offers insights into each modality’s contribution, potentially enabling cost-effective biomarkers for Alzheimer’s disease research and clinical tasks.

title: Robust Helicopter Ship Deck Landing With Guaranteed Timing Using Shrinking-Horizon Model Predictive Control
comment: This version was submitted to the American Control Conference 2026 and has been accepted
summary: We present a runtime efficient algorithm for autonomous helicopter landings on moving ship decks based on Shrinking-Horizon Model Predictive Control (SHMPC). First, a suitable planning model capturing the relevant aspects of the full nonlinear helicopter dynamics is derived. Next, we use the SHMPC together with a touchdown controller stage to ensure a pre-specified maneuver time and an associated landing time window despite the presence of disturbances. A high disturbance rejection performance is achieved by designing an ancillary controller with disturbance feedback. Thus, given a target position and time, a safe landing with suitable terminal conditions is be guaranteed if the initial optimization problem is feasible. The efficacy of our approach is shown in simulation where all maneuvers achieve a high landing precision in strong winds while satisfying timing and operational constraints with maximum computation times in the millisecond range.

AI.tldr: A SHMPC-based framework for autonomous helicopter landings on moving decks, featuring a touchdown stage and disturbance-feedback control, achieving millisecond-scale real-time planning in simulation under strong winds.
AI.motivation: Enable safe, timely autonomous shipboard helicopter landings with robustness to disturbances and real-time computational feasibility.
AI.method: Derive a reduced nonlinear planning model of helicopter dynamics, apply Shrinking-Horizon MPC with a dedicated touchdown controller to meet a predefined maneuver and landing time window, and augment with a disturbance-feedback ancillary controller to enhance robustness; feasibility guarantees safe landing with appropriate terminal conditions if the optimization is feasible.
AI.result: Simulation demonstrates high landing precision in strong winds, adherence to timing/operational constraints, and computation times in the millisecond range.
AI.conclusion: SHMPC with a touchdown stage and disturbance feedback provides reliable, fast, and robust autonomous shipboard landings within specified time windows, contingent on accurate planning model and problem feasibility.

title: Data-Driven Supervision of a Thermal-Hydraulic Process Towards a Physics-Based Digital Twin
summary: The real-time supervision of production processes is a common challenge across several industries. It targets process component monitoring and its predictive maintenance in order to ensure safety, uninterrupted production and maintain high efficiency level. The rise of advanced tools for the simulation of physical systems in addition to data-driven machine learning models offers the possibility to design numerical tools dedicated to efficient system monitoring. In that respect, the digital twin concept presents an adequate framework that proffers solution to these challenges. The main purpose of this paper is to develop such a digital twin dedicated to fault detection and diagnosis in the context of a thermal-hydraulic process supervision. Based on a numerical simulation of the system, in addition to machine learning methods, we propose different modules dedicated to process parameter change detection and their on-line estimation. The proposed fault detection and diagnosis algorithm is validated on a specific test scenario, with single one-off parameter change occurrences in the system. The numerical results show good accuracy in terms of parameter variation localization and the update of their values.

AI.tldr: Digital twin-based fault detection/diagnosis for a thermal-hydraulic process using numerical simulation and ML, with online parameter-change detection and estimation; validated on a scenario showing accurate localization and value updates.
AI.motivation: Provide real-time monitoring and predictive maintenance for safe, efficient production; leverage physics-based simulation + data-driven ML within digital twin framework.
AI.method: Develop a digital twin; integrate numerical system simulation with ML components; implement modules for online parameter-change detection and online estimation; propose a fault detection/diagnosis algorithm; validate on test scenario with single parameter change.
AI.result: Numerical results show good accuracy in localizing parameter variations and updating their values.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of a hybrid physics-ML digital twin for fault detection/diagnosis in thermal-hydraulic supervision; supports real-time monitoring and parameter tracking.

title: CWM: Contrastive World Models for Action Feasibility Learning in Embodied Agent Pipelines
summary: A reliable action feasibility scorer is a critical bottleneck in embodied agent pipelines: before any planning or reasoning occurs, the agent must identify which candidate actions are physically executable in the current state. Existing approaches use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to train action scorers, but SFT treats each candidate independently and does not explicitly teach the model to discriminate between actions that are physically correct and those that are subtly wrong. We propose the Contrastive World Model (CWM), which fine-tunes a large language model (LLM) as an action scorer using an InfoNCE contrastive objective with hard-mined negative examples. The key idea is to push valid actions away from invalid ones in scoring space, with special emphasis on hard negatives: semantically similar but physically incompatible candidates. We evaluate CWM on the ScienceWorld benchmark through two studies. First, an intrinsic affordance evaluation on 605 hard-negative test pairs shows that CWM outperforms SFT by +6.76 percentage points on Precision@1 for minimal-edit negatives -- cases where a single word changes the physical outcome -- and achieves a higher AUC-ROC (0.929 vs. 0.906). Second, a live filter characterisation study measures how well CWM ranks gold-path actions against all valid environment actions during task execution. Under out-of-distribution stress conditions, CWM maintains a significantly better safety margin (-2.39) than SFT (-3.96), indicating that the gold action is ranked closer to the top. These results support the hypothesis that contrastive training induces representations that capture physical feasibility more faithfully than SFT alone.

AI.tldr: Contrastive World Model (CWM) improves action feasibility scoring over SFT using InfoNCE with hard negatives; achieves higher Precision@1 and AUC-ROC, and better safety margins in live tests.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents need reliable action feasibility scoring to prune candidate actions; SFT treats actions independently and fails to distinguish subtly invalid actions. A contrastive objective can separate feasible from infeasible actions, especially for hard negatives.
AI.method: Fine-tune a large language model as an action scorer using an InfoNCE contrastive objective with hard-mined negative examples, pushing valid actions away from invalid ones in scoring space; evaluate on ScienceWorld with intrinsic (605 hard-negative pairs) and live filter characterization protocols.
AI.result: CWM outperforms SFT: Precision@1 improves by 6.76 points on minimal-edit negatives; AUC-ROC rises to 0.929 (vs 0.906). In a live, out-of-distribution stress test, CWM achieves a safer ranking with a margin of -2.39 versus SFT's -3.96; gold actions ranked closer to the top.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive training yields representations that better capture physical feasibility than SFT alone, providing more reliable action scoring for embodied planning and improved safety margins.

title: DrivePTS: A Progressive Learning Framework with Textual and Structural Enhancement for Driving Scene Generation
summary: Synthesis of diverse driving scenes serves as a crucial data augmentation technique for validating the robustness and generalizability of autonomous driving systems. Current methods aggregate high-definition (HD) maps and 3D bounding boxes as geometric conditions in diffusion models for conditional scene generation. However, implicit inter-condition dependency causes generation failures when control conditions change independently. Additionally, these methods suffer from insufficient details in both semantic and structural aspects. Specifically, brief and view-invariant captions restrict semantic contexts, resulting in weak background modeling. Meanwhile, the standard denoising loss with uniform spatial weighting neglects foreground structural details, causing visual distortions and blurriness. To address these challenges, we propose DrivePTS, which incorporates three key innovations. Firstly, our framework adopts a progressive learning strategy to mitigate inter-dependency between geometric conditions, reinforced by an explicit mutual information constraint. Secondly, a Vision-Language Model is utilized to generate multi-view hierarchical descriptions across six semantic aspects, providing fine-grained textual guidance. Thirdly, a frequency-guided structure loss is introduced to strengthen the model's sensitivity to high-frequency elements, improving foreground structural fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DrivePTS achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability in generating diverse driving scenes. Notably, DrivePTS successfully generates rare scenes where prior methods fail, highlighting its strong generalization ability.

AI.tldr: DrivePTS is a diffusion-based framework for conditional driving-scene synthesis that tackles inter-condition dependency, semantic richness, and high-frequency structural details via progressive learning with mutual-information decoupling, a Vision-Language model for multi-view hierarchical descriptions, and a frequency-guided loss.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, controllable, and richly described synthetic driving scenes for data augmentation and evaluation of autonomous systems, addressing failures from inter-condition dependencies and poor foreground/semantic details in existing HD-map/3D-box conditioned diffusion methods.
AI.method: 1) Progressive learning with an explicit mutual information constraint to reduce inter-dependency among geometric conditions. 2) A Vision-Language Model to produce multi-view hierarchical textual descriptions across six semantic aspects for fine-grained guidance. 3) A frequency-guided structure loss to emphasize high-frequency foreground details during denoising.
AI.result: Empirical results claim state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability, successful generation of diverse and rare scenes that previous methods fail to produce, indicating strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: DrivePTS provides a robust, controllable, and detailed framework for synthetic driving-scene generation, improving semantic and structural fidelity and offering practical benefits for data augmentation and robustness testing.

title: Sapling-NeRF: Geo-Localised Sapling Reconstruction in Forests for Ecological Monitoring
summary: Saplings are key indicators of forest regeneration and overall forest health. However, their fine-scale architectural traits are difficult to capture with existing 3D sensing methods, which make quantitative evaluation difficult. Terrestrial Laser Scanners (TLS), Mobile Laser Scanners (MLS), or traditional photogrammetry approaches poorly reconstruct thin branches, dense foliage, and lack the scale consistency needed for long-term monitoring. Implicit 3D reconstruction methods such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) are promising alternatives, but cannot recover the true scale of a scene and lack any means to be accurately geo-localised. In this paper, we present a pipeline which fuses NeRF, LiDAR SLAM, and GNSS to enable repeatable, geo-localised ecological monitoring of saplings. Our system proposes a three-level representation: (i) coarse Earth-frame localisation using GNSS, (ii) LiDAR-based SLAM for centimetre-accurate localisation and reconstruction, and (iii) NeRF-derived object-centric dense reconstruction of individual saplings. This approach enables repeatable quantitative evaluation and long-term monitoring of sapling traits. Our experiments in forest plots in Wytham Woods (Oxford, UK) and Evo (Finland) show that stem height, branching patterns, and leaf-to-wood ratios can be captured with increased accuracy as compared to TLS. We demonstrate that accurate stem skeletons and leaf distributions can be measured for saplings with heights between 0.5m and 2m in situ, giving ecologists access to richer structural and quantitative data for analysing forest dynamics.

AI.tldr: A multi-sensor, multi-scale pipeline fusing GNSS, LiDAR SLAM, and NeRF to enable geo-localised, repeatable 3D reconstruction of saplings for long-term ecological monitoring, overcoming limitations of TLS/MLS/photogrammetry for fine-scale traits.
AI.motivation: Forest managers require accurate, scale-consistent measurements of sapling architecture (height, branching, leaf–wood ratios) for regeneration assessment and forest health; existing 3D sensing methods struggle with thin branches, dense foliage, scale, and geo-localisation for long-term monitoring.
AI.method: Three-level representation: (i) coarse Earth-frame localisation with GNSS; (ii) centimetre-accurate LiDAR-based SLAM for localisation and reconstruction; (iii) NeRF-derived object-centric dense reconstruction of individual saplings. Fusion of GNSS, LiDAR SLAM, and NeRF/3DGS to produce geo-localised, repeatable reconstructions. Field validation in Wytham Woods (UK) and Evo (Finland).
AI.result: Demonstrates measurable improvements in stem height, branching patterns, and leaf-to-wood ratios compared with TLS. Achieves accurate stem skeletons and leaf distributions for saplings 0.5–2 m tall in situ, enabling richer structural and quantitative data for forest dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GNSS–LiDAR SLAM–NeRF pipeline provides repeatable, geo-localised, object-centric 3D reconstructions of saplings, enabling long-term ecological monitoring and quantitative trait extraction that surpasses traditional TLS-based approaches.

title: AutoQRA: Joint Optimization of Mixed-Precision Quantization and Low-rank Adapters for Efficient LLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Quantization followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning has emerged as a promising paradigm for downstream adaptation under tight GPU memory constraints. However, this sequential pipeline fails to leverage the intricate interaction between quantization bit-width and LoRA rank. Specifically, a carefully optimized quantization allocation with low quantization error does not always translate to strong fine-tuning performance, and different bit-width and rank configurations can lead to significantly varying outcomes under the same memory budget. To address this limitation, we propose AutoQRA, a joint optimization framework that simultaneously optimizes the bit-width and LoRA rank configuration for each layer during the mixed quantized fine-tuning process. To tackle the challenges posed by the large discrete search space and the high evaluation cost associated with frequent fine-tuning iterations, AutoQRA decomposes the optimization process into two stages. First, it first conducts a global multi-fidelity evolutionary search, where the initial population is warm-started by injecting layer-wise importance priors. This stage employs specific operators and a performance model to efficiently screen candidate configurations. Second, trust-region Bayesian optimization is applied to locally refine promising regions of the search space and identify optimal configurations under the given memory budget. This approach enables active compensation for quantization noise in specific layers during training. Experiments show that AutoQRA achieves performance close to full-precision fine-tuning with a memory footprint comparable to uniform 4-bit methods.

AI.tldr: AutoQRA jointly optimizes per-layer quantization bit-width and LoRA rank during mixed-precision fine-tuning, using a two-stage search (global multi-fidelity evolutionary search with priors, then trust-region Bayesian optimization) to meet memory budgets and approach full-precision performance.
AI.motivation: Quantization bit-width and LoRA rank interact in nontrivial ways; sequential quantization followed by fine-tuning underutilizes the memory budget and yields suboptimal adaptation. A joint optimization can better exploit trade-offs to improve performance under tight memory constraints.
AI.method: Two-stage optimization: (1) global multi-fidelity evolutionary search with layer-wise importance priors to explore configurations efficiently, aided by a performance model; (2) trust-region Bayesian optimization to locally refine promising configurations under the given memory budget. The framework targets per-layer bit-width and LoRA rank jointly during mixed quantized fine-tuning, with mechanisms to compensate quantization noise and reduce evaluation cost.
AI.result: Experiments show AutoQRA achieves performance close to full-precision fine-tuning while keeping memory usage comparable to uniform 4-bit methods.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of per-layer bit-width and LoRA rank in a staged search framework effectively navigates the large discrete search space, enabling efficient mixed-precision fine-tuning under strict memory budgets and near-UL performance.

title: ConstraintBench: Benchmarking LLM Constraint Reasoning on Direct Optimization
comment: Preprint. 10 pages, 1 figure, 6 tables. Benchmark and evaluation code will be publicly released
summary: Large language models are increasingly applied to operational decision-making where the underlying structure is constrained optimization. Existing benchmarks evaluate whether LLMs can formulate optimization problems as solver code, but leave open a complementary question. Can LLMs directly produce correct solutions to fully specified constrained optimization problems without access to a solver? We introduce ConstraintBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on direct constrained optimization across 10 operations research domains, with all ground-truth solutions verified by the Gurobi solver. Each task presents a natural-language scenario with entities, constraints, and an optimization objective; the model must return a structured solution that a deterministic verifier checks against every constraint and the solver-proven optimum. We evaluate six frontier models on 200 tasks and find that feasibility, not optimality, is the primary bottleneck. The best model achieves only 65.0% constraint satisfaction, yet feasible solutions average 89 to 96% of the Gurobi-optimal objective. No model exceeds 30.5% on joint feasibility and optimality within 0.1% of the solver reference. Per-domain analysis shows large variation in difficulty, with average feasibility spanning from 83.3% in the production mix domain to 0.8% in the crew assignment domain. Further, systematic failure modes include duration constraint misunderstanding, entity hallucination, and a feasibility-optimality decoupling in facility location and vehicle routing where models achieve high feasibility but 0% optimality. ConstraintBench and all evaluation infrastructure will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: ConstraintBench evaluates whether LLMs can directly solve fully specified constrained optimization problems, revealing feasibility as the main bottleneck and limited joint feasibility+optimality across 200 tasks in 10 OR domains; six frontier models show modest performance with solver-proven ground-truth verification.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in evaluating LLMs' ability to produce correct, solver-free solutions to constrained optimization, not just translate problems into solver code.
AI.method: ConstraintBench presents 200 tasks across 10 operations research domains with natural-language scenarios (entities, constraints, objective). Models must output a structured solution that a deterministic verifier checks for constraint satisfaction and near-optimality against the Gurobi solver’s optimum. Ground-truth solutions are verified by the solver; six frontier models are evaluated.
AI.result: Best model achieves 65.0% constraint satisfaction; feasible solutions attain 89–96% of Gurobi-optimal objective; no model surpasses 30.5% on joint feasibility and optimality within 0.1% of solver reference; domain variation ranges from 83.3% feasibility (production mix) to 0.8% (crew assignment); common failure modes include duration constraint misunderstanding and entity hallucination; notable feasibility–optimality decoupling in facility location and vehicle routing; evaluation infrastructure to be released.
AI.conclusion: ConstraintBench provides a rigorous, public benchmark for direct constrained optimization by LLMs, highlighting feasibility as the primary hurdle, substantial domain-specific variability, and the need for methods that bridge feasibility and optimality in solver-free settings.

title: SwiftNDC: Fast Neural Depth Correction for High-Fidelity 3D Reconstruction
summary: Depth-guided 3D reconstruction has gained popularity as a fast alternative to optimization-heavy approaches, yet existing methods still suffer from scale drift, multi-view inconsistencies, and the need for substantial refinement to achieve high-fidelity geometry. Here, we propose SwiftNDC, a fast and general framework built around a Neural Depth Correction field that produces cross-view consistent depth maps. From these refined depths, we generate a dense point cloud through back-projection and robust reprojection-error filtering, obtaining a clean and uniformly distributed geometric initialization for downstream reconstruction. This reliable dense geometry substantially accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for mesh reconstruction, enabling high-quality surfaces with significantly fewer optimization iterations. For novel-view synthesis, SwiftNDC can also improve 3DGS rendering quality, highlighting the benefits of strong geometric initialization. We conduct a comprehensive study across five datasets, including two for mesh reconstruction, as well as three for novel-view synthesis. SwiftNDC consistently reduces running time for accurate mesh reconstruction and boosts rendering fidelity for view synthesis, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining neural depth refinement with robust geometric initialization for high-fidelity and efficient 3D reconstruction.

AI.tldr: SwiftNDC introduces a Neural Depth Correction field to produce cross-view-consistent depth maps, enabling fast, robust dense geometry initialization that accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting and improves novel-view synthesis.
AI.motivation: Prevent scale drift and multi-view inconsistencies in depth-guided 3D reconstruction while reducing optimization burden.
AI.method: Compute cross-view-consistent depth via a Neural Depth Correction field; back-project to dense point cloud with reprojection-error filtering; use as initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting; optionally enhance novel-view rendering.
AI.result: Consistent runtime reductions and improved rendering fidelity across five datasets (two mesh-reconstruction, three view-synthesis); fewer optimization iterations required for high-fidelity surfaces.
AI.conclusion: Combining neural depth refinement with robust geometric initialization yields fast, high-quality 3D reconstruction and improved view synthesis.

title: Pixel2Catch: Multi-Agent Sim-to-Real Transfer for Agile Manipulation with a Single RGB Camera
summary: To catch a thrown object, a robot must be able to perceive the object's motion and generate control actions in a timely manner. Rather than explicitly estimating the object's 3D position, this work focuses on a novel approach that recognizes object motion using pixel-level visual information extracted from a single RGB image. Such visual cues capture changes in the object's position and scale, allowing the policy to reason about the object's motion. Furthermore, to achieve stable learning in a high-DoF system composed of a robot arm equipped with a multi-fingered hand, we design a heterogeneous multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that defines the arm and hand as independent agents with distinct roles. Each agent is trained cooperatively using role-specific observations and rewards, and the learned policies are successfully transferred from simulation to the real world.

AI.tldr: Vision-based motion recognition from a single RGB frame substitutes full 3D pose estimation for catching, paired with a heterogeneous MARL framework where the arm and hand are separate cooperative agents, enabling stable learning and sim-to-real transfer in a high-DoF robot.
AI.motivation: Address the reliance on explicit 3D pose estimation for fast manipulation and improve learning stability in high-DoF robotic systems, by leveraging pixel-level motion cues.
AI.method: Extract pixel-level visual cues from a single RGB image to infer object motion; model the arm and hand as independent, role-specific agents within a cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning framework; train in simulation and transfer to real robot.
AI.result: Demonstrates successful catching using the learned policies in the real world with effective sim-to-real transfer and stable learning in a high-DoF system.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-based motion understanding combined with role-specific heterogeneous MARL yields effective high-DoF manipulation for catching, reducing dependence on precise 3D pose estimation and enabling robust sim-to-real transfer.

title: CQSA: Byzantine-robust Clustered Quantum Secure Aggregation in Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data. However, shared local model updates remain vulnerable to inference and poisoning attacks. Secure aggregation schemes have been proposed to mitigate these attacks. In this work, we aim to understand how these techniques are implemented in quantum-assisted FL. Quantum Secure Aggregation (QSA) has been proposed, offering information-theoretic privacy by encoding client updates into the global phase of multipartite entangled states. Existing QSA protocols, however, rely on a single global Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state shared among all participating clients. This design poses fundamental challenges: fidelity of large-scale GHZ states deteriorates rapidly with the increasing number of clients; and (ii) the global aggregation prevents the detection of Byzantine clients. We propose Clustered Quantum Secure Aggregation (CQSA), a modular aggregation framework that reconciles the physical constraints of near-term quantum hardware along with the need for Byzantine-robustness in FL. CQSA randomly partitions the clients into small clusters, each performing local quantum aggregation using high-fidelity, low-qubit GHZ states. The server analyzes statistical relationships between cluster-level aggregates employing common statistical measures such as cosine similarity and Euclidean distance to identify malicious contributions.
