Pre-training improves initialization under modest compute cost. Code, trained models, and data splits released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: A principled multi-modal architecture with cross-attention and Boltzmann-weighted conformer priors substantially boosts molecular property prediction over single-modal baselines; conformer ensembles and cross-modal fusion provide complementary gains; pre-training further enhances performance, with open-source resources to support reproducibility.

title: When Should an AI Act? A Human-Centered Model of Scene, Context, and Behavior for Agentic AI Design
summary: Agentic AI increasingly intervenes proactively by inferring users' situations from contextual data yet often fails for lack of principled judgment about when, why, and whether to act. We address this gap by proposing a conceptual model that reframes behavior as an interpretive outcome integrating Scene (observable situation), Context (user-constructed meaning), and Human Behavior Factors (determinants shaping behavioral likelihood). Grounded in multidisciplinary perspectives across the humanities, social sciences, HCI, and engineering, the model separates what is observable from what is meaningful to the user and explains how the same scene can yield different behavioral meanings and outcomes. To translate this lens into design action, we derive five agent design principles (behavioral alignment, contextual sensitivity, temporal appropriateness, motivational calibration, and agency preservation) that guide intervention depth, timing, intensity, and restraint. Together, the model and principles provide a foundation for designing agentic AI systems that act with contextual sensitivity and judgment in interactions.

AI.tldr: A Scene–Context–Human Behavior Factors model with five design principles empowers agentic AI to intervene with contextual sensitivity and prudent judgment.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of principled judgment in proactive agentic AI by distinguishing observable scenes from user-meaningful context and linking them to behavioral likelihood.
AI.method: Synthesis of multidisciplinary perspectives (humanities, social sciences, HCI, engineering) to formulate an interpretive model that separates observable data from meaning and to derive design principles.
AI.result: A conceptual model (Scene, Context, Human Behavior Factors) and five design principles—behavioral alignment, contextual sensitivity, temporal appropriateness, motivational calibration, and agency preservation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a foundation for designing agentic AI systems that act with contextual sensitivity and judgment in interactions.

title: No Caption, No Problem: Caption-Free Membership Inference via Model-Fitted Embeddings
summary: Latent diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in high-fidelity text-to-image generation, but their tendency to memorize training data raises critical privacy and intellectual property concerns. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) provide a principled way to audit such memorization by determining whether a given sample was included in training. However, existing approaches assume access to ground-truth captions. This assumption fails in realistic scenarios where only images are available and their textual annotations remain undisclosed, rendering prior methods ineffective when substituted with vision-language model (VLM) captions. In this work, we propose MoFit, a caption-free MIA framework that constructs synthetic conditioning inputs that are explicitly overfitted to the target model's generative manifold. Given a query image, MoFit proceeds in two stages: (i) model-fitted surrogate optimization, where a perturbation applied to the image is optimized to construct a surrogate in regions of the model's unconditional prior learned from member samples, and (ii) surrogate-driven embedding extraction, where a model-fitted embedding is derived from the surrogate and then used as a mismatched condition for the query image. This embedding amplifies conditional loss responses for member samples while leaving hold-outs relatively less affected, thereby enhancing separability in the absence of ground-truth captions. Our comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and diffusion models demonstrate that MoFit consistently outperforms prior VLM-conditioned baselines and achieves performance competitive with caption-dependent methods.

AI.tldr: MoFit is a caption-free membership inference attack against latent diffusion models, using a two-stage surrogate optimization to construct a synthetic conditioning input and extracted embedding, achieving strong separability without ground-truth captions.
AI.motivation: To audit memorization in diffusion models when ground-truth captions are unavailable; existing MIAs rely on captions, limiting applicability in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: 2-stage pipeline: (i) model-fitted surrogate optimization to perturb the query image and craft a surrogate within the model's unconditional prior learned from member data; (ii) surrogate-driven embedding extraction to obtain a model-fitted embedding from the surrogate, used as a mismatched conditioning for the query image; the embedding amplifies conditional loss on member samples, improving separability.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple datasets and diffusion models shows MoFit surpasses state-of-the-art VLM-conditioned baselines and is competitive with caption-dependent MIAs.
AI.conclusion: Caption-free MIAs for diffusion models are viable and effective; MoFit reduces reliance on textual annotations while maintaining privacy auditing performance, with implications for security and privacy in generative models.

title: Latent Gaussian Splatting for 4D Panoptic Occupancy Tracking
summary: Capturing 4D spatiotemporal surroundings is crucial for the safe and reliable operation of robots in dynamic environments. However, most existing methods address only one side of the problem: they either provide coarse geometric tracking via bounding boxes, or detailed 3D structures like voxel-based occupancy that lack explicit temporal association. In this work, we present Latent Gaussian Splatting for 4D Panoptic Occupancy Tracking (LaGS) that advances spatiotemporal scene understanding in a holistic direction. Our approach incorporates camera-based end-to-end tracking with mask-based multi-view panoptic occupancy prediction, and addresses the key challenge of efficiently aggregating multi-view information into 3D voxel grids via a novel latent Gaussian splatting approach. Specifically, we first fuse observations into 3D Gaussians that serve as a sparse point-centric latent representation of the 3D scene, and then splat the aggregated features onto a 3D voxel grid that is decoded by a mask-based segmentation head. We evaluate LaGS on the Occ3D nuScenes and Waymo datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking. We make our code available at https://lags.cs.uni-freiburg.de/.

AI.tldr: LaGS introduces latent Gaussian splatting for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking, fusing multi-view data into 3D Gaussians and projecting onto a voxel grid for mask-based segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on Occ3D nuScenes and Waymo.
AI.motivation: Capture robust, holistic 4D spatiotemporal scene understanding for dynamic environments; existing methods either provide coarse geometric tracking (bounding boxes) or dense 3D occupancy without temporal association.
AI.method: Latent Gaussian Splatting (LaGS) fuses observations into sparse 3D Gaussians as a point-centric latent representation, then splats aggregated features onto a 3D voxel grid decoded by a mask-based segmentation head for 4D panoptic occupancy. It combines camera-based end-to-end tracking with multi-view panoptic occupancy prediction.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance for 4D panoptic occupancy tracking on the Occ3D nuScenes and Waymo datasets.
AI.conclusion: LaGS enables efficient, holistic 4D scene understanding by leveraging a sparse latent Gaussian representation to fuse multi-view information into voxel grids, suitable for accurate 4D panoptic occupancy tracking.

title: A Learning-Based Hybrid Decision Framework for Matching Systems with User Departure Detection
summary: In matching markets such as kidney exchanges and freight exchanges, delayed matching has been shown to improve overall market efficiency. The benefits of delay are highly sensitive to participants' sojourn times and departure behavior, and delaying matches can impose significant costs, including longer waiting times and increased market congestion. These competing effects make fixed matching policies inherently inflexible in dynamic environments. We propose a learning-based Hybrid framework that adaptively combines immediate and delayed matching. The framework continuously collects data on user departures over time, estimates the underlying departure distribution via regression, and determines whether to delay matching in the subsequent period based on a decision threshold that governs the system's tolerance for matching efficiency loss. The proposed framework can substantially reduce waiting times and congestion while sacrificing only a limited amount of matching efficiency. By dynamically adjusting its matching strategy, the Hybrid framework enables system performance to flexibly interpolate between purely greedy and purely patient policies, offering a robust and adaptive alternative to static matching mechanisms.

AI.tldr: A learning-based Hybrid framework adaptively blends immediate and delayed matching in dynamic markets to reduce waiting and congestion with modest efficiency loss.
AI.motivation: In markets like kidney exchanges and freight exchanges, delay can improve overall efficiency but effects depend on how long participants stay and when they depart. Fixed, static matching policies are inflexible in dynamic environments, potentially causing excessive waiting time or congestion.
AI.method: Develop a Hybrid framework that continuously collects departure data, estimates the departure distribution via regression, and uses a decision threshold to decide whether to delay matches in the next period. This yields an adaptive policy that interpolates between immediate (greedy) and delayed (patient) matching.
AI.result: The framework substantially reduces waiting times and congestion with only a limited sacrifice in matching efficiency, by flexibly adjusting the mix of immediate and delayed matching.
AI.conclusion: The Hybrid framework provides a robust, adaptive alternative to static matching mechanisms, enabling performance to interpolate between purely greedy and purely patient policies in dynamic markets.

title: FlexMS is a flexible framework for benchmarking deep learning-based mass spectrum prediction tools in metabolomics
summary: The identification and property prediction of chemical molecules is of central importance in the advancement of drug discovery and material science, where the tandem mass spectrometry technology gives valuable fragmentation cues in the form of mass-to-charge ratio peaks. However, the lack of experimental spectra hinders the attachment of each molecular identification, and thus urges the establishment of prediction approaches for computational models. Deep learning models appear promising for predicting molecular structure spectra, but overall assessment remains challenging as a result of the heterogeneity in methods and the lack of well-defined benchmarks. To address this, our contribution is the creation of benchmark framework FlexMS for constructing and evaluating diverse model architectures in mass spectrum prediction. With its easy-to-use flexibility, FlexMS supports the dynamic construction of numerous distinct combinations of model architectures, while assessing their performance on preprocessed public datasets using different metrics. In this paper, we provide insights into factors influencing performance, including the structural diversity of datasets, hyperparameters like learning rate and data sparsity, pretraining effects, metadata ablation settings and cross-domain transfer learning analysis. This provides practical guidance in choosing suitable models. Moreover, retrieval benchmarks simulate practical identification scenarios and score potential matches based on predicted spectra.

AI.tldr: FlexMS provides a flexible benchmark framework for predicting and identifying mass spectra, enabling dynamic model architecture construction, multi-metric evaluation on diverse public datasets, and retrieval-based identification tasks.
AI.motivation: Bridge the heterogeneity and lack of standardized benchmarks in deep learning for mass spectrum prediction by offering a modular, extensible framework to compare architectures, datasets, and training strategies.
AI.method: Develop FlexMS to allow dynamic composition of model architectures; preprocess and evaluate on public mass-spectrometry datasets; measure performance with multiple metrics; analyze factors such as structural diversity, learning rate, data sparsity, pretraining, metadata ablation, and cross-domain transfer; include retrieval benchmarks for practical identification.
AI.result: A benchmark framework that enables systematic, cross-domain evaluation of mass-spectrum prediction models, with insights into how dataset diversity, hyperparameters, pretraining, and metadata decisions affect performance; provides guidance for selecting suitable models and settings.
AI.conclusion: FlexMS offers a practical, flexible benchmarking platform for model development and evaluation in mass spectrum prediction, including retrieval-based identification scenarios and cross-domain transfer analysis.

title: GFRRN: Explore the Gaps in Single Image Reflection Removal
summary: Prior dual-stream methods with the feature interaction mechanism have achieved remarkable performance in single image reflection removal (SIRR). However, they often struggle with (1) semantic understanding gap between the features of pre-trained models and those of reflection removal models, and (2) reflection label inconsistencies between synthetic and real-world training data. In this work, we first adopt the parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategy by integrating several learnable Mona layers into the pre-trained model to align the training directions. Then, a label generator is designed to unify the reflection labels for both synthetic and real-world data. In addition, a Gaussian-based Adaptive Frequency Learning Block (G-AFLB) is proposed to adaptively learn and fuse the frequency priors, and a Dynamic Agent Attention (DAA) is employed as an alternative to window-based attention by dynamically modeling the significance levels across windows (inter-) and within an individual window (intra-). These components constitute our proposed Gap-Free Reflection Removal Network (GFRRN). Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our GFRRN, achieving superior performance against state-of-the-art SIRR methods.

AI.tldr: GFRRN: A gap-free SIRR model that uses PEFT with Mona layers, a label generator for synthetic/real consistency, Gaussian-based Adaptive Frequency Learning Block, and Dynamic Agent Attention, achieving state-of-the-art results on single image reflection removal.
AI.motivation: Address two core bottlenecks in SIRR: (1) the semantic understanding gap between features from pre-trained backbones and reflection removal tasks, and (2) reflection label inconsistencies between synthetic and real-world data that hinder generalization.
AI.method: Integrate Mona-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) into a pre-trained backbone; introduce a label generator to unify reflection labels across synthetic/real data; design Gaussian-based Adaptive Frequency Learning Block (G-AFLB) to learn and fuse frequency priors; replace window-based attention with Dynamic Agent Attention (DAA) to model inter- and intra-window significance; assemble these into the Gap-Free Reflection Removal Network (GFRRN).
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that GFRRN achieves superior performance compared with state-of-the-art single image reflection removal methods.
AI.conclusion: GFRRN effectively mitigates semantic gaps and label inconsistencies in SIRR while delivering state-of-the-art performance, and introduces modular components (PEFT, label generator, G-AFLB, DAA) that can benefit reflection removal and related tasks.

title: Motion-aware Event Suppression for Event Cameras
summary: In this work, we introduce the first framework for Motion-aware Event Suppression, which learns to filter events triggered by IMOs and ego-motion in real time. Our model jointly segments IMOs in the current event stream while predicting their future motion, enabling anticipatory suppression of dynamic events before they occur. Our lightweight architecture achieves 173 Hz inference on consumer-grade GPUs with less than 1 GB of memory usage, outperforming previous state-of-the-art methods on the challenging EVIMO benchmark by 67\% in segmentation accuracy while operating at a 53\% higher inference rate. Moreover, we demonstrate significant benefits for downstream applications: our method accelerates Vision Transformer inference by 83\% via token pruning and improves event-based visual odometry accuracy, reducing Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) by 13\%.

AI.tldr: A real-time Motion-aware Event Suppression framework that jointly segments IMOs and predicts their future motion to suppress events before occurrence, delivering high efficiency and downstream gains.
AI.motivation: To reduce erroneous or disruptive events caused by ego-motion and IMOs in event streams and to improve downstream tasks by anticipatory suppression.
AI.method: A lightweight architecture that jointly segments IMOs in the current event stream and predicts their future motion, enabling anticipatory suppression of dynamic events; achieves high runtime efficiency (173 Hz, <1 GB) and supports downstream acceleration. Also demonstrates effectiveness on EVIMO benchmark and token-pruning for ViT inference.
AI.result: 173 Hz inference on consumer GPUs with memory <1 GB; 67% improvement in segmentation accuracy on EVIMO; 53% higher inference rate vs. baselines; 83% acceleration of Vision Transformer inference via token pruning; 13% improvement in event-based VO ATE.
AI.conclusion: Motion-aware event suppression can substantially improve real-time event processing and downstream vision tasks, offering a practical approach for robust, high-speed event-based perception systems.

title: Revisiting Chebyshev Polynomial and Anisotropic RBF Models for Tabular Regression
comment: 32 pages, 6 figures, 11 tables. Submitted to Information Sciences
summary:
Smooth-basis models such as Chebyshev polynomial regressors and radial basis function (RBF) networks are well established in numerical analysis. Their continuously differentiable prediction surfaces suit surrogate optimisation, sensitivity analysis, and other settings where the response varies gradually with inputs. Despite these properties, smooth models seldom appear in tabular regression, where tree ensembles dominate. We ask whether they can compete, benchmarking models across 55 regression datasets organised by application domain.
  We develop an anisotropic RBF network with data-driven centre placement and gradient-based width optimisation, a ridge-regularised Chebyshev polynomial regressor, and a smooth-tree hybrid (Chebyshev model tree); all three are released as scikit-learn-compatible packages. We benchmark these against tree ensembles, a pre-trained transformer, and standard baselines, evaluating accuracy alongside generalisation behaviour.
  The transformer ranks first on accuracy across a majority of datasets, but its GPU dependence, inference latency, and dataset-size limits constrain deployment in the CPU-based settings common across applied science and industry. Among CPU-viable models, smooth models and tree ensembles are statistically tied on accuracy, but the former tend to exhibit tighter generalisation gaps. We recommend routinely including smooth-basis models in the candidate pool, particularly when downstream use benefits from tighter generalisation and gradually varying predictions.

AI.tldr: A head-to-head benchmark of smooth-basis regression models (anisotropic RBF with data-driven centers and width optimization, ridge Chebyshev regression, and smooth Chebyshev model trees) against tree ensembles and a transformer on 55 tabular regression datasets. Transformer usually wins on accuracy but CPU-only deployment favors smooth-basis models; smooth models show tighter generalisation gaps and are statistically competitive with trees.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether smooth-basis models can meaningfully compete with tree ensembles in tabular regression, and to understand their generalisation and deployment characteristics.
AI.method: Develop three smooth-basis models (anisotropic RBF with data-driven centers and gradient-based width optimization; ridge-regularised Chebyshev polynomial regressor; smooth Chebyshev model tree). Release as scikit-learn-compatible packages. Benchmark against tree ensembles, a pre-trained transformer, and standard baselines across 55 regression datasets organized by domain. Assess accuracy and generalisation properties.
AI.result: Transformer ranks first in accuracy on a majority of datasets; however, its GPU dependence and inference latency hinder CPU-based deployment. Among CPU-viable models, smooth-basis methods and tree ensembles are statistically tied on accuracy, with smooth models typically exhibiting tighter generalisation gaps.
AI.conclusion: Routinely include smooth-basis models in the candidate pool for tabular regression, especially when deployment constraints favor CPU-only inference and when tighter generalisation and smoother response surfaces are advantageous.

title: DeepPresenter: Environment-Grounded Reflection for Agentic Presentation Generation
summary: Presentation generation requires deep content research, coherent visual design, and iterative refinement based on observation. However, existing presentation agents often rely on predefined workflows and fixed templates. To address this, we present DeepPresenter, an agentic framework that adapts to diverse user intents, enables effective feedback-driven refinement, and generalizes beyond a scripted pipeline. Specifically, DeepPresenter autonomously plans, renders, and revises intermediate slide artifacts to support long-horizon refinement with environmental observations. Furthermore, rather than relying on self-reflection over internal signals (e.g., reasoning traces), our environment-grounded reflection conditions the generation process on perceptual artifact states (e.g., rendered slides), enabling the system to identify and correct presentation-specific issues during execution. Results on the evaluation set covering diverse presentation-generation scenarios show that DeepPresenter achieves state-of-the-art performance, and the fine-tuned 9B model remains highly competitive at substantially lower cost. Our project is available at: https://github.com/icip-cas/PPTAgent

AI.tldr: DeepPresenter is an agentic framework for presentation generation that uses environment-grounded, long-horizon planning to autonomously plan, render, and revise slides, enabling feedback-driven refinement beyond scripted templates; it achieves state-of-the-art results with a 9B model at lower cost.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of fixed-template, scripted presentation agents by enabling flexible, long-horizon refinement and environmental feedback through perceptual artifact states rather than internal reasoning traces.
AI.method: An autonomous agentic loop where DeepPresenter plans, renders, and revises intermediate slide artifacts. It uses environment-grounded reflection conditioned on perceptual artifact states (e.g., rendered slides) to identify and correct presentation-specific issues during execution, allowing generalization beyond fixed pipelines.
AI.result: Evaluations across diverse presentation-generation scenarios show state-of-the-art performance; a fine-tuned 9B model remains highly competitive at substantially lower cost.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective generalization beyond scripted pipelines with feedback-driven refinement via environment-grounded reflection, and offers a cost-efficient, adaptable approach for complex presentation generation; code/software is available online.

title: UFO-DETR: Frequency-Guided End-to-End Detector for UAV Tiny Objects
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures, published to 2026 International Conference on Computer Supported Cooperative Work in Design
summary: Small target detection in UAV imagery faces significant challenges such as scale variations, dense distribution, and the dominance of small targets. Existing algorithms rely on manually designed components, and general-purpose detectors are not optimized for UAV images, making it difficult to balance accuracy and complexity. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an end-to-end object detection framework, UFO-DETR, which integrates an LSKNet-based backbone network to optimize the receptive field and reduce the number of parameters. By combining the DAttention and AIFI modules, the model flexibly models multi-scale spatial relationships, improving multi-scale target detection performance. Additionally, the DynFreq-C3 module is proposed to enhance small target detection capability through cross-space frequency feature enhancement. Experimental results show that, compared to RT-DETR-L, the proposed method offers significant advantages in both detection performance and computational efficiency, providing an efficient solution for UAV edge computing.

AI.tldr: An end-to-end UAV target detector, UFO-DETR, improves small target detection and efficiency by using an LSKNet-based backbone and frequency-aware modules.
AI.motivation: Small target detection in UAV imagery suffers from scale variation, dense distributions, and computational constraints; general-purpose detectors are not optimized for UAV data.
AI.method: UFO-DETR uses an LSKNet-based backbone to optimize receptive field and reduce parameters; incorporates DAttention and AIFI to model multi-scale spatial relationships; introduces DynFreq-C3 for cross-space frequency feature enhancement; designed for efficient UAV edge computing.
AI.result: Outperforms RT-DETR-L in detection performance and computational efficiency, indicating suitability for UAV edge devices.
AI.conclusion: Provides a compact, multi-scale capable detector tailored for UAV imagery, achieving better accuracy-efficiency balance for edge deployment.

title: UniScale: Unified Scale-Aware 3D Reconstruction for Multi-View Understanding via Prior Injection for Robotic Perception
summary: We present UniScale, a unified, scale-aware multi-view 3D reconstruction framework for robotic applications that flexibly integrates geometric priors through a modular, semantically informed design. In vision-based robotic navigation, the accurate extraction of environmental structure from raw image sequences is critical for downstream tasks. UniScale addresses this challenge with a single feed-forward network that jointly estimates camera intrinsics and extrinsics, scale-invariant depth and point maps, and the metric scale of a scene from multi-view images, while optionally incorporating auxiliary geometric priors when available. By combining global contextual reasoning with camera-aware feature representations, UniScale is able to recover the metric-scale of the scene. In robotic settings where camera intrinsics are known, they can be easily incorporated to improve performance, with additional gains obtained when camera poses are also available. This co-design enables robust, metric-aware 3D reconstruction within a single unified model. Importantly, UniScale does not require training from scratch, and leverages world priors exhibited in pre-existing models without geometric encoding strategies, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained robotic teams. We evaluate UniScale on multiple benchmarks, demonstrating strong generalization and consistent performance across diverse environments. We will release our implementation upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: UniScale is a single feed-forward network that jointly estimates camera intrinsics/extrinsics, scale-invariant depth/point maps, and the metric scene scale from multi-view images, optionally using geometric priors, to achieve robust, metric-aware 3D reconstruction in robotics without training from scratch.
AI.motivation: Robotic navigation requires accurate, scale-consistent 3D reconstruction from multi-view imagery, even with varying camera intrinsics and poses; leveraging priors and a unified model can improve robustness, generalization, and efficiency for resource-constrained robots.
AI.method: A unified, scale-aware multi-view framework (UniScale) using a single feed-forward network that jointly predicts camera intrinsics, extrinsics, scale-invariant depth/points, and scene metric scale from multi-view inputs; incorporates global context with camera-aware features and optional auxiliary geometric priors; can utilize known intrinsics/poses to boost performance without re-training.
AI.result: Evaluations on multiple benchmarks show strong generalization and consistent metric-scale reconstruction across diverse environments; claims robustness and good performance, with plans to release implementation.
AI.conclusion: UniScale enables robust, metric-aware 3D reconstruction within a single model by co-designing intrinsic/extrinsic estimation, scale handling, and priors, making it suitable for resource-constrained robotic teams.

title: Calibrated Test-Time Guidance for Bayesian Inference
summary: Test-time guidance is a widely used mechanism for steering pretrained diffusion models toward outcomes specified by a reward function. Existing approaches, however, focus on maximizing reward rather than sampling from the true Bayesian posterior, leading to miscalibrated inference. In this work, we show that common test-time guidance methods do not recover the correct posterior distribution and identify the structural approximations responsible for this failure. We then propose consistent alternative estimators that enable calibrated sampling from the Bayesian posterior. We significantly outperform previous methods on a set of Bayesian inference tasks, and match state-of-the-art in black hole image reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Identifies miscalibration in test-time guidance for diffusion models and offers consistent estimators for calibrated Bayesian posterior sampling, improving Bayesian inference tasks and black hole image reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Test-time guidance optimizes reward but does not ensure sampling from the true Bayesian posterior, causing miscalibrated inferences.
AI.method: Analyze the structural approximations that cause miscalibration, derive consistent estimators, and enable calibrated sampling from the Bayesian posterior; comprehensive empirical evaluation.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over prior guidance methods on Bayesian inference tasks; matches state-of-the-art in black hole image reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Proposes consistent estimators that restore Bayesian-calibrated sampling in test-time guided diffusion, with broad applicability and improved reliability.

title: The AI Research Assistant: Promise, Peril, and a Proof of Concept
summary:
Can artificial intelligence truly contribute to creative mathematical research, or does it merely automate routine calculations while introducing risks of error? We provide empirical evidence through a detailed case study: the discovery of novel error representations and bounds for Hermite quadrature rules via systematic human-AI collaboration.
  Working with multiple AI assistants, we extended results beyond what manual work achieved, formulating and proving several theorems with AI assistance. The collaboration revealed both remarkable capabilities and critical limitations. AI excelled at algebraic manipulation, systematic proof exploration, literature synthesis, and LaTeX preparation. However, every step required rigorous human verification, mathematical intuition for problem formulation, and strategic direction.
  We document the complete research workflow with unusual transparency, revealing patterns in successful human-AI mathematical collaboration and identifying failure modes researchers must anticipate. Our experience suggests that, when used with appropriate skepticism and verification protocols, AI tools can meaningfully accelerate mathematical discovery while demanding careful human oversight and deep domain expertise.

AI.tldr: A systematic human–AI collaboration in mathematics can yield new error representations and Hermite quadrature bounds, showing AI's strengths and the necessity of rigorous human verification.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether AI can meaningfully contribute to creative mathematical research and to characterize its capabilities and limitations in a rigorous setting.
AI.method: Case study with multiple AI assistants engaging in algebraic manipulation, systematic proof exploration, literature synthesis, and LaTeX preparation. The complete research workflow is documented to identify successful collaboration patterns and failure modes, with AI-assisted formulation and proof contributing to extended results.
AI.result: Discovery of novel error representations and bounds for Hermite quadrature rules; AI collaboration extended results beyond manual effort, enabling formulation and proof of several theorems. AI demonstrated strengths in algebraic manipulation, proof exploration, literature synthesis, and LaTeX, but required rigorous human verification, mathematical intuition for problem formulation, and strategic direction.
AI.conclusion: AI tools can meaningfully accelerate mathematical discovery when used with skepticism and verification protocols, but require careful human oversight and deep domain expertise.

title: SoPE: Spherical Coordinate-Based Positional Embedding for Enhancing Spatial Perception of 3D LVLMs
summary: 3D Large Vision-Language Models (3D LVLMs) built upon Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across various multimodal tasks. However, their inherited position-dependent modeling mechanism, Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), remains suboptimal for 3D multimodal understanding. The vanilla RoPE formulation fails to preserve essential three-dimensional spatial structures when encoding 3D tokens, and its relative distance computation overlooks angular dependencies, hindering the model's ability to capture directional variations in visual representations. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Spherical Coordinate-based Positional Embedding (SoPE). Our method maps point-cloud token indices into a 3D spherical coordinate space, enabling unified modeling of spatial locations and directional angles. This formulation preserves the inherent geometric structure of point-cloud data, enhances spatial awareness, and yields more consistent and expressive geometric representations for multimodal learning. In addition, we introduce a multi-scale frequency mixing strategy to fuse feature information across different frequency domains. Experimental results on multiple 3D scene benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our approach, while real-world deployment experiments further demonstrate its strong generalization capability.

AI.tldr: SoPE uses spherical-coordinate positional embeddings for 3D LVLMs to preserve spatial and angular information, addressing RoPE limitations, with a multi-scale frequency mixing strategy. It improves performance on 3D scene benchmarks and generalizes well in real-world deployments.
AI.motivation: RoPE’s standard formulation is not well suited for 3D geometry: it loses spatial structure and ignores angular dependencies when encoding 3D tokens, hindering 3D multimodal understanding. A geometry-preserving positional encoding is needed for 3D LVLMs.
AI.method: Map point-cloud token indices into a 3D spherical coordinate space and design a spherical-coordinate positional embedding (SoPE) that jointly represents location and direction. Introduce a multi-scale frequency mixing strategy to fuse information across different frequency domains.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple 3D scene benchmarks show improved performance. Real-world deployment experiments demonstrate strong generalization capability.
AI.conclusion: SoPE preserves geometric structure in 3D data for LVLMs, improving spatial and directional representation; the multi-scale frequency mixing further enhances feature fusion, with demonstrated generalization to real-world tasks.

title: Risk-Aware World Model Predictive Control for Generalizable End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: With advances in imitation learning (IL) and large-scale driving datasets, end-to-end autonomous driving (E2E-AD) has made great progress recently. Currently, IL-based methods have become a mainstream paradigm: models rely on standard driving behaviors given by experts, and learn to minimize the discrepancy between their actions and expert actions. However, this objective of "only driving like the expert" suffers from limited generalization: when encountering rare or unseen long-tail scenarios outside the distribution of expert demonstrations, models tend to produce unsafe decisions in the absence of prior experience. This raises a fundamental question: Can an E2E-AD system make reliable decisions without any expert action supervision? Motivated by this, we propose a unified framework named Risk-aware World Model Predictive Control (RaWMPC) to address this generalization dilemma through robust control, without reliance on expert demonstrations. Practically, RaWMPC leverages a world model to predict the consequences of multiple candidate actions and selects low-risk actions through explicit risk evaluation. To endow the world model with the ability to predict the outcomes of risky driving behaviors, we design a risk-aware interaction strategy that systematically exposes the world model to hazardous behaviors, making catastrophic outcomes predictable and thus avoidable. Furthermore, to generate low-risk candidate actions at test time, we introduce a self-evaluation distillation method to distill riskavoidance capabilities from the well-trained world model into a generative action proposal network without any expert demonstration. Extensive experiments show that RaWMPC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios, while providing superior decision interpretability.

AI.tldr: Proposes Risk-aware World Model Predictive Control (RaWMPC) for end-to-end autonomous driving that operates without expert supervision, using a world model and risk evaluation to choose safe actions and a distillation method to transfer risk-avoidance to a generative action network; shows superior performance and interpretability in ID and OOD settings.
AI.motivation: Address generalization gaps in imitation-learning-based E2E-AD by removing dependence on expert demonstrations and enabling explicit risk management for rare/unseen scenarios.
AI.method: Train a world model to forecast outcomes of multiple candidate actions; implement a risk-aware interaction strategy to expose the world model to hazardous behaviors so catastrophic outcomes become learnable and avoidable; perform explicit risk evaluation to select low-risk actions; develop a self-evaluation distillation process to transfer risk-avoidance capabilities from the world model into a generative action proposal network without using expert data.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate RaWMPC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios and offers superior decision interpretability.
AI.conclusion: A risk-aware world-model planning framework with self-evaluation distillation can achieve reliable, interpretable E2E autonomous driving without expert supervision, addressing long-tail generalization challenges.

title: From Bias to Balance: Fairness-Aware Paper Recommendation for Equitable Peer Review
summary: Despite frequent double-blind review, systemic biases related to author demographics still disadvantage underrepresented groups. We start from a simple hypothesis: if a post-review recommender is trained with an explicit fairness regularizer, it should increase inclusion without degrading quality. To test this, we introduce Fair-PaperRec, a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with a differentiable fairness loss over intersectional attributes (e.g., race, country) that re-ranks papers after double-blind review. We first probe the hypothesis on synthetic datasets spanning high, moderate, and near-fair biases. Across multiple randomized runs, these controlled studies map where increasing the fairness weight strengthens macro/micro diversity while keeping utility approximately stable, demonstrating robustness and adaptability under varying disparity levels. We then carry the hypothesis into the original setting, conference data from ACM Special Interest Group on Computer-Human Interaction (SIGCHI), Designing Interactive Systems (DIS), and Intelligent User Interfaces (IUI). In this real-world scenario, an appropriately tuned configuration of Fair-PaperRec achieves up to a 42.03% increase in underrepresented-group participation with at most a 3.16% change in overall utility relative to the historical selection. Taken together, the synthetic-to-original progression shows that fairness regularization can act as both an equity mechanism and a mild quality regularizer, especially in highly biased regimes. By first analyzing the behavior of the fairness parameters under controlled conditions and then validating them on real submissions, Fair-PaperRec offers a practical, equity-focused framework for post-review paper selection that preserves, and in some settings can even enhance, measured scholarly quality.

AI.tldr: Fair-PaperRec: an MLP re-ranker with a differentiable fairness loss over intersectional attributes that improves underrepresented-group inclusion with minimal utility loss, validated on synthetic and real conference data.
AI.motivation: Address persistent biases in double-blind peer review that disadvantage underrepresented groups; test whether a fairness regularizer can increase inclusivity without sacrificing quality; explore robustness across disparity regimes and real-world applicability.
AI.method: Train an MLP post-review re-ranker with a differentiable fairness loss over intersectional attributes (e.g., race, country). Validate on synthetic datasets with varying bias levels and on real conference data (SIGCHI, DIS, IUI). Evaluate diversity (macro/micro) and utility across different fairness weights.
AI.result: Increasing fairness weight boosts representation of underrepresented groups and diversity metrics while keeping overall utility near-stable in synthetic experiments; on real data, up to 42.03% higher participation of underrepresented groups with only ~3.16% utility change; the approach serves as an equity mechanism and mild regularizer.
AI.conclusion: Fair-PaperRec provides a practical, equity-focused post-review re-ranking framework that can preserve or enhance scholarly quality, with demonstrated robustness from synthetic to real-world settings and particular strength in highly biased regimes.

title: Towards LLM-Empowered Knowledge Tracing via LLM-Student Hierarchical Behavior Alignment in Hyperbolic Space
summary: Knowledge Tracing (KT) diagnoses students' concept mastery through continuous learning state monitoring in education.Existing methods primarily focus on studying behavioral sequences based on ID or textual information.While existing methods rely on ID-based sequences or shallow textual features, they often fail to capture (1) the hierarchical evolution of cognitive states and (2) individualized problem difficulty perception due to limited semantic modeling. Therefore, this paper proposes a Large Language Model Hyperbolic Aligned Knowledge Tracing(L-HAKT). First, the teacher agent deeply parses question semantics and explicitly constructs hierarchical dependencies of knowledge points; the student agent simulates learning behaviors to generate synthetic data. Then, contrastive learning is performed between synthetic and real data in hyperbolic space to reduce distribution differences in key features such as question difficulty and forgetting patterns. Finally, by optimizing hyperbolic curvature, we explicitly model the tree-like hierarchical structure of knowledge points, precisely characterizing differences in learning curve morphology for knowledge points at different levels. Extensive experiments on four real-world educational datasets validate the effectiveness of our Large Language Model Hyperbolic Aligned Knowledge Tracing (L-HAKT) framework.

AI.tldr: Proposes L-HAKT, a hyperbolic-aligned knowledge tracing framework that uses LLM-driven semantic parsing to build hierarchical knowledge structures, synthetic data from a student agent, and hyperbolic contrastive learning to align real and synthetic data and model learning curves across knowledge levels.
AI.motivation: Existing KT models rely on ID sequences or shallow text and struggle to capture hierarchical evolution of cognitive states and individualized problem difficulty due to limited semantic modeling.
AI.method: 1) A teacher agent semantically parses questions to construct hierarchical dependencies among knowledge points. 2) A student agent generates synthetic learning data. 3) Contrastive learning is performed between synthetic and real data in hyperbolic space to reduce distribution differences in features such as question difficulty and forgetting patterns. 4) Hyperbolic curvature optimization explicitly models the tree-like knowledge hierarchy to characterize differing learning curves across knowledge levels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four real-world educational datasets demonstrates the effectiveness of L-HAKT, indicating improved modeling of hierarchical cognitive states and learning curves and superior or competitive predictive performance relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: L-HAKT integrates LLM-based semantic parsing, synthetic data generation, hyperbolic space contrastive learning, and curvature optimization to produce a hierarchical, personalized KT framework that is validated across multiple datasets.

title: IRSDE-Despeckle: A Physics-Grounded Diffusion Model for Generalizable Ultrasound Despeckling
comment: 12 pages main text + 6 pages appendix, 7 figures main + 3 figures appendix, 3 tables main + 1 table appendix. Preprint
summary: Ultrasound imaging is widely used for real-time, noninvasive diagnosis, but speckle and related artifacts reduce image quality and can hinder interpretation. We present a diffusion-based ultrasound despeckling method built on the Image Restoration Stochastic Differential Equations framework. To enable supervised training, we curate large paired datasets by simulating ultrasound images from speckle-free magnetic resonance images using the Matlab UltraSound Toolbox. The proposed model reconstructs speckle-suppressed images while preserving anatomically meaningful edges and contrast. On a held-out simulated test set, our approach consistently outperforms classical filters and recent learning-based despeckling baselines. We quantify prediction uncertainty via cross-model variance and show that higher uncertainty correlates with higher reconstruction error, providing a practical indicator of difficult or failure-prone regions. Finally, we evaluate sensitivity to simulation probe settings and observe domain shift, motivating diversified training and adaptation for robust clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based ultrasound despeckling using ISDE with supervised training on simulated paired data; preserves edges/contrast and provides uncertainty estimates; domain shift observed with probe settings.
AI.motivation: Speckle artifacts degrade ultrasound quality; need reliable despeckling that preserves anatomy while enabling uncertainty-aware assessment and robust deployment.
AI.method: Diffusion-based image restoration via ISDE framework. Supervised training uses paired speckled images generated from speckle-free MR images by the Matlab Ultrasound Toolbox. The model yields despeckled images; uncertainty is quantified through cross-model variance. Assessments include simulated held-out data and sensitivity to probe settings to gauge domain shift.
AI.result: Outperforms classical filters and recent learning-based baselines on held-out simulated tests. Cross-model variance correlates with reconstruction error, enabling practical identification of difficult regions. Domain shift observed with varying probe settings, highlighting need for diverse training and adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows promise for clinical deployment but robustness requires diversified data and adaptation to probe/configuration changes; uncertainty provides a useful metric for failure-prone regions.

title: Physics Informed Viscous Value Representations
summary: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) learns goal-conditioned policies from static pre-collected datasets. However, accurate value estimation remains a challenge due to the limited coverage of the state-action space. Recent physics-informed approaches have sought to address this by imposing physical and geometric constraints on the value function through regularization defined over first-order partial differential equations (PDEs), such as the Eikonal equation. However, these formulations can often be ill-posed in complex, high-dimensional environments. In this work, we propose a physics-informed regularization derived from the viscosity solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation. By providing a physics-based inductive bias, our approach grounds the learning process in optimal control theory, explicitly regularizing and bounding updates during value iterations. Furthermore, we leverage the Feynman-Kac theorem to recast the PDE solution as an expectation, enabling a tractable Monte Carlo estimation of the objective that avoids numerical instability in higher-order gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our method improves geometric consistency, making it broadly applicable to navigation and high-dimensional, complex manipulation tasks. Open-source codes are available at https://github.com/HrishikeshVish/phys-fk-value-GCRL.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed HJB-based regularization improves value estimation in offline GCRL by leveraging viscosity solutions and the Feynman-Kac representation to provide stable, geometry-consistent updates in high-dimensional tasks; demonstrated on navigation and manipulation with open-source code.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of value estimation due to limited state-action coverage in offline GCRL and the instability/ill-posedness of previous PDE-based regularizations (e.g., Eikonal) in complex environments.
AI.method: Introduce a regularization term derived from the viscosity solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation. Use the Feynman-Kac theorem to recast the PDE solution as an expectation, enabling Monte Carlo estimation to regularize and bound value-iteration updates without unstable higher-order gradients.
AI.result: Experimental results show improved geometric consistency and applicability to navigation and high-dimensional manipulation tasks; code released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: A physics-informed regularization grounded in optimal control theory provides a stable, scalable inductive bias for offline GCRL value learning, addressing ill-posedness and enhancing performance in complex environments.

title: ECHO: Encoding Communities via High-order Operators
summary: Community detection in attributed networks faces a fundamental divide: topological algorithms ignore semantic features, while Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) encounter devastating computational bottlenecks. Specifically, GNNs suffer from a Semantic Wall of feature over smoothing in dense or heterophilic networks, and a Systems Wall driven by the O(N^2) memory constraints of pairwise clustering. To dismantle these barriers, we introduce ECHO (Encoding Communities via High order Operators), a scalable, self supervised architecture that reframes community detection as an adaptive, multi scale diffusion process. ECHO features a Topology Aware Router that automatically analyzes structural heuristics sparsity, density, and assortativity to route graphs through the optimal inductive bias, preventing heterophilic poisoning while ensuring semantic densification. Coupled with a memory sharded full batch contrastive objective and a novel chunked O(N \cdot K) similarity extraction method, ECHO completely bypasses traditional O(N^2) memory bottlenecks without sacrificing the mathematical precision of global gradients. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that this topology feature synergy consistently overcomes the classical resolution limit. On synthetic LFR benchmarks scaled up to 1 million nodes, ECHO achieves scale invariant accuracy despite severe topological noise. Furthermore, on massive real world social networks with over 1.6 million nodes and 30 million edges, it completes clustering in mere minutes with throughputs exceeding 2,800 nodes per second matching the speed of highly optimized purely topological baselines. The implementation utilizes a unified framework that automatically engages memory sharded optimization to support adoption across varying hardware constraints. GitHub Repository: https://github.com/emilioferrara/ECHO-GNN

AI.tldr: ECHO is a scalable, self-supervised architecture for community detection in attributed networks that combines a topology-aware routing mechanism with memory-efficient contrastive learning to bypass O(N^2) memory costs, achieving high accuracy at large scales.
AI.motivation: To overcome two major barriers in community detection: (1) topological methods ignore semantic features, and (2) GNNs suffer from semantic oversmoothing and prohibitive memory costs in dense/heterophilic graphs.
AI.method: Introduce ECHO with (a) a Topology Aware Router that chooses inductive biases based on sparsity, density, and assortativity, (b) a memory-sharded full-batch contrastive objective, and (c) a chunked O(N*K) similarity extraction technique; implemented in a unified framework with memory-sharded optimization.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved performance over classical methods, achieving scale-invariant accuracy on LFR benchmarks up to 1M nodes; on real-world networks with 1.6M nodes and 30M edges, clustering completes in minutes with throughput >2,800 nodes/s, matching fast topological baselines.
AI.conclusion: ECHO enables topology-semantic synergy for scalable community detection, effectively bypassing O(N^2) memory bottlenecks while maintaining precise global gradients; the approach scales to very large graphs and is practical for real-world deployment.

title: OmniGAIA: Towards Native Omni-Modal AI Agents
summary: Human intelligence naturally intertwines omni-modal perception -- spanning vision, audio, and language -- with complex reasoning and tool usage to interact with the world. However, current multi-modal LLMs are primarily confined to bi-modal interactions (e.g., vision-language), lacking the unified cognitive capabilities required for general AI assistants. To bridge this gap, we introduce OmniGAIA, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate omni-modal agents on tasks necessitating deep reasoning and multi-turn tool execution across video, audio, and image modalities. Constructed via a novel omni-modal event graph approach, OmniGAIA synthesizes complex, multi-hop queries derived from real-world data that require cross-modal reasoning and external tool integration. Furthermore, we propose OmniAtlas, a native omni-modal foundation agent under tool-integrated reasoning paradigm with active omni-modal perception. Trained on trajectories synthesized via a hindsight-guided tree exploration strategy and OmniDPO for fine-grained error correction, OmniAtlas effectively enhances the tool-use capabilities of existing open-source models. This work marks a step towards next-generation native omni-modal AI assistants for real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes OmniGAIA benchmark and OmniAtlas agent for native omni-modal reasoning and tool use across video, audio, and image modalities; plus training methods to enable tool integration.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal LLMs are typically bimodal and struggle with unified cross-modal reasoning and external tool usage; there is a need for a benchmark and foundation agent that operate across multiple modalities.
AI.method: OmniGAIA uses an omni-modal event-graph to synthesize complex, multi-hop queries from real-world data that require cross-modal reasoning and external tool integration. OmniAtlas is a native omni-modal foundation agent trained with hindsight-guided tree exploration and OmniDPO for fine-grained error correction, enabling active omni-modal perception and tool use.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates enhanced tool-use capabilities and cross-modal reasoning in open-source models, marking progress toward native omni-modal AI assistants.
AI.conclusion: This work advances the development of native omni-modal AI assistants capable of real-world interaction through integrated perception and tool-driven reasoning.

title: HulluEdit: Single-Pass Evidence-Consistent Subspace Editing for Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Object hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) significantly hinders their reliable deployment. Existing methods struggle to balance efficiency and accuracy: they often require expensive reference models and multiple forward passes, or apply static edits that risk suppressing genuine visual evidence. To address this, we introduce HulluEdit, a single-pass, reference-free intervention framework. Our core innovation is orthogonal subspace editing: we decompose the hidden states of the model into orthogonal subspaces - visual evidence, conflicting priors, and residual uncertainty - enabling selective suppression of hallucinatory patterns without interfering with visual grounding. This approach mathematically guarantees that edits applied to the prior subspace leave the visual component entirely unaffected. Extensive experiments show that HulluEdit achieves state-of-the-art hallucination reduction on benchmarks including POPE and CHAIR across diverse architectures, while preserving general capabilities on MME and maintaining efficient inference. Our method consistently outperforms contrastive decoding and static subspace editing baselines, offering a new pathway toward more trustworthy LVLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces HulluEdit, a single-pass, reference-free method to reduce object hallucinations in LVLMs by orthogonally editing hidden-state subspaces, preserving visual grounding while suppressing erroneous priors.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination in LVLMs is prevalent and harmful; existing fixes are costly (reference models, multi-pass) or risk erasing genuine visual evidence. A lightweight, single-pass, reference-free intervention is needed.
AI.method: Orthogonal subspace editing: decompose hidden states into three orthogonal subspaces—visual evidence, conflicting priors, and residual uncertainty—and selectively suppress hallucinatory patterns by editing only the prior subspace, guaranteeing no impact on the visual component.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art hallucination reduction on POPE and CHAIR across diverse architectures; preserves general capabilities on MME; maintains efficient inference; outperforms contrastive decoding and static subspace editing baselines.
AI.conclusion: HulluEdit provides a new, efficient pathway to trustworthy LVLMs by eliminating hallucinations without compromising grounding, applicable across architectures and outperforming existing baselines.

title: Beyond performance-wise Contribution Evaluation in Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning offers a privacy-friendly collaborative learning framework, yet its success, like any joint venture, hinges on the contributions of its participants. Existing client evaluation methods predominantly focus on model performance, such as accuracy or loss, which represents only one dimension of a machine learning model's overall utility. In contrast, this work investigates the critical, yet overlooked, issue of client contributions towards a model's trustworthiness -- specifically, its reliability (tolerance to noisy data), resilience (resistance to adversarial examples), and fairness (measured via demographic parity). To quantify these multifaceted contributions, we employ the state-of-the-art approximation of the Shapley value, a principled method for value attribution. Our results reveal that no single client excels across all dimensions, which are largely independent from each other, highlighting a critical flaw in current evaluation scheme: no single metric is adequate for comprehensive evaluation and equitable rewarding allocation.

AI.tldr: Multidimensional attribution of client contributions in federated learning using Shapley value approximations across reliability, resilience, and fairness; reveals trade-offs and independence among dimensions; single-metric evaluation is insufficient for fair rewards.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap that existing FL evaluation focuses on accuracy/loss while contributions to trustworthiness are crucial; need principled attribution framework for multi-dimensional value.
AI.method: Applies state-of-the-art Shapley value approximation to quantify each client's contributions along three dimensions (reliability, resilience, fairness) and analyzes correlations/independence and implications for rewarding.
AI.result: No client dominates across all dimensions; the three dimensions are largely independent; current single-metric evaluation schemes are flawed for comprehensive evaluation and fair reward allocation.
AI.conclusion: Advocates multi-criteria evaluation and Shapley-based attribution as a principled approach; future work should integrate multiple dimensions into reward/selection policies in FL.

title: General Agent Evaluation
summary: The promise of general-purpose agents - systems that perform tasks in unfamiliar environments without domain-specific engineering - remains largely unrealized. Existing agents are predominantly specialized, and while emerging implementations like OpenAI SDK Agent and Claude Code hint at broader capabilities, no systematic evaluation of their general performance has been pursued. Current agentic benchmarks assume domain-specific integration, encoding task information in ways that preclude fair evaluation of general agents. This paper frames general-agent evaluation as a first-class research objective. We propose conceptual principles for such evaluation, a Unified Protocol enabling agent-benchmark integration, and Exgentic - a practical framework for general agent evaluation. We benchmark five prominent agent implementations across six environments as the first Open General Agent Leaderboard. Our experiments show that general agents generalize across diverse environments, achieving performance comparable to domain-specific agents without any environment-specific tuning. We release our evaluation protocol, framework, and leaderboard to establish a foundation for systematic research on general-purpose agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces the Open General Agent Leaderboard and Exgentic framework to evaluate truly general-purpose agents across diverse environments, showing that general agents can match domain-specific performance without environment-specific tuning.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks rely on domain-specific encodings that bias evaluation; there is no fair, systematic assessment of truly general agents across environments.
AI.method: Proposes conceptual principles for general-agent evaluation, a Unified Protocol for agent-benchmark integration, and Exgentic as a practical evaluation framework; benchmarks five prominent agent implementations across six environments to form the Open General Agent Leaderboard.
AI.result: Evidence that general agents generalize across diverse environments and achieve performance comparable to domain-specific agents without environment-specific tuning.
AI.conclusion: Releases evaluation protocol, framework, and leaderboard to enable systematic research and fair assessment of general-purpose agents.

title: Asymmetric Idiosyncrasies in Multimodal Models
summary: In this work, we study idiosyncrasies in the caption models and their downstream impact on text-to-image models. We design a systematic analysis: given either a generated caption or the corresponding image, we train neural networks to predict the originating caption model. Our results show that text classification yields very high accuracy (99.70\%), indicating that captioning models embed distinctive stylistic signatures. In contrast, these signatures largely disappear in the generated images, with classification accuracy dropping to at most 50\% even for the state-of-the-art Flux model. To better understand this cross-modal discrepancy, we further analyze the data and find that the generated images fail to preserve key variations present in captions, such as differences in the level of detail, emphasis on color and texture, and the distribution of objects within a scene. Overall, our classification-based framework provides a novel methodology for quantifying both the stylistic idiosyncrasies of caption models and the prompt-following ability of text-to-image systems.

AI.tldr: Captions exhibit near-perfect stylistic signatures that persist in captions but largely disappear in generated images; introduces a framework to quantify caption-model idiosyncrasies and prompt-following in text-to-image systems.
AI.motivation: Investigate cross-modal transfer of stylistic cues from caption models to T2I outputs and quantify how well images reflect caption prompts.
AI.method: Train neural classifiers to predict the source caption model from either a caption or its corresponding image; compare accuracy across modalities; analyze data for preserved variations such as detail level, color/texture emphasis, and object arrangement; evaluate across models including Flux.
AI.result: Caption-model source classification on captions reaches ~99.7% accuracy; image-based classification drops to ≤50%, even for Flux; generated images fail to preserve caption-level variations; reveals a cross-modal discrepancy.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel framework to quantify caption-model idiosyncrasies and prompt-following ability of T2I systems; highlights a gap between caption stylistics and image generation fidelity; provides a diagnostic tool for evaluating T2I systems.

title: Efficient Continual Learning in Language Models via Thalamically Routed Cortical Columns
summary: Continual learning is a core requirement for deployed language models, yet standard training and fine-tuning pipelines remain brittle under non-stationary data. Online updates often induce catastrophic forgetting, while methods that improve stability frequently increase latency, memory footprint, or dense computation in ways that do not scale well to long contexts. We introduce TRC$^{2}$ (Thalamically Routed Cortical Columns), a decoder-only backbone that addresses continual learning at the architectural level. TRC$^{2}$ combines sparse thalamic routing over cortical columns with mechanisms for modulation, prediction, memory, and feedback, together with a fast corrective pathway that supports rapid adaptation without destabilizing slower parameters. The resulting block is sparse and chunk-parallel, enabling efficient training and inference while preserving clean ablations of each subsystem. We instantiate a reproducible training and evaluation stack and a continual-learning harness that measures proxy forgetting under streaming domain shifts. Across language modeling and continual learning benchmarks, TRC$^{2}$ improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff at comparable compute, enabling rapid on-stream adaptation while preserving previously acquired behavior.

AI.tldr: A decoder-only backbone with sparse thalamic routing (TRC^2) for continual learning, achieving a better stability-plasticity tradeoff with efficient, scalable compute and rapid on-stream adaptation under streaming domain shifts.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and brittle training in non-stationary data for deployed language models; existing methods trade off stability, latency, and memory and do not scale to long contexts.
AI.method: Introduce TRC^2: sparse thalamic routing over cortical columns, plus modulation, prediction, memory, feedback, and a fast corrective pathway. The block is sparse and chunk-parallel, enabling efficient training/inference. Provide a reproducible training/evaluation stack and a continual-learning harness to measure proxy forgetting under streaming shifts.
AI.result: Across language modeling and continual learning benchmarks, TRC^2 improves the stability-plasticity tradeoff at comparable compute, enabling rapid on-stream adaptation while preserving previously acquired behavior.
AI.conclusion: An architecture-level solution for continual learning in decoder-only models; routing-based sparsity and fast corrective pathways yield improved stability with efficient compute, suitable for streaming, non-stationary data.

title: FactGuard: Agentic Video Misinformation Detection via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have substantially advanced video misinformation detection through unified multimodal reasoning, but they often rely on fixed-depth inference and place excessive trust in internally generated assumptions, particularly in scenarios where critical evidence is sparse, fragmented, or requires external verification. To address these limitations, we propose FactGuard, an agentic framework for video misinformation detection that formulates verification as an iterative reasoning process built upon MLLMs. FactGuard explicitly assesses task ambiguity and selectively invokes external tools to acquire critical evidence, enabling progressive refinement of reasoning trajectories. To further strengthen this capability, we introduce a two-stage training strategy that combines domain-specific agentic supervised fine-tuning with decision-aware reinforcement learning to optimize tool usage and calibrate risk-sensitive decision making. Extensive experiments on FakeSV, FakeTT, and FakeVV demonstrate FactGuard's state-of-the-art performance and validate its excellent robustness and generalization capacity.

AI.tldr: FactGuard introduces an agentic framework for video misinformation detection that uses iterative verification and selective external tool use, combined with domain-specific agentic supervised fine-tuning and decision-aware reinforcement learning, achieving state-of-the-art results on FakeSV, FakeTT, and FakeVV with strong robustness and generalization.
AI.motivation: To overcome fixed-depth inference and overreliance on internally generated assumptions in multimodal LLMs for video misinformation detection, especially when evidence is sparse or requires external verification.
AI.method: An iterative, agentic reasoning process that assesses task ambiguity and selectively invokes external tools for evidence gathering; a two-stage training procedure consisting of domain-specific agentic supervised fine-tuning and decision-aware reinforcement learning to optimize tool usage and risk-sensitive decisions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on FakeSV, FakeTT, and FakeVV, with demonstrated robustness and generalization across datasets.
AI.conclusion: FactGuard enhances video misinformation detection by enabling iterative, verifiable reasoning with external tools and calibrated decision-making, yielding superior performance and robustness.

title: AMLRIS: Alignment-aware Masked Learning for Referring Image Segmentation
summary: Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment an object in an image identified by a natural language expression. The paper introduces Alignment-Aware Masked Learning (AML), a training strategy to enhance RIS by explicitly estimating pixel-level vision-language alignment, filtering out poorly aligned regions during optimization, and focusing on trustworthy cues. This approach results in state-of-the-art performance on RefCOCO datasets and also enhances robustness to diverse descriptions and scenarios

AI.tldr: Alignment-Aware Masked Learning (AML) is a training strategy for Referring Image Segmentation that explicitly estimates pixel-level vision-language alignment, filters poorly aligned regions during training, and emphasizes trustworthy cues, achieving state-of-the-art results on RefCOCO and improved robustness to diverse descriptions.
AI.motivation: RIS models often rely on coarse, potentially misaligned region-language pairs; explicit pixel-level alignment and masking can improve generalization and robustness across diverse expressions.
AI.method: AML introduces a training regime that (1) estimates pixel-level vision-language alignment during learning, (2) masks or filters regions with low alignment confidence to exclude them from optimization, and (3) concentrates learning on cues that are trustworthy for segmentation, effectively focusing the model on reliable alignments.
AI.result: On RefCOCO datasets, AML achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with prior RIS methods and shows enhanced robustness to descriptive variation and scenario diversity.
AI.conclusion: Explicit alignment-aware masking during training can significantly improve RIS accuracy and robustness, suggesting a practical paradigm for alignment-aware vision-language learning in RIS and related tasks.

title: Reinforcement-aware Knowledge Distillation for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training has recently driven major gains in long chain-of-thought reasoning large language models (LLMs), but the high inference cost of such models motivates distillation into smaller students. Most existing knowledge distillation (KD) methods are designed for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), relying on fixed teacher traces or teacher-student Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence-based regularization. When combined with RL, these approaches often suffer from distribution mismatch and objective interference: teacher supervision may not align with the student's evolving rollout distribution, and the KL regularizer can compete with reward maximization and require careful loss balancing. To address these issues, we propose RL-aware distillation (RLAD), which performs selective imitation during RL -- guiding the student toward the teacher only when it improves the current policy update. Our core component, Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD), replaces the teacher-student KL regularizer with a PPO/GRPO-style likelihood-ratio objective anchored to a teacher--old-policy mixture, yielding advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts and naturally balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation. Across diverse logic reasoning and math benchmarks, RLAD consistently outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student knowledge distillation.

AI.tldr: RLAD introduces selective imitation in RL-based distillation for LLMs, using TRRD—a PPO-like objective anchored to a teacher-old-policy mixture—to balance exploration, exploitation, and imitation, addressing distribution mismatch and objective interference inherent in KL-based KD during RL.
AI.motivation: Distilling RL-based teachers into smaller students is challenged by distribution mismatch (teacher vs. student rollout) and objective interference (KL regularization conflicting with reward maximization). The work seeks to enable effective knowledge transfer during RL by aligning imitation with policy improvement and maintaining stable optimization.
AI.method: RL-aware distillation (RLAD) with Trust Region Ratio Distillation (TRRD). The student undergoes selective imitation, guided by a likelihood-ratio objective derived from a teacher-old-policy mixture, resembling PPO/GRPO style trust region constraints. This yields advantage-aware, trust-region-bounded distillation on student rollouts, balancing exploration, exploitation, and imitation.
AI.result: Empirically, RLAD outperforms offline distillation, standard GRPO, and KL-based on-policy teacher-student distillation across various logic reasoning and math benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Selective, advantage-aware imitation via a PPO-like ratio objective anchored to a teacher-old-policy mix provides stable, effective RL-based distillation, improving policy updates and performance over traditional KD approaches in RL settings.

title: Certified Circuits: Stability Guarantees for Mechanistic Circuits
summary: Understanding how neural networks arrive at their predictions is essential for debugging, auditing, and deployment. Mechanistic interpretability pursues this goal by identifying circuits - minimal subnetworks responsible for specific behaviors. However, existing circuit discovery methods are brittle: circuits depend strongly on the chosen concept dataset and often fail to transfer out-of-distribution, raising doubts whether they capture concept or dataset-specific artifacts. We introduce Certified Circuits, which provide provable stability guarantees for circuit discovery. Our framework wraps any black-box discovery algorithm with randomized data subsampling to certify that circuit component inclusion decisions are invariant to bounded edit-distance perturbations of the concept dataset. Unstable neurons are abstained from, yielding circuits that are more compact and more accurate. On ImageNet and OOD datasets, certified circuits achieve up to 91% higher accuracy while using 45% fewer neurons, and remain reliable where baselines degrade. Certified Circuits puts circuit discovery on formal ground by producing mechanistic explanations that are provably stable and better aligned with the target concept. Code will be released soon!

AI.tldr: Certified Circuits provides provable stability guarantees for mechanistic circuit discovery by adding randomized data subsampling to a black-box discovery process, certifying that circuit component choices are invariant under bounded dataset perturbations and abstaining from unstable neurons to yield compact, accurate circuits. Empirically, they report large gains on ImageNet and out-of-distribution data with fewer neurons.
AI.motivation: Existing circuit discovery methods are brittle and highly dataset-specific, raising questions about whether discovered circuits reflect genuine concepts or artifacts; robust, transferable, and interpretable explanations are needed.
AI.method: Wrap any black-box circuit discovery algorithm with randomized data subsampling to certify that component inclusion decisions are invariant to bounded edit-distance perturbations of the concept dataset; abstain from unstable neurons to produce more compact circuits; provide formal stability guarantees.
