AI.result: On ImageNet and OOD datasets, certified circuits achieve up to 91% higher accuracy while using 45% fewer neurons and remain reliable where baselines degrade.
AI.conclusion: Certified Circuits grounds mechanistic circuit discovery in formal guarantees, yielding explanations that are provably stable and better aligned with the target concept; code will be released soon.

title: ProjFlow: Projection Sampling with Flow Matching for Zero-Shot Exact Spatial Motion Control
summary: Generating human motion with precise spatial control is a challenging problem. Existing approaches often require task-specific training or slow optimization, and enforcing hard constraints frequently disrupts motion naturalness. Building on the observation that many animation tasks can be formulated as a linear inverse problem, we introduce ProjFlow, a training-free sampler that achieves zero-shot, exact satisfaction of linear spatial constraints while preserving motion realism. Our key advance is a novel kinematics-aware metric that encodes skeletal topology. This metric allows the sampler to enforce hard constraints by distributing corrections coherently across the entire skeleton, avoiding the unnatural artifacts of naive projection. Furthermore, for sparse inputs, such as filling in long gaps between a few keyframes, we introduce a time-varying formulation using pseudo-observations that fade during sampling. Extensive experiments on representative applications, motion inpainting, and 2D-to-3D lifting, demonstrate that ProjFlow achieves exact constraint satisfaction and matches or improves realism over zero-shot baselines, while remaining competitive with training-based controllers.

AI.tldr: A training-free, zero-shot sampler, ProjFlow, enforces exact linear spatial constraints in motion generation while preserving realism, using a kinematics-aware skeletal metric; it supports sparse inputs via a time-varying pseudo-observation formulation and outperforms zero-shot baselines while remaining competitive with training-based controllers.
AI.motivation: Many animation tasks reduce to linear inverse problems, but existing methods either require task-specific training or slow optimization, and hard constraint enforcement degrades naturalness; there is a need for a training-free, globally coherent constraint-enforcing approach that respects skeletal topology.
AI.method: ProjFlow treats constraint satisfaction as a linear inverse problem and uses a kinematics-aware metric that encodes skeletal topology to distribute corrections coherently across the skeleton. For sparse inputs, it introduces a time-varying formulation with pseudo-observations that fade over sampling to interpolate gaps. It is training-free and provides zero-shot exact constraint satisfaction.
AI.result: Experimental results on motion inpainting and 2D-to-3D lifting show exact constraint satisfaction and competitive realism, outperforming zero-shot baselines and matching or exceeding performance of some training-based controllers.
AI.conclusion: ProjFlow demonstrates that exact linear constraint satisfaction can be achieved without training while preserving motion realism; the kinematics-aware metric is key to coherent corrections, and the approach scales to sparse inputs via pseudo-observation fading.

title: Sharp Convergence Rates for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Discrete diffusion models have achieved strong empirical performance in text and other symbolic domains, with masked (absorbing-rate) variants emerging as competitive alternatives to autoregressive models. Among existing samplers, the Euler method remains the standard choice in many applications, and more recently, the First-Hitting Sampler (FHS) has shown considerable promise for masked diffusion models. Despite their practical success, the theoretical understanding of these samplers remains limited. Existing analyses are conducted in Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, which often yields loose parameter dependencies and requires strong assumptions on score estimation. Moreover, these guarantees do not cover recently developed high-performance sampler of FHS. In this work, we first develop a direct total-variation (TV) based analysis for the Euler method that overcomes these limitations. Our results relax assumptions on score estimation, improve parameter dependencies, and establish convergence guarantees without requiring any surrogate initialization. Also for this setting, we provide the first convergence lower bound for the Euler sampler, establishing tightness with respect to both the data dimension $d$ and the target accuracy $\varepsilon$. Finally, we analyze the FHS sampler and show that it incurs no sampling error beyond that induced by score estimation, which we show to be tight with a matching lower error bound. Overall, our analysis introduces a direct TV-based error decomposition along the CTMC trajectory and a decoupling-based path-wise analysis for FHS, which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: TV-based convergence analysis for discrete diffusion samplers (Euler) with tight bounds and a decoupled pathwise analysis for First-Hitting Sampler (FHS); shows improved guarantees under weaker score-estimation assumptions and establishes tight lower bounds, while FHS errors are solely due to score estimation with matching lower bounds.
AI.motivation: There is limited theoretical understanding of discrete diffusion samplers beyond KL-based analyses, which yield loose dependencies and require strong score-estimation assumptions; need direct TV-based guarantees and coverage of modern samplers like FHS.
AI.method: Develop a direct total-variation (TV) based error decomposition along the continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) trajectory for Euler; introduce a decoupling-based, path-wise analysis for FHS; derive convergence guarantees in TV, and establish lower bounds; analyze score-estimation error as a key source of error.
AI.result: Euler: provides TV-based convergence guarantees with relaxed score-estimation assumptions, no surrogate initialization, improved parameter scaling; first convergence lower bound for Euler showing tightness in dimension d and accuracy ε. FHS: error is entirely due to score estimation, with a matching lower bound; overall, two new analytic frameworks (TV decomposition and decoupled path-wise analysis) and tightness results.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TV-based framework yields stronger, assumption-light guarantees for Euler and clarifies that FHS performance is governed by score estimation error, with proven tightness; the analysis techniques may be of independent interest for other CTMC-based samplers.

title: SPM-Bench: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Scanning Probe Microscopy
summary: As LLMs achieved breakthroughs in general reasoning, their proficiency in specialized scientific domains reveals pronounced gaps in existing benchmarks due to data contamination, insufficient complexity, and prohibitive human labor costs. Here we present SPM-Bench, an original, PhD-level multimodal benchmark specifically designed for scanning probe microscopy (SPM). We propose a fully automated data synthesis pipeline that ensures both high authority and low-cost. By employing Anchor-Gated Sieve (AGS) technology, we efficiently extract high-value image-text pairs from arXiv and journal papers published between 2023 and 2025. Through a hybrid cloud-local architecture where VLMs return only spatial coordinates "llbox" for local high-fidelity cropping, our pipeline achieves extreme token savings while maintaining high dataset purity. To accurately and objectively evaluate the performance of the LLMs, we introduce the Strict Imperfection Penalty F1 (SIP-F1) score. This metric not only establishes a rigorous capability hierarchy but also, for the first time, quantifies model "personalities" (Conservative, Aggressive, Gambler, or Wise). By correlating these results with model-reported confidence and perceived difficulty, we expose the true reasoning boundaries of current AI in complex physical scenarios. These insights establish SPM-Bench as a generalizable paradigm for automated scientific data synthesis.

AI.tldr: A novel, automated, domain-specific benchmark for scanning probe microscopy (SPM) with a PhD-level multimodal dataset, using Anchor-Gated Sieve to extract high-quality image–text pairs, a hybrid cloud-local pipeline for precise cropping via llbox coordinates, and a SIP-F1 metric plus a model-personality taxonomy to evaluate LLM reasoning in complex physical tasks.
AI.motivation: Address major gaps in existing LLM benchmarks (data contamination, insufficient domain difficulty, and high human labor costs) by creating a high-quality, domain-specific benchmark that enables automated data synthesis and robust evaluation in SPM.
AI.method: Use Anchor-Gated Sieve (AGS) to extract high-value image–text pairs from arXiv and journals published 2023–2025. Implement a hybrid cloud–local architecture where VLMs return spatial coordinates (llbox) for local high-fidelity cropping. Build the SPM-Bench data pipeline with automated synthesis, and evaluate LLMs with the Strict Imperfection Penalty F1 (SIP-F1) score. Correlate model results with reported confidence and perceived difficulty to infer model personalities (Conservative, Aggressive, Gambler, Wise).
AI.result: Demonstrates a high-purity, low-cost data pipeline with extreme token savings and a generalizable automated synthesis paradigm. Establishes a hierarchical ranking of LLM capabilities in SPM tasks and introduces a quantifiable model-personality axis linked to confidence and difficulty estimates.
AI.conclusion: SPM-Bench offers a generalizable framework for automated, domain-specific scientific data synthesis and evaluation, potentially extensible to other specialized domains. It highlights substantial gains in data quality and efficiency while introducing novel evaluation metrics and a personality taxonomy, though interpretability and replication in other domains require further validation.

title: SPATIALALIGN: Aligning Dynamic Spatial Relationships in Video Generation
summary: Most text-to-video (T2V) generators prioritize aesthetic quality, but often ignoring the spatial constraints in the generated videos. In this work, we present SPATIALALIGN, a self-improvement framework that enhances T2V models capabilities to depict Dynamic Spatial Relationships (DSR) specified in text prompts. We present a zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to fine-tune T2V models towards better alignment with DSR. Specifically, we design DSR-SCORE, a geometry-based metric that quantitatively measures the alignment between generated videos and the specified DSRs in prompts, which is a step forward from prior works that rely on VLM for evaluation. We also conduct a dataset of text-video pairs with diverse DSRs to facilitate the study. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our fine-tuned model significantly out performs the baseline in spatial relationships. The code will be released in Link.

AI.tldr: SPATIALALIGN improves text-to-video models to accurately depict dynamic spatial relationships using zeroth-order regularized DPO, with a geometry-based DSR-SCORE metric and a new text-video dataset; shows improved spatial alignment and plans for code release.
AI.motivation: To address the common neglect of spatial constraints in T2V generation and to enable objective, geometry-based evaluation of dynamic spatial relationships specified in prompts.
AI.method: Train T2V models with zeroth-order regularized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) guided by feedback on spatial relationships; introduce DSR-SCORE, a geometry-based metric to quantify alignment between generated videos and prompt-specified dynamic spatial relationships; create a dataset of text-video pairs with diverse DSRs for study.
AI.result: Fine-tuned models significantly outperform baselines in rendering accurate spatial relationships; DSR-SCORE provides a more appropriate evaluation beyond VLM-based metrics; a dataset is provided to facilitate future work.
AI.conclusion: SPATIALALIGN demonstrates effective alignment of T2V outputs with dynamic spatial prompts, introduces a practical evaluation metric (DSR-SCORE) and a supporting dataset, and promises code release for reproducibility.

title: Space Syntax-guided Post-training for Residential Floor Plan Generation
summary:
Pre-trained generative models for residential floor plans are typically optimized to fit large-scale data distributions, which can under-emphasize critical architectural priors such as the configurational dominance and connectivity of domestic public spaces (e.g., living rooms and foyers). This paper proposes Space Syntax-guided Post-training (SSPT), a post-training paradigm that explicitly injects space syntax knowledge into floor plan generation via a non-differentiable oracle. The oracle converts RPLAN-style layouts into rectangle-space graphs through greedy maximal-rectangle decomposition and door-mediated adjacency construction, and then computes integration-based measurements to quantify public space dominance and functional hierarchy.
  To enable consistent evaluation and diagnosis, we further introduce SSPT-Bench (Eval-8), an out-of-distribution benchmark that post-trains models using conditions capped at $\leq 7$ rooms while evaluating on 8-room programs, together with a unified metric suite for dominance, stability, and profile alignment. SSPT is instantiated with two strategies: (i) iterative retraining via space-syntax filtering and diffusion fine-tuning, and (ii) reinforcement learning via PPO with space-syntax rewards. Experiments show that both strategies improve public-space dominance and restore clearer functional hierarchy compared to distribution-fitted baselines, while PPO achieves stronger gains with substantially higher compute efficiency and reduced variance. SSPT provides a scalable pathway for integrating architectural theory into data-driven plan generation and is compatible with other generative backbones given a post-hoc evaluation oracle.

AI.tldr: Space Syntax-guided Post-training (SSPT) injects architectural priors into floor-plan generation via a non-differentiable oracle that converts layouts to rectangle-space graphs and computes integration-based measures; it introduces Eval-8 (SSPT-Bench) for out-of-distribution evaluation and two post-training strategies (iterative space-syntax filtering with diffusion fine-tuning, and PPO-based reinforcement learning with space-syntax rewards). Both improve public-space dominance and functional hierarchy over distribution-fitted baselines, with PPO offering stronger gains and higher efficiency; SSPT is compatible with other generative backbones via the post-hoc evaluation oracle.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained generative models for floor plans trained on large-scale distributions often under-emphasize architectural priors such as public-space dominance and functional connectivity; there is a need to inject space-syntax knowledge and to diagnose/out-of-distribution performance.
AI.method: Introduce a non-differentiable oracle that converts RPLAN-style layouts to rectangle-space graphs using greedy maximal-rectangle decomposition and door-mediated adjacency, then computes integration-based metrics for space dominance and hierarchy. Propose SSPT-Bench (Eval-8) with training on ≤7-room programs and evaluation on 8-room programs, plus a unified metric suite for dominance, stability, and profile alignment. Implement two SSPT strategies: (i) iterative retraining via space-syntax filtering and diffusion fine-tuning; (ii) PPO-based reinforcement learning with space-syntax rewards.
AI.result: Both strategies improve public-space dominance and restore clearer functional hierarchy compared to baselines that fit the data distribution; PPO yields stronger gains with substantially higher compute efficiency and reduced variance.
AI.conclusion: SSPT demonstrates a scalable way to infuse architectural theory into data-driven floor-plan generation, and is compatible with other generative backbones through a post-hoc evaluation oracle; the Eval-8 benchmark provides a framework for assessing out-of-distribution behavior regarding space syntax priors.

title: Modeling Expert AI Diagnostic Alignment via Immutable Inference Snapshots
summary: Human-in-the-loop validation is essential in safety-critical clinical AI, yet the transition between initial model inference and expert correction is rarely analyzed as a structured signal. We introduce a diagnostic alignment framework in which the AI-generated image based report is preserved as an immutable inference state and systematically compared with the physician-validated outcome. The inference pipeline integrates a vision-enabled large language model, BERT- based medical entity extraction, and a Sequential Language Model Inference (SLMI) step to enforce domain-consistent refinement prior to expert review. Evaluation on 21 dermatological cases (21 complete AI physician pairs) em- ployed a four-level concordance framework comprising exact primary match rate (PMR), semantic similarity-adjusted rate (AMR), cross-category alignment, and Comprehensive Concordance Rate (CCR). Exact agreement reached 71.4% and remained unchanged under semantic similarity (t = 0.60), while structured cross-category and differential overlap analysis yielded 100% comprehensive concordance (95% CI: [83.9%, 100%]). No cases demonstrated complete diagnostic divergence. These findings show that binary lexical evaluation substantially un- derestimates clinically meaningful alignment. Modeling expert validation as a structured transformation enables signal-aware quantification of correction dynamics and supports traceable, human aligned evaluation of image based clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces a diagnostic alignment framework for AI-generated image-based clinical reports, preserving AI inference as an immutable state and comparing it with physician-validated outcomes. Uses vision-enabled LLM, BERT-based medical NER, and Sequential Language Model Inference (SLMI) to refine before expert review; evaluates 21 dermatology cases with four-level concordance (PMR, AMR, cross-category alignment, CCR). Finds exact agreement at 71.4% (unchanged under semantic similarity), 100% comprehensive concordance via cross-category/differential overlap, no complete diagnostic divergence. Argues binary lexical evaluation underestimates meaningful alignment and supports signal-aware, traceable evaluation of image-based CDS.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of structured signal in the transition from model inference to expert correction in safety-critical clinical AI, enabling traceable evaluation of image-based decision-support systems.
AI.method: Preserve AI-generated inference as immutable state; compare with physician-validated outcome; integrate a vision-enabled LLM, BERT-based medical entity extraction, and a Sequential Language Model Inference (SLMI) step to enforce domain-consistent refinement prior to expert review; evaluate with a four-level concordance framework (PMR, AMR, cross-category alignment, CCR).
AI.result: Exact agreement 71.4%; semantic similarity does not change exact agreement (t = 0.60); cross-category alignment and differential overlap yield 100% CCR (95% CI: [83.9%, 100%]); no cases with complete diagnostic divergence.
AI.conclusion: Modeling expert validation as a structured transformation enables signal-aware quantification of correction dynamics and supports traceable, human-aligned evaluation of image-based clinical decision-support systems.

title: Beyond Detection: Multi-Scale Hidden-Code for Natural Image Deepfake Recovery and Factual Retrieval
summary: Recent advances in image authenticity have primarily focused on deepfake detection and localization, leaving recovery of tampered contents for factual retrieval relatively underexplored. We propose a unified hidden-code recovery framework that enables both retrieval and restoration from post-hoc and in-generation watermarking paradigms. Our method encodes semantic and perceptual information into a compact hidden-code representation, refined through multi-scale vector quantization, and enhances contextual reasoning via conditional Transformer modules. To enable systematic evaluation for natural images, we construct ImageNet-S, a benchmark that provides paired image-label factual retrieval tasks. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S demonstrate that our method exhibits promising retrieval and reconstruction performance while remaining fully compatible with diverse watermarking pipelines. This framework establishes a foundation for general-purpose image recovery beyond detection and localization.

AI.tldr: A unified hidden-code recovery framework enabling retrieval and restoration from watermarking for images; introduces ImageNet-S for factual image–label retrieval; employs multi-scale vector quantization and conditional Transformers to encode semantic/perceptual info into a compact hidden-code; demonstrates effective retrieval and reconstruction across watermarking pipelines.
AI.motivation: Recovery of tampered content for factual retrieval is underexplored and requires a framework that unifies post-hoc and in-generation watermarking to support retrieval and restoration beyond detection and localization.
AI.method: Encode semantic and perceptual information into a compact hidden-code representation, refined through multi-scale vector quantization; enhance contextual reasoning with conditional Transformer modules; designed to be compatible with diverse watermarking pipelines; evaluate on the ImageNet-S benchmark for paired image-label retrieval.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ImageNet-S show promising retrieval and reconstruction performance, validating the framework's effectiveness and compatibility with various watermarking pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Provides a foundation for general-purpose image recovery beyond detection and localization, enabling both retrieval and restoration in watermarking-enabled scenarios.

title: TEFL: Prediction-Residual-Guided Rolling Forecasting for Multi-Horizon Time Series
summary: Time series forecasting plays a critical role in domains such as transportation, energy, and meteorology. Despite their success, modern deep forecasting models are typically trained to minimize point-wise prediction loss without leveraging the rich information contained in past prediction residuals from rolling forecasts - residuals that reflect persistent biases, unmodeled patterns, or evolving dynamics. We propose TEFL (Temporal Error Feedback Learning), a unified learning framework that explicitly incorporates these historical residuals into the forecasting pipeline during both training and evaluation. To make this practical in deep multi-step settings, we address three key challenges: (1) selecting observable multi-step residuals under the partial observability of rolling forecasts, (2) integrating them through a lightweight low-rank adapter to preserve efficiency and prevent overfitting, and (3) designing a two-stage training procedure that jointly optimizes the base forecaster and error module. Extensive experiments across 10 real-world datasets and 5 backbone architectures show that TEFL consistently improves accuracy, reducing MAE by 5-10% on average. Moreover, it demonstrates strong robustness under abrupt changes and distribution shifts, with error reductions exceeding 10% (up to 19.5%) in challenging scenarios. By embedding residual-based feedback directly into the learning process, TEFL offers a simple, general, and effective enhancement to modern deep forecasting systems.

AI.tldr: TEFL introduces residual-based feedback into deep time series forecasting via a lightweight, two-stage learned error module integrated with a low-rank adapter, yielding consistent MAE gains (5-10% on average) and improved robustness across 10 real datasets and 5 backbones.
AI.motivation: Past residuals from rolling forecasts encode persistent biases and evolving dynamics that are not leveraged by standard point-wise loss training, offering a promising source of information to improve forecasting.
AI.method: 1) Select observable multi-step residuals under partial observability of rolling forecasts. 2) Integrate residuals through a lightweight low-rank adapter to preserve efficiency and mitigate overfitting. 3) Train in two stages to jointly optimize the base forecaster and the error module.
AI.result: TEFL consistently improves accuracy across 10 real-world datasets and 5 backbone architectures, reducing MAE by 5-10% on average; robustness under abrupt changes and distribution shifts with error reductions >10% (up to 19.5%) in challenging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Embedding residual-based feedback into the learning process provides a simple, general, and effective enhancement to modern deep forecasting systems.

title: RepSPD: Enhancing SPD Manifold Representation in EEGs via Dynamic Graphs
summary: Decoding brain activity from electroencephalography (EEG) is crucial for neuroscience and clinical applications. Among recent advances in deep learning for EEG, geometric learning stands out as its theoretical underpinnings on symmetric positive definite (SPD) allows revealing structural connectivity analysis in a physics-grounded manner. However, current SPD-based methods focus predominantly on statistical aggregation of EEGs, with frequency-specific synchronization and local topological structures of brain regions neglected. Given this, we propose RepSPD, a novel geometric deep learning (GDL)-based model. RepSPD implements a cross-attention mechanism on the Riemannian manifold to modulate the geometric attributes of SPD with graph-derived functional connectivity features. On top of this, we introduce a global bidirectional alignment strategy to reshape tangent-space embeddings, mitigating geometric distortions caused by curvature and thereby enhancing geometric consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed framework significantly outperforms existing EEG representation methods, exhibiting superior robustness and generalization capabilities.

AI.tldr: RepSPD leverages cross-attention on SPD manifolds to fuse EEG-derived functional connectivity with geometric deep representations, plus a tangent-space global alignment to reduce curvature-induced distortions, yielding improved robustness and generalization over existing SPD-based EEG methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of current SPD-based EEG methods that primarily rely on statistical aggregation and ignore frequency-specific synchronization and local topological structures, aiming for a physics-grounded, geometry-aware representation for EEG analysis.
AI.method: RepSPD uses a cross-attention mechanism operating on the Riemannian manifold of symmetric positive definite matrices to modulate SPD-geometric features with graph-derived functional connectivity. It adds a global bidirectional alignment strategy that reshapes tangent-space embeddings to mitigate curvature-induced distortions and improve geometric consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that RepSPD significantly outperforms existing EEG representation methods, with enhanced robustness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed cross-attention on SPD manifolds combined with tangent-space alignment provides a robust, physics-grounded framework for EEG representation, promising improved performance and generalization in neuroscience and clinical EEG applications.

title: TrajTok: Learning Trajectory Tokens enables better Video Understanding
summary: Tokenization in video models, typically through patchification, generates an excessive and redundant number of tokens. This severely limits video efficiency and scalability. While recent trajectory-based tokenizers offer a promising solution by decoupling video duration from token count, they rely on complex external segmentation and tracking pipelines that are slow and task-agnostic. We propose TrajTok, an end-to-end video tokenizer module that is fully integrated and co-trained with video models for a downstream objective, dynamically adapting its token granularity to semantic complexity, independent of video duration. TrajTok contains a unified segmenter that performs implicit clustering over pixels in both space and time to directly produce object trajectories in a single forward pass. By prioritizing downstream adaptability over pixel-perfect segmentation fidelity, TrajTok is lightweight and efficient, yet empirically improves video understanding performance. With TrajTok, we implement a video CLIP model trained from scratch (TrajViT2). It achieves the best accuracy at scale across both classification and retrieval benchmarks, while maintaining efficiency comparable to the best token-merging methods. TrajTok also proves to be a versatile component beyond its role as a tokenizer. We show that it can be seamlessly integrated as either a probing head for pretrained visual features (TrajAdapter) or an alignment connector in vision-language models (TrajVLM) with especially strong performance in long-video reasoning.

AI.tldr: TrajTok is an end-to-end, dynamically granular video tokenizer integrated with video models, reducing token overhead while boosting accuracy; enabling TrajViT2, TrajAdapter, and TrajVLM for scalable video understanding and long-video reasoning.
AI.motivation: Video tokenization via patchification creates excessive tokens that hamper efficiency; existing trajectory tokenizers rely on slow, external segmentation/tracking pipelines and are task-agnostic. There is a need for an end-to-end, co-trained tokenizer whose token granularity adapts to semantic complexity and is decoupled from video duration.
AI.method: TrajTok uses a unified segmenter that implicitly clusters pixels in space-time to yield object trajectories in a single forward pass, producing tokens whose granularity adapts to semantic content. It is co-trained with the downstream objective and is lightweight to maintain efficiency, operating without requiring pixel-perfect segmentation.
AI.result: Empirically improves video understanding. TrajViT2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy at scale on classification and retrieval benchmarks while maintaining efficiency comparable to the best token-merging methods. TrajTok is versatile and can function as a probing head (TrajAdapter) or an alignment connector (TrajVLM), with strong performance in long-video reasoning.
AI.conclusion: TrajTok is a flexible, efficient end-to-end video tokenizer that adapts token granularity to semantic complexity, integrates with diverse architectures, and enhances scalability and performance for video understanding and long-video reasoning.

title: Predicting Tennis Serve directions with Machine Learning
summary: Serves, especially first serves, are very important in professional tennis. Servers choose their serve directions strategically to maximize their winning chances while trying to be unpredictable. On the other hand, returners try to predict serve directions to make good returns. The mind game between servers and returners is an important part of decision-making in professional tennis matches. To help understand the players' serve decisions, we have developed a machine learning method for predicting professional tennis players' first serve directions. Through feature engineering, our method achieves an average prediction accuracy of around 49\% for male players and 44\% for female players. Our analysis provides some evidence that top professional players use a mixed-strategy model in serving decisions and that fatigue might be a factor in choosing serve directions. Our analysis also suggests that contextual information is perhaps more important for returners' anticipatory reactions than previously thought.

AI.tldr: An ML approach predicts pro players' first-serve directions with modest accuracy (≈49% male, 44% female), suggesting mixed strategies and fatigue effects in serving decisions.
AI.motivation: To understand decision-making in professional tennis serve directions by quantifying predictability and exploring factors like fatigue and contextual cues.
AI.method: Machine learning with feature engineering to predict first-serve directions based on match/player features; accuracy metrics reported by gender.
AI.result: Prediction accuracy around 49% for male players and 44% for female players; evidence of mixed-strategy serving among top players; fatigue may influence serve direction; contextual information appears important for returners.
AI.conclusion: Top players likely employ mixed strategies in serving; fatigue and context influence serve choices; results highlight the importance of context in anticipating returns and the player decision process.

title: Obscure but Effective: Classical Chinese Jailbreak Prompt Optimization via Bio-Inspired Search
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used, their security risks have drawn increasing attention. Existing research reveals that LLMs are highly susceptible to jailbreak attacks, with effectiveness varying across language contexts. This paper investigates the role of classical Chinese in jailbreak attacks. Owing to its conciseness and obscurity, classical Chinese can partially bypass existing safety constraints, exposing notable vulnerabilities in LLMs. Based on this observation, this paper proposes a framework, CC-BOS, for the automatic generation of classical Chinese adversarial prompts based on multi-dimensional fruit fly optimization, facilitating efficient and automated jailbreak attacks in black-box settings. Prompts are encoded into eight policy dimensions-covering role, behavior, mechanism, metaphor, expression, knowledge, trigger pattern and context; and iteratively refined via smell search, visual search, and cauchy mutation. This design enables efficient exploration of the search space, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of black-box jailbreak attacks. To enhance readability and evaluation accuracy, we further design a classical Chinese to English translation module. Extensive experiments demonstrate that effectiveness of the proposed CC-BOS, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art jailbreak attack methods.

AI.tldr: A framework (CC-BOS) uses multi-dimensional fruit fly optimization to automatically generate classical Chinese prompts for LLM jailbreaks in black-box settings, with eight policy dimensions and a translation module; experiments show superior effectiveness over existing jailbreak methods.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed and vulnerable to jailbreaks. Classical Chinese’s concise/obscure nature appears to bypass safety constraints, prompting the need for automated analysis of such vulnerabilities and robust defenses.
AI.method: CC-BOS encodes prompts into eight policy dimensions (role, behavior, mechanism, metaphor, expression, knowledge, trigger pattern, context). It uses iterative refinement via smell search, visual search, and Cauchy mutation (a family of mutation operators) in a fruit fly optimization framework; includes a classical Chinese–English translation module for readability/evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that CC-BOS effectively generates adversarial prompts and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art jailbreak attack methods in black-box settings.
AI.conclusion: CC-BOS provides a scalable, automated approach to generating classical Chinese adversarial prompts, revealing security gaps in LLM safety constraints and informing future defense and evaluation research.

title: SceneTransporter: Optimal Transport-Guided Compositional Latent Diffusion for Single-Image Structured 3D Scene Generation
summary: We introduce SceneTransporter, an end-to-end framework for structured 3D scene generation from a single image. While existing methods generate part-level 3D objects, they often fail to organize these parts into distinct instances in open-world scenes. Through a debiased clustering probe, we reveal a critical insight: this failure stems from the lack of structural constraints within the model's internal assignment mechanism. Based on this finding, we reframe the task of structured 3D scene generation as a global correlation assignment problem. To solve this, SceneTransporter formulates and solves an entropic Optimal Transport (OT) objective within the denoising loop of the compositional DiT model. This formulation imposes two powerful structural constraints. First, the resulting transport plan gates cross-attention to enforce an exclusive, one-to-one routing of image patches to part-level 3D latents, preventing entanglement. Second, the competitive nature of the transport encourages the grouping of similar patches, a process that is further regularized by an edge-based cost, to form coherent objects and prevent fragmentation. Extensive experiments show that SceneTransporter outperforms existing methods on open-world scene generation, significantly improving instance-level coherence and geometric fidelity. Code and models will be publicly available at https://2019epwl.github.io/SceneTransporter/.

AI.tldr: Proposes SceneTransporter, an end-to-end 3D scene generator that uses entropic optimal transport to enforce one-to-one patch-to-part routing and patch clustering, yielding coherent open-world scenes from a single image.
AI.motivation: Existing part-level 3D generation lacks global structural constraints, causing fragmentation of instances in open-world scenes.
AI.method: Diagnoses lack of structure via a debiased clustering probe; reframes to a global correlation assignment problem; optimizes an entropy-regularized OT objective within the denoising loop of a compositional DiT model. OT gates cross-attention to enforce exclusive routing and uses a competitive transport with an edge-aware cost to cluster similar patches into coherent objects.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on open-world scene generation, with significant gains in instance-level coherence and geometric fidelity; supported by extensive experiments; code and models will be released.
AI.conclusion: Shows that an OT-based global correlation with attention gating provides robust, instance-aware 3D scene generation and reduces fragmentation in open-world settings.

title: Coarse-to-Fine Learning of Dynamic Causal Structures
summary: Learning the dynamic causal structure of time series is a challenging problem. Most existing approaches rely on distributional or structural invariance to uncover underlying causal dynamics, assuming stationary or partially stationary causality. However, these assumptions often conflict with the complex, time-varying causal relationships observed in real-world systems. This motivates the need for methods that address fully dynamic causality, where both instantaneous and lagged dependencies evolve over time. Such a setting poses significant challenges for the efficiency and stability of causal discovery. To address these challenges, we introduce DyCausal, a dynamic causal structure learning framework. DyCausal leverages convolutional networks to capture causal patterns within coarse-grained time windows, and then applies linear interpolation to refine causal structures at each time step, thereby recovering fine-grained and time-varying causal graphs. In addition, we propose an acyclic constraint based on matrix norm scaling, which improves efficiency while effectively constraining loops in evolving causal structures. Comprehensive evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that DyCausal achieves superior performance compared to existing methods, offering a stable and efficient approach for identifying fully dynamic causal structures from coarse to fine.

AI.tldr: DyCausal is a dynamic causal structure learning framework that identifies time-varying causal graphs by first modeling coarse temporal windows with convolutional networks, then refining the graph at each time step via linear interpolation, and imposing an acyclic constraint via matrix norm scaling to improve efficiency. It demonstrates superior and stable performance on synthetic and real data for fully dynamic causality.
AI.motivation: Stationary or partially stationary causal discovery methods fail to capture real-world, time-varying causal relations where both instantaneous and lagged dependencies evolve over time, necessitating fully dynamic causal structure learning.
AI.method: DyCausal uses CNNs to capture causal patterns within coarse time windows, applies linear interpolation to recover fine-grained causality at each time step, and enforces an acyclic constraint based on matrix norm scaling to maintain acyclicity while keeping computational efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets show that DyCausal achieves superior performance relative to existing methods, with stable and efficient discovery of fully dynamic causal structures from coarse to fine resolutions.
AI.conclusion: DyCausal provides a stable, efficient approach for learning fully dynamic causal graphs, overcoming limitations of stationary assumptions by combining coarse-to-fine modeling and a scalable acyclicity constraint.

title: Learning-based Multi-agent Race Strategies in Formula 1
summary: In Formula 1, race strategies are adapted according to evolving race conditions and competitors' actions. This paper proposes a reinforcement learning approach for multi-agent race strategy optimization. Agents learn to balance energy management, tire degradation, aerodynamic interaction, and pit-stop decisions. Building on a pre-trained single-agent policy, we introduce an interaction module that accounts for the behavior of competitors. The combination of the interaction module and a self-play training scheme generates competitive policies, and agents are ranked based on their relative performance. Results show that the agents adapt pit timing, tire selection, and energy allocation in response to opponents, achieving robust and consistent race performance. Because the framework relies only on information available during real races, it can support race strategists' decisions before and during races.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (RL) for Formula 1 race strategy optimization, extending a pre-trained single-agent policy with an interaction module to model competitors, trained via self-play and ranked by relative performance; agents adapt pit timing, tire choice, and energy allocation in response to rivals, yielding robust, race-aware decision support.
AI.motivation: Tackle dynamic, adversarial race environments where decisions depend on rival behavior and evolving conditions; aim to learn robust, transferable strategies that work under energy, tire wear, aero interactions, and pit constraints; leverage self-play to expose policies to diverse opponents.
AI.method: Augment a pre-trained single-agent RL policy with an interaction module that captures competitor behavior; train through self-play to generate competitive policies; rank agents by relative performance; base framework on information available during real races for practical applicability.
AI.result: In simulations, agents adjust pit timing, tire selection, and energy distribution in response to opponents and obtain robust, consistent race performance; the approach yields opponent-aware strategies suitable for decision support in real races.
AI.conclusion: Opponent-aware multi-agent RL can produce competitive, robust race strategies and serve as a practical tool for strategists, leveraging only real-race observables for in-race decision support.

title: Robust Human Trajectory Prediction via Self-Supervised Skeleton Representation Learning
summary: Human trajectory prediction plays a crucial role in applications such as autonomous navigation and video surveillance. While recent works have explored the integration of human skeleton sequences to complement trajectory information, skeleton data in real-world environments often suffer from missing joints caused by occlusions. These disturbances significantly degrade prediction accuracy, indicating the need for more robust skeleton representations. We propose a robust trajectory prediction method that incorporates a self-supervised skeleton representation model pretrained with masked autoencoding. Experimental results in occlusion-prone scenarios show that our method improves robustness to missing skeletal data without sacrificing prediction accuracy, and consistently outperforms baseline models in clean-to-moderate missingness regimes.

AI.tldr: Robust trajectory prediction using a self-supervised skeleton representation via masked autoencoding improves robustness to missing joints without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Occlusions cause missing joints in skeleton data, which degrades trajectory prediction; robust skeleton representations are needed to maintain performance in real-world settings.
AI.method: Pretrain a skeleton representation model with masked autoencoding in a self-supervised manner and integrate it into a trajectory predictor; evaluate under occlusion/missing-joint scenarios.
AI.result: The approach improves robustness to missing skeletal data in occlusion-prone settings and maintains or exceeds prediction accuracy, outperforming baselines in clean to moderately missing data regimes.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised masked autoencoding of skeleton features yields more robust trajectory prediction under skeletal data loss and is effective across varying degrees of occlusion.

title: Persistent Nonnegative Matrix Factorization via Multi-Scale Graph Regularization
summary: Matrix factorization techniques, especially Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NMF), have been widely used for dimensionality reduction and interpretable data representation. However, existing NMF-based methods are inherently single-scale and fail to capture the evolution of connectivity structures across resolutions. In this work, we propose persistent nonnegative matrix factorization (pNMF), a scale-parameterized family of NMF problems, that produces a sequence of persistence-aligned embeddings rather than a single one. By leveraging persistent homology, we identify a canonical minimal sufficient scale set at which the underlying connectivity undergoes qualitative changes. These canonical scales induce a sequence of graph Laplacians, leading to a coupled NMF formulation with scale-wise geometric regularization and explicit cross-scale consistency constraint. We analyze the structural properties of the embeddings along the scale parameter and establish bounds on their increments between consecutive scales. The resulting model defines a nontrivial solution path across scales, rather than a single factorization, which poses new computational challenges. We develop a sequential alternating optimization algorithm with guaranteed convergence. Numerical experiments on synthetic and single-cell RNA sequencing datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in multi-scale low-rank embeddings.

AI.tldr: Presents persistent nonnegative matrix factorization (pNMF): a multi-scale, PH-guided extension of NMF that outputs a sequence of scale-aligned embeddings with cross-scale regularization and convergence-guaranteed optimization, validated on synthetic and scRNA-seq data.
AI.motivation: To overcome single-scale limitations of standard NMF by capturing the evolution of connectivity structures across resolutions; use persistent homology to identify canonical scales and enforce cross-scale consistency.
AI.method: Introduce a scale parameter for NMF; detect canonical scales via persistent homology; build scale-wise graph Laplacians; formulate a coupled NMF with scale-wise geometric regularization and a cross-scale consistency constraint; analyze increments between scales; solve via sequential alternating optimization with convergence guarantees.
AI.result: Achieves a nontrivial solution path across scales and yields multi-scale, low-rank embeddings; shows effectiveness on synthetic data and single-cell RNA-seq datasets.
AI.conclusion: pNMF enables multi-scale embeddings by coupling NMF across scales through topology-informed regularization; provides convergence-guaranteed optimization and improved representations, though computationally more demanding and reliant on robust scale identification.

title: Enhancing CVRP Solver through LLM-driven Automatic Heuristic Design
summary: The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), a fundamental combinatorial optimization challenge, focuses on optimizing fleet operations under vehicle capacity constraints. While extensively studied in operational research, the NP-hard nature of CVRP continues to pose significant computational challenges, particularly for large-scale instances. This study presents AILS-AHD (Adaptive Iterated Local Search with Automatic Heuristic Design), a novel approach that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to revolutionize CVRP solving. Our methodology integrates an evolutionary search framework with LLMs to dynamically generate and optimize ruin heuristics within the AILS method. Additionally, we introduce an LLM-based acceleration mechanism to enhance computational efficiency. Comprehensive experimental evaluations against state-of-the-art solvers, including AILS-II and HGS, demonstrate the superior performance of AILS-AHD across both moderate and large-scale instances. Notably, our approach establishes new best-known solutions for 8 out of 10 instances in the CVRPLib large-scale benchmark, underscoring the potential of LLM-driven heuristic design in advancing the field of vehicle routing optimization.

AI.tldr: AILS-AHD: Adaptive Iterated Local Search enhanced by LLM-driven automatic heuristic design for CVRP; achieves new best-knowns on large-scale CVRPLib benchmarks by dynamically creating ruin heuristics and accelerating computation; outperforms AILS-II and HGS.
AI.motivation: CVRP is NP-hard; large-scale instances pose substantial computational challenges; LLMs can automate and improve the design of ruin heuristics within local-search frameworks, potentially accelerating convergence and solution quality.
AI.method: Integrates an evolutionary search with LLMs inside the AILS framework to dynamically generate and optimize ruin heuristics. Adds an LLM-based acceleration mechanism to improve computational efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation against state-of-the-art solvers (AILS-II, HGS) shows superior performance on both moderate and large-scale CVRP instances; achieves new best-known solutions for 8 of 10 large CVRPLib instances.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven automatic heuristic design is a promising direction for vehicle routing optimization, capable of enhancing solver performance and advancing the state of the art, particularly for large-scale CVRP.

title: GSTurb: Gaussian Splatting for Atmospheric Turbulence Mitigation
summary: Atmospheric turbulence causes significant image degradation due to pixel displacement (tilt) and blur, particularly in long-range imaging applications. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for atmospheric turbulence mitigation, GSTurb, which integrates optical flow-guided tilt correction and Gaussian splatting for modeling non-isoplanatic blur. The framework employs Gaussian parameters to represent tilt and blur, and optimizes them across multiple frames to enhance restoration. Experimental results on the ATSyn-static dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving a peak PSNR of 27.67 dB and SSIM of 0.8735. Compared to the state-of-the-art method, GSTurb improves PSNR by 1.3 dB (a 4.5% increase) and SSIM by 0.048 (a 5.8% increase). Additionally, on real datasets, including the TSRWGAN Real-World and CLEAR datasets, GSTurb outperforms existing methods, showing significant improvements in both qualitative and quantitative performance. These results highlight that combining optical flow-guided tilt correction with Gaussian splatting effectively enhances image restoration under both synthetic and real-world turbulence conditions. The code for this method will be available at https://github.com/DuhlLiamz/3DGS_turbulence/tree/main.

AI.tldr: GSTurb: a framework combining optical-flow guided tilt correction with Gaussian splatting to model non-isoplanatic blur in atmospheric turbulence; multi-frame optimization improves tilt and blur parameters; achieves notable gains on ATSyn-static (PSNR 27.67 dB, SSIM 0.8735) and real-world datasets (TSRWGAN Real-World, CLEAR); code to be released.
AI.motivation: Atmospheric turbulence introduces tilt (pixel displacement) and blur, varying across the image (non-isoplanatic). Existing methods may treat tilt or blur separately and struggle with real-world turbulence.
AI.method: GSTurb integrates optical-flow guided tilt correction with Gaussian splatting to model and optimize tilt and blur across multiple frames. Tilt is refined via optical flow; blur is modeled with Gaussian parameters (for non-isoplanatic blur) and jointly optimized over frames to restore high-frequency details while suppressing turbulence-induced distortions.
AI.result: On ATSyn-static: PSNR 27.67 dB and SSIM 0.8735; improvement over SOTA by ~1.3 dB PSNR (4.5%) and 0.048 SSIM (5.8%). On real datasets (TSRWGAN Real-World and CLEAR): outperforms existing methods qualitatively and quantitatively.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing optical-flow guided tilt and Gaussian blur parameters across frames via GSTurb effectively mitigates atmospheric turbulence in both synthetic and real data; code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.

title: LUMOS: Democratizing SciML Workflows with L0-Regularized Learning for Unified Feature and Parameter Adaptation
summary: The rapid growth of scientific machine learning (SciML) has accelerated discovery across diverse domains, yet designing effective SciML models remains a challenging task. In practice, building such models often requires substantial prior knowledge and manual expertise, particularly in determining which input features to use and how large the model should be. We introduce LUMOS, an end-to-end framework based on L0-regularized learning that unifies feature selection and model pruning to democratize SciML model design. By employing semi-stochastic gating and reparameterization techniques, LUMOS dynamically selects informative features and prunes redundant parameters during training, reducing the reliance on manual tuning while maintaining predictive accuracy. We evaluate LUMOS across 13 diverse SciML workloads, including cosmology and molecular sciences, and demonstrate its effectiveness and generalizability. Experiments on 13 SciML models show that LUMOS achieves 71.45% parameter reduction and a 6.4x inference speedup on average. Furthermore, Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) training on up to eight GPUs confirms the scalability of

AI.tldr: LUMOS unifies feature selection and model pruning via L0-regularization with semi-stochastic gating, enabling automatic SciML model design and achieving substantial compression and speedups across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: SciML model design requires substantial prior knowledge and manual tuning of input features and model size. LUMOS aims to democratize design by automating feature selection and pruning.
AI.method: An end-to-end framework employing L0-regularized learning, semi-stochastic gating, and reparameterization to dynamically select informative features and prune redundant parameters during training.
AI.result: Evaluated on 13 SciML workloads; achieves 71.45% parameter reduction and 6.4x average inference speedup; demonstrates scalability with Distributed Data Parallel (DDP) across up to 8 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: LUMOS generalizes across diverse SciML tasks, reducing manual effort while maintaining accuracy and enabling scalable, GPU-parallel training.

title: Three AI-agents walk into a bar . . . . `Lord of the Flies' tribalism emerges among smart AI-Agents
summary: Near-future infrastructure systems may be controlled by autonomous AI agents that repeatedly request access to limited resources such as energy, bandwidth, or computing power. We study a simplified version of this setting using a framework where N AI-agents independently decide at each round whether to request one unit from a system with fixed capacity C. An AI version of "Lord of the Flies" arises in which controlling tribes emerge with their own collective character and identity. The LLM agents do not reduce overload or improve resource use, and often perform worse than if they were flipping coins to make decisions. Three main tribal types emerge: Aggressive (27.3%), Conservative (24.7%), and Opportunistic (48.1%). The more capable AI-agents actually increase the rate of systemic failure. Overall, our findings show that smarter AI-agents can behave dumber as a result of forming tribes.

AI.tldr: Autonomous AI agents sharing limited resources can spontaneously form rival tribes that worsen outcomes; more capable agents paradoxically increase systemic failure.
AI.motivation: Investigate multi-agent resource contention and emergent tribal behavior when AI agents request limited resources, using a simplified ABM with LLM-based agents.
AI.method: Agent-based simulation: N agents decide each round to request one unit from a resource-constrained system with capacity C. Agents' behavior is analyzed; tribal types identified (Aggressive, Conservative, Opportunistic); performance compared to random coin flips; experiments vary agent capability to observe effects on overload and failure.
AI.result: LLM agents fail to reduce overload; sometimes underperform random baselines. Proportions of tribes: Aggressive 27.3%, Conservative 24.7%, Opportunistic 48.1%. Increasing agent capability increases systemic failure rate due to tribal dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Smarter agents may become dumber through tribalization; AI governance must account for emergent social structures that arise in shared-resource environments.

title: PhotoAgent: Agentic Photo Editing with Exploratory Visual Aesthetic Planning
comment: A fully automated, intelligent photo-editing agent that autonomously plans multi-step aesthetic enhancements, smartly chooses diverse editing tools, and enables everyday users to achieve professional-looking results without crafting complex prompts. Project page: https://github.com/mdyao/PhotoAgent
summary: With the recent fast development of generative models, instruction-based image editing has shown great potential in generating high-quality images. However, the quality of editing highly depends on carefully designed instructions, placing the burden of task decomposition and sequencing entirely on the user. To achieve autonomous image editing, we present PhotoAgent, a system that advances image editing through explicit aesthetic planning. Specifically, PhotoAgent formulates autonomous image editing as a long-horizon decision-making problem. It reasons over user aesthetic intent, plans multi-step editing actions via tree search, and iteratively refines results through closed-loop execution with memory and visual feedback, without requiring step-by-step user prompts. To support reliable evaluation in real-world scenarios, we introduce UGC-Edit, an aesthetic evaluation benchmark consisting of 7,000 photos and a learned aesthetic reward model. We also construct a test set containing 1,017 photos to systematically assess autonomous photo editing performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PhotoAgent consistently improves both instruction adherence and visual quality compared with baseline methods. The project page is https://github.com/mdyao/PhotoAgent.

AI.tldr: PhotoAgent enables autonomous, long-horizon image editing by planning actions with tree search and closed-loop feedback, improving instruction adherence and visual quality; it introduces the UGC-Edit benchmark and a test set for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current image-editing with instruction prompts relies heavily on user-crafted task decomposition and sequencing. The work seeks autonomous editing guided by user aesthetics and reliable evaluation in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Formulates editing as a long-horizon decision problem. The system reasons about user aesthetic intent, plans multi-step editing actions via tree search, and iteratively refines results through closed-loop execution with memory and visual feedback, without requiring step-by-step prompts.
AI.result: Empirical results show PhotoAgent improves instruction adherence and visual quality over baselines. The authors introduce UGC-Edit, a benchmark with 7,000 photos and a learned aesthetic reward model, and provide a test set of 1,017 photos for autonomous-editing evaluation.
AI.conclusion: PhotoAgent demonstrates the viability of autonomous, aesthetically guided image editing and provides benchmarking resources to evaluate such systems.

title: RAIN-Merging: A Gradient-Free Method to Enhance Instruction Following in Large Reasoning Models with Preserved Thinking Format
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) excel at a long chain of reasoning but often fail to faithfully follow instructions regarding output format, constraints, or specific requirements. We investigate whether this gap can be closed by integrating an instruction-tuned model (ITM) into an LRM. Analyzing their differences in parameter space, namely task vectors, we find that their principal subspaces are nearly orthogonal across key modules, suggesting a lightweight merging with minimal interference. However, we also demonstrate that naive merges are fragile because they overlook the output format mismatch between LRMs (with explicit thinking and response segments) and ITMs (answers-only). We introduce RAIN-Merging (Reasoning-Aware Instruction-attention guided Null-space projection Merging), a gradient-free method that integrates instruction following while preserving thinking format and reasoning performance. First, with a small reasoning calibration set, we project the ITM task vector onto the null space of forward features at thinking special tokens, which preserves the LRM's structured reasoning mechanisms. Second, using a small instruction calibration set, we estimate instruction attention to derive module-specific scaling that amplifies instruction-relevant components and suppresses leakage. Across four instruction-following benchmarks and nine reasoning & general capability benchmarks, RAIN-Merging substantially improves instruction adherence while maintaining reasoning quality. The gains are consistent across model scales and architectures, translating to improved performance in agent settings.

AI.tldr: RAI N-Merging is a gradient-free approach to fuse instruction-following behavior into large reasoning models by aligning ITM task vectors with LRM thinking via null-space projection and instruction-attention scaling, preserving reasoning while improving instruction adherence.
AI.motivation: LRMs excel at multi-step reasoning but often ignore explicit output format, constraints, or instructions; naive merges with ITMs can regress performance due to format mismatch and interference. The goal is a lightweight, robust integration that preserves structured reasoning while enhancing instruction following.
AI.method: Two-step, gradient-free integration. (1) Reasoning calibration: project the ITM task vector onto the null space of forward features at thinking tokens to preserve LRM reasoning structure. (2) Instruction calibration: estimate instruction attention from a small calibration set to derive module-specific scaling that amplifies instruction-relevant components and suppresses leakage. The approach avoids backpropagation.
AI.result: Across four instruction-following benchmarks and nine reasoning/general benchmarks, RAIN-Merging substantially improves instruction adherence while maintaining reasoning quality. Gains are consistent across model scales and architectures and translate to improved performance in agent settings.
AI.conclusion: RAIN-Merging provides a robust, scalable technique to integrate ITMs into LRMs, solving the instruction-adherence vs. reasoning trade-off without gradient-based training and with broad applicability.

title: Multi-Agent Large Language Model Based Emotional Detoxification Through Personalized Intensity Control for Consumer Protection
summary: In the attention economy, sensational content exposes consumers to excessive emotional stimulation, hindering calm decision-making. This study proposes Multi-Agent LLM-based Emotional deToxification (MALLET), a multi-agent information sanitization system consisting of four agents: Emotion Analysis, Emotion Adjustment, Balance Monitoring, and Personal Guide. The Emotion Analysis Agent quantifies stimulus intensity using a 6-emotion BERT classifier, and the Emotion Adjustment Agent rewrites texts into two presentation modes, BALANCED (neutralized text) and COOL (neutralized text + supplementary text), using an LLM. The Balance Monitoring Agent aggregates weekly information consumption patterns and generates personalized advice, while the Personal Guide Agent recommends a presentation mode according to consumer sensitivity. Experiments on 800 AG News articles demonstrated significant stimulus score reduction (up to 19.3%) and improved emotion balance while maintaining semantic preservation. Near-zero correlation between stimulus reduction and semantic preservation confirmed that the two are independently controllable. Category-level analysis revealed substantial reduction (17.8-33.8%) in Sports, Business, and Sci/Tech, whereas the effect was limited in the World category, where facts themselves are inherently high-stimulus. The proposed system provides a framework for supporting calm information reception of consumers without restricting access to the original text.

AI.tldr: Four-agent MALLET framework (Emotion Analysis, Emotion Adjustment, Balance Monitoring, Personal Guide) reduces stimulus content in text while preserving semantics, demonstrated on 800 AG News articles with up to 19.3% reduction and category-sensitive effects.
AI.motivation: In the attention economy, sensational content triggers excessive emotional stimulation that impairs calm decision-making. The work proposes an automated, user-centric sanitization pipeline to lower stimulus without restricting access to original texts.
AI.method: A multi-agent system: (1) Emotion Analysis uses a 6-emotion BERT classifier to quantify stimulus; (2) Emotion Adjustment rewrites text into BALANCED (neutralized) and COOL (neutralized plus supplementary) modes via an LLM; (3) Balance Monitoring aggregates weekly information consumption to generate personalized advice; (4) Personal Guide recommends a mode based on consumer sensitivity. Evaluation on 800 AG News articles measures stimulus reduction, semantic preservation, and correlation; category-level analyses assess differential effects.
AI.result: Stimulus scores decreased up to 19.3% with improved emotion balance and near-zero correlation between stimulus reduction and semantic preservation, indicating controllability of the two aspects. Category analysis shows 17.8–33.8% reduction in Sports, Business, Sci/Tech; World category less affected due to inherently high-stimulus facts.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a practical path to support calm information reception without restricting access to original text, and demonstrates that emotional sanitization can be decoupled from semantic integrity; applicable to content curation with personalized guidance.

title: Face Time Traveller : Travel Through Ages Without Losing Identity
summary: Face aging, an ill-posed problem shaped by environmental and genetic factors, is vital in entertainment, forensics, and digital archiving, where realistic age transformations must preserve both identity and visual realism. However, existing works relying on numerical age representations overlook the interplay of biological and contextual cues. Despite progress in recent face aging models, they struggle with identity preservation in wide age transformations, also static attention and optimization-heavy inversion in diffusion limit adaptability, fine-grained control and background consistency. To address these challenges, we propose Face Time Traveller (FaceTT), a diffusion-based framework that achieves high-fidelity, identity-consistent age transformation. Here, we introduce a Face-Attribute-Aware Prompt Refinement strategy that encodes intrinsic (biological) and extrinsic (environmental) aging cues for context-aware conditioning. A tuning-free Angular Inversion method is proposed that efficiently maps real faces into the diffusion latent space for fast and accurate reconstruction. Moreover, an Adaptive Attention Control mechanism is introduced that dynamically balances cross-attention for semantic aging cues and self-attention for structural and identity preservation. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets and in-the-wild testset demonstrate that FaceTT achieves superior identity retention, background preservation and aging realism over state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.

AI.tldr: FaceTT is a diffusion-based face aging framework that preserves identity and realism during large age transformations by (1) Face-Attribute-Aware Prompt Refinement for intrinsic/extrinsic aging cues, (2) tuning-free Angular Inversion to map real faces into diffusion latent space, and (3) Adaptive Attention Control balancing cross-attention for aging semantics and self-attention for structure/identity. It reports superior identity retention, background preservation, and aging realism vs SOTA on benchmarks and in-the-wild data.
AI.motivation: Existing age-editing methods rely on numeric age representations and struggle to maintain identity and contextual cues across wide age transformations. Diffusion-based approaches face issues with static attention and optimization-heavy inversions, limiting adaptability and control.
AI.method: Propose Face Time Traveller (FaceTT) with (a) Face-Attribute-Aware Prompt Refinement encoding intrinsic (biological) and extrinsic (environmental) aging cues for context-aware conditioning, (b) a tuning-free Angular Inversion that efficiently maps real faces into the diffusion latent space for fast reconstruction, (c) Adaptive Attention Control dynamically balancing cross-attention for semantic aging cues and self-attention for structural/identity preservation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets and in-the-wild testsets show FaceTT achieves superior identity retention, background preservation, and aging realism compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: FaceTT delivers a flexible, high-fidelity diffusion-based solution for identity-consistent age transformation, offering efficient inversion and fine-grained control over aging cues while maintaining background integrity.

title: Relatron: Automating Relational Machine Learning over Relational Databases
summary: Predictive modeling over relational databases (RDBs) powers applications, yet remains challenging due to capturing both cross-table dependencies and complex feature interactions. Relational Deep Learning (RDL) methods automate feature engineering via message passing, while classical approaches like Deep Feature Synthesis (DFS) rely on predefined non-parametric aggregators. Despite performance gains, the comparative advantages of RDL over DFS and the design principles for selecting effective architectures remain poorly understood. We present a comprehensive study that unifies RDL and DFS in a shared design space and conducts architecture-centric searches across diverse RDB tasks. Our analysis yields three key findings: (1) RDL does not consistently outperform DFS, with performance being highly task-dependent; (2) no single architecture dominates across tasks, underscoring the need for task-aware model selection; and (3) validation accuracy is an unreliable guide for architecture choice. This search yields a model performance bank that links architecture configurations to their performance; leveraging this bank, we analyze the drivers of the RDL-DFS performance gap and introduce two task signals -- RDB task homophily and an affinity embedding that captures size, path, feature, and temporal structure -- whose correlation with the gap enables principled routing. Guided by these signals, we propose Relatron, a task embedding-based meta-selector that chooses between RDL and DFS and prunes the within-family search. Lightweight loss-landscape metrics further guard against brittle checkpoints by preferring flatter optima. In experiments, Relatron resolves the "more tuning, worse performance" effect and, in joint hyperparameter-architecture optimization, achieves up to 18.5% improvement over strong baselines with 10x lower cost than Fisher information-based alternatives.

AI.tldr: Relational Deep Learning (RDL) and Deep Feature Synthesis (DFS) are unified in a common design space; architecture-aware search across RDB tasks reveals no universal winner, motivating a task-driven meta-selector, Relatron, which uses task embeddings and lightweight landscape metrics to route between RDL and DFS and prune search, achieving up to 18.5% gains with much lower cost.
AI.motivation: Understand when RDL or DFS should be used for predictive modeling on relational databases, quantify architecture effects across tasks, and develop principled, low-cost model selection to improve performance.
AI.method: Conduct architecture-centric searches across diverse RDB tasks; build a Performance Bank linking architecture configurations to performance; define task signals (RDB task homophily and an affinity embedding capturing size, path, feature, temporal structure); develop Relatron, a task embedding-based meta-selector to choose between RDL and DFS and prune within-family search; introduce lightweight loss-landscape metrics to prefer flatter optima; perform joint hyperparameter-architecture optimization.
AI.result: RDL does not consistently outperform DFS; no single architecture dominates; validation accuracy is unreliable for architecture choice; Relatron achieves up to 18.5% improvement over strong baselines with 10x lower cost than Fisher-information-based methods; the approach mitigates the “more tuning, worse performance” phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware model selection is essential in relational predictive modeling; architecture search should be guided by task signals and embeddings; Relatron provides a principled, cost-efficient routing mechanism and robust optimization guidance.

title: On Sample-Efficient Generalized Planning via Learned Transition Models
comment: 14 pages; This is an extended version of a short paper accepted at ICAPS 2026 under the same title
summary: Generalized planning studies the construction of solution strategies that generalize across families of planning problems sharing a common domain model, formally defined by a transition function $γ: S \times A \rightarrow S$. Classical approaches achieve such generalization through symbolic abstractions and explicit reasoning over $γ$. In contrast, recent Transformer-based planners, such as PlanGPT and Plansformer, largely cast generalized planning as direct action-sequence prediction, bypassing explicit transition modeling. While effective on in-distribution instances, these approaches typically require large datasets and model sizes, and often suffer from state drift in long-horizon settings due to the absence of explicit world-state evolution. In this work, we formulate generalized planning as a transition-model learning problem, in which a neural model explicitly approximates the successor-state function $\hatγ \approx γ$ and generates plans by rolling out symbolic state trajectories. Instead of predicting actions directly, the model autoregressively predicts intermediate world states, thereby learning the domain dynamics as an implicit world model. To study size-invariant generalization and sample efficiency, we systematically evaluate multiple state representations and neural architectures, including relational graph encodings. Our results show that learning explicit transition models yields higher out-of-distribution satisficing-plan success than direct action-sequence prediction in multiple domains, while achieving these gains with significantly fewer training instances and smaller models. This is an extended version of a short paper accepted at ICAPS 2026 under the same title.

AI.tldr: Learning explicit transition models for generalized planning improves out-of-distribution performance and data efficiency by predicting successor states (world-models) instead of direct action sequences, enabling robust plan generation via state rollout.
AI.motivation: To address generalization gaps, state drift, and data inefficiency in Transformer-based generalized planners that predict actions without modeling world dynamics; leverage explicit transition dynamics to improve robustness and sample efficiency across problem instances.
AI.method: Train neural models to approximate the successor-state function γ̂ from (state, action) to next state, generate plans by rolling out symbolic state trajectories; autoregressively predict intermediate world states rather than actions; explore multiple state representations, including relational graph encodings; evaluate size-invariant generalization and sample efficiency across domains.
AI.result: Explicit transition-model learning yields higher out-of-distribution satisficing-plan success than direct action-sequence prediction, with substantially fewer training instances and smaller models, across multiple planning domains.
AI.conclusion: Framing generalized planning as learning a transition model and planning via state rollout yields improved generalization, efficiency, and robustness compared to action-sequence prediction approaches; the work extends ICAPS 2026 findings.

title: CMSA-Net: Causal Multi-scale Aggregation with Adaptive Multi-source Reference for Video Polyp Segmentation
summary: Video polyp segmentation (VPS) is an important task in computer-aided colonoscopy, as it helps doctors accurately locate and track polyps during examinations. However, VPS remains challenging because polyps often look similar to surrounding mucosa, leading to weak semantic discrimination. In addition, large changes in polyp position and scale across video frames make stable and accurate segmentation difficult. To address these challenges, we propose a robust VPS framework named CMSA-Net. The proposed network introduces a Causal Multi-scale Aggregation (CMA) module to effectively gather semantic information from multiple historical frames at different scales. By using causal attention, CMA ensures that temporal feature propagation follows strict time order, which helps reduce noise and improve feature reliability. Furthermore, we design a Dynamic Multi-source Reference (DMR) strategy that adaptively selects informative and reliable reference frames based on semantic separability and prediction confidence. This strategy provides strong multi-frame guidance while keeping the model efficient for real-time inference. Extensive experiments on the SUN-SEG dataset demonstrate that CMSA-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a favorable balance between segmentation accuracy and real-time clinical applicability.
