It fuses information from both speakers to capture interaction dynamics, uses a motion dictionary to encode motion priors, and can optionally utilize the conversational partner's gestures to produce more responsive motion. We evaluate DyaDiT on standard motion generation metrics and conduct quantitative user studies, demonstrating that it not only surpasses existing methods on objective metrics but is also strongly preferred by users, highlighting its robustness and socially favorable motion generation. Code and models will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: DyaDiT is a dyadic, multi-modal diffusion transformer that generates context-appropriate conversational gestures by fusing both speakers' audio (with optional social-context tokens) and motion priors, optionally using the partner's gestures, achieving superior objective metrics and user-preferred motion on the Seamless Interaction Dataset.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of single-audio-to-single-motion mapping and neglect of social context and mutual interaction in dyadic conversations, enabling natural, socially engaging digital humans.
AI.method: A diffusion transformer that ingests dyadic audio and optional social-context tokens, fuses both speakers, uses a motion dictionary as motion priors, and can condition on the partner's gestures; trained on the Seamless Interaction Dataset; outputs dyadic motion.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on standard motion-generation metrics and is strongly preferred by users in quantitative studies; demonstrates robustness and socially favorable motion; code/models to be released.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating social context and bidirectional interaction dynamics via dyadic audio and motion priors yields more natural and engaging gestures for digital humans; DyaDiT advances dyadic gesture synthesis and enables reproducible, end-to-end evaluation.

title: Generalization Bounds of Stochastic Gradient Descent in Homogeneous Neural Networks
summary: Algorithmic stability is among the most potent techniques in generalization analysis. However, its derivation usually requires a stepsize $η_t = \mathcal{O}(1/t)$ under non-convex training regimes, where $t$ denotes iterations. This rigid decay of the stepsize potentially impedes optimization and may not align with practical scenarios. In this paper, we derive the generalization bounds under the homogeneous neural network regimes, proving that this regime enables slower stepsize decay of order $Ω(1/\sqrt{t})$ under mild assumptions. We further extend the theoretical results from several aspects, e.g., non-Lipschitz regimes. This finding is broadly applicable, as homogeneous neural networks encompass fully-connected and convolutional neural networks with ReLU and LeakyReLU activations.

AI.tldr: Generalization bounds under homogeneous neural networks permit slower stepsize decay Ω(1/√t) than the usual O(1/t) in non-convex regimes.
AI.motivation: To relax stepsize decay in stability-based generalization analysis and align theory with practical optimization.
AI.method: Theoretical derivation of algorithmic stability bounds in homogeneous neural network regimes; extends to non-Lipschitz settings; demonstrates applicability to ReLU/LeakyReLU FCNs/CNNs.
AI.result: Proves Ω(1/√t) decay suffices for generalization guarantees; broadens validity to non-Lipschitz cases and typical architectures.
AI.conclusion: Homogeneous network structure enables slower decay of step sizes without sacrificing generalization guarantees, improving practical applicability.

title: AgentVista: Evaluating Multimodal Agents in Ultra-Challenging Realistic Visual Scenarios
comment: The project website is available at \url{https://agentvista-bench.github.io/}, and the code is available at \url{https://github.com/hkust-nlp/AgentVista}
summary: Real-world multimodal agents solve multi-step workflows grounded in visual evidence. For example, an agent can troubleshoot a device by linking a wiring photo to a schematic and validating the fix with online documentation, or plan a trip by interpreting a transit map and checking schedules under routing constraints. However, existing multimodal benchmarks mainly evaluate single-turn visual reasoning or specific tool skills, and they do not fully capture the realism, visual subtlety, and long-horizon tool use that practical agents require. We introduce AgentVista, a benchmark for generalist multimodal agents that spans 25 sub-domains across 7 categories, pairing realistic and detail-rich visual scenarios with natural hybrid tool use. Tasks require long-horizon tool interactions across modalities, including web search, image search, page navigation, and code-based operations for both image processing and general programming. Comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art models exposes significant gaps in their ability to carry out long-horizon multimodal tool use. Even the best model in our evaluation, Gemini-3-Pro with tools, achieves only 27.3% overall accuracy, and hard instances can require more than 25 tool-calling turns. We expect AgentVista to accelerate the development of more capable and reliable multimodal agents for realistic and ultra-challenging problem solving.

AI.tldr: AgentVista is a new benchmark for generalist multimodal agents, covering 25 sub-domains across 7 categories and emphasizing long-horizon, cross-modal tool use. State-of-the-art models perform poorly (best ~27.3% accuracy), highlighting substantial gaps in multi-step multimodal reasoning and tool integration.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks focus on single-turn visual reasoning or limited tool skills, failing to capture realistic, long-horizon multimodal problem solving required by practical agents.
AI.method: Introduce AgentVista with 25 sub-domains across 7 categories featuring realistic visual scenarios and natural, hybrid tool use. Tasks require long-horizon tool interactions across modalities (web/image search, page navigation, code-based operations for image processing and programming). Evaluate state-of-the-art models, revealing gaps.
AI.result: Significant performance gaps exist; even the best model with tools achieves only 27.3% overall accuracy, with hard instances needing over 25 tool calls, underscoring the challenge of long-horizon multimodal tool use.
AI.conclusion: AgentVista has the potential to accelerate development of more capable and reliable multimodal agents for realistic, ultra-challenging problem solving by providing a comprehensive, diverse benchmark that stresses long-horizon tool use and cross-modal reasoning.

title: MSINO: Curvature-Aware Sobolev Optimization for Manifold Neural Networks
comment: 32 pages, 6 figures. Submitted for journal consideration
summary:
We introduce Manifold Sobolev Informed Neural Optimization (MSINO), a curvature aware training framework for neural networks defined on Riemannian manifolds. The method replaces standard Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss that aligns gradients using parallel transport and improves stability via a Laplace Beltrami smoothness regularization term.
  Building on classical results in Riemannian optimization and Sobolev theory on manifolds, we derive geometry dependent constants that yield (i) a Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) a Sobolev Polyak Lojasiewicz inequality giving linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent under explicit step size bounds, and (iii) a two step Newton Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature controlled neighborhoods.
  Unlike prior Sobolev training in Euclidean space, MSINO provides training time guarantees that explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians. Applications include surface imaging, physics informed learning settings, and robotics on Lie groups such as SO(3) and SE(3). The framework unifies value and gradient based learning with curvature aware convergence guarantees for neural training on manifolds.

AI.tldr: MSINO is a curvature-aware neural optimization framework on Riemannian manifolds that uses a covariant Sobolev loss and Laplace-Beltrami regularization to align gradients and ensure convergence guarantees (Descent Lemma, Sobolev PL inequality, Newton-Sobolev method) with explicit curvature tracking; enables stable training for manifold-valued networks and Lie group tasks.
AI.motivation: Extend Sobolev-based training from Euclidean space to Riemannian manifolds to address gradient alignment across tangent spaces, curvature effects, and stability in optimization; provide geometric convergence guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce a covariant Sobolev loss incorporating parallel transport for gradient alignment, plus a Laplace-Beltrami smoothness regularization term. Derive geometry-dependent constants yielding (i) Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) Sobolev Polyak–Lojasiewicz inequality for linear convergence of Riemannian gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent under explicit step sizes, (iii) a two-step Newton–Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature-controlled neighborhoods; provide explicit tracking of curvature and transported Jacobians for training-time guarantees.
AI.result: Provides linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent and SGD, plus a Newton–Sobolev method with quadratic contraction, all with curvature-aware step-size bounds; delivers training-time guarantees and a unified framework for manifold-valued learning.
AI.conclusion: MSINO unifies value and gradient-based learning with curvature-aware convergence guarantees for neural training on manifolds, enabling applications in surface imaging, physics-informed learning, and robotics on Lie groups (e.g., SO(3), SE(3)).

title: Learning Continuous Wasserstein Barycenter Space for Generalized All-in-One Image Restoration
summary: Despite substantial advances in all-in-one image restoration for addressing diverse degradations within a unified model, existing methods remain vulnerable to out-of-distribution degradations, thereby limiting their generalization in real-world scenarios. To tackle the challenge, this work is motivated by the intuition that multisource degraded feature distributions are induced by different degradation-specific shifts from an underlying degradation-agnostic distribution, and recovering such a shared distribution is thus crucial for achieving generalization across degradations. With this insight, we propose BaryIR, a representation learning framework that aligns multisource degraded features in the Wasserstein barycenter (WB) space, which models a degradation-agnostic distribution by minimizing the average of Wasserstein distances to multisource degraded distributions. We further introduce residual subspaces, whose embeddings are mutually contrasted while remaining orthogonal to the WB embeddings. Consequently, BaryIR explicitly decouples two orthogonal spaces: a WB space that encodes the degradation-agnostic invariant contents shared across degradations, and residual subspaces that adaptively preserve the degradation-specific knowledge. This disentanglement mitigates overfitting to in-distribution degradations and enables adaptive restoration grounded on the degradation-agnostic shared invariance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BaryIR performs competitively against state-of-the-art all-in-one methods. Notably, BaryIR generalizes well to unseen degradations (\textit{e.g.,} types and levels) and shows remarkable robustness in learning generalized features, even when trained on limited degradation types and evaluated on real-world data with mixed degradations.

AI.tldr: BaryIR aligns multisource degraded features to a degradation-agnostic Wasserstein barycenter and disentangles invariant WB space from degradation-specific residual subspaces, achieving better generalization to unseen degradations and real-world mixed degradations while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art all-in-one restoration methods.
AI.motivation: All-in-one image restoration methods achieve limited generalization to out-of-distribution degradations; a degradation-agnostic representation is needed to improve robustness.
AI.method: Learn a Wasserstein barycenter (WB) space representing degradation-agnostic content by minimizing the average Wasserstein distance to multisource degraded distributions. Introduce residual subspaces that are orthogonal to the WB embeddings and mutually contrasted, enabling decoupled representations: WB for invariant content, residuals for degradation-specific knowledge.
AI.result: Empirical results show competitive performance with state-of-the-art all-in-one methods; strong generalization to unseen degradations (types and levels); robust learning of generalized features even when trained on limited degradation types and evaluated on real-world data with mixed degradations.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling degradation-agnostic WB content from degradation-specific residuals mitigates overfitting to in-distribution degradations and enables adaptive restoration across degradations, yielding robust and generalizable restoration.

title: Scaling Laws of Global Weather Models
summary: Data-driven models are revolutionizing weather forecasting. To optimize training efficiency and model performance, this paper analyzes empirical scaling laws within this domain. We investigate the relationship between model performance (validation loss) and three key factors: model size ($N$), dataset size ($D$), and compute budget ($C$). Across a range of models, we find that Aurora exhibits the strongest data-scaling behavior: increasing the training dataset by 10x reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x. GraphCast demonstrates the highest parameter efficiency, yet suffers from limited hardware utilization. Our compute-optimal analysis indicates that, under fixed compute budgets, allocating resources to longer training durations yields greater performance gains than increasing model size. Furthermore, we analyze model shape and uncover scaling behaviors that differ fundamentally from those observed in language models: weather forecasting models consistently favor increased width over depth. These findings suggest that future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance.

AI.tldr: Empirical scaling laws for weather forecasting models show data scaling is strong (Aurora), parameter efficiency (GraphCast) with hardware constraints, and compute budgets favor longer training; architecture should favor width over depth; larger training datasets and wider models improve performance.
AI.motivation: To guide design of data-driven weather models by understanding how validation loss scales with model size, data size, and compute.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across multiple models (Aurora, GraphCast) varying N, D, and C; compute-optimal analysis; assessment of model shape (width vs depth) in weather-domain models.
AI.result: Aurora data scaling: 10x data reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x; GraphCast achieves high parameter efficiency but limited hardware utilization; under fixed compute budgets, longer training duration yields greater gains than increasing N; weather models prefer width over depth, differing from language models.
AI.conclusion: Future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance; scaling behaviors differ from NLP, implying domain-specific design strategies.

title: Phys-3D: Physics-Constrained Real-Time Crowd Tracking and Counting on Railway Platforms
summary: Accurate, real-time crowd counting on railway platforms is essential for safety and capacity management. We propose to use a single camera mounted in a train, scanning the platform while arriving. While hardware constraints are simple, counting remains challenging due to dense occlusions, camera motion, and perspective distortions during train arrivals. Most existing tracking-by-detection approaches assume static cameras or ignore physical consistency in motion modeling, leading to unreliable counting under dynamic conditions. We propose a physics-constrained tracking framework that unifies detection, appearance, and 3D motion reasoning in a real-time pipeline. Our approach integrates a transfer-learned YOLOv11m detector with EfficientNet-B0 appearance encoding within DeepSORT, while introducing a physics-constrained Kalman model (Phys-3D) that enforces physically plausible 3D motion dynamics through pinhole geometry. To address counting brittleness under occlusions, we implement a virtual counting band with persistence. On our platform benchmark, MOT-RailwayPlatformCrowdHead Dataset(MOT-RPCH), our method reduces counting error to 2.97%, demonstrating robust performance despite motion and occlusions. Our results show that incorporating first-principles geometry and motion priors enables reliable crowd counting in safety-critical transportation scenarios, facilitating effective train scheduling and platform safety management.

AI.tldr: A physics-constrained 3D tracking framework integrates detection, appearance, and motion priors for real-time crowd counting on moving platforms, achieving low error on a platform-specific dataset.
AI.motivation: Counter safety-critical requirements on railway platforms under camera motion and occlusion; existing tracking-by-detection methods assume static cameras or ignore 3D motion consistency, leading to unreliable counts.
AI.method: A fusion of a transfer-learned YOLOv11m detector with EfficientNet-B0 appearance encoding within DeepSORT, augmented by a physics-constrained 3D Kalman filter (Phys-3D) that enforces pinhole-geometry-based 3D motion; a virtual counting band with persistence mitigates occlusion-induced counting brittleness.
AI.result: On the MOT-RPCH benchmark, the approach reduces counting error to 2.97%, demonstrating robust counting under motion and occlusion in dynamic railway scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating first-principles geometry and motion priors yields reliable, real-time crowd counting for safety and operations in transportation, enabling better train scheduling and platform safety management.

title: Exploratory Memory-Augmented LLM Agent via Hybrid On- and Off-Policy Optimization
summary: Exploration remains the key bottleneck for large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning. While prior methods exploit pretrained knowledge, they fail in environments requiring the discovery of novel states. We propose Exploratory Memory-Augmented On- and Off-Policy Optimization (EMPO$^2$), a hybrid RL framework that leverages memory for exploration and combines on- and off-policy updates to make LLMs perform well with memory while also ensuring robustness without it. On ScienceWorld and WebShop, EMPO$^2$ achieves 128.6% and 11.3% improvements over GRPO, respectively. Moreover, in out-of-distribution tests, EMPO$^2$ demonstrates superior adaptability to new tasks, requiring only a few trials with memory and no parameter updates. These results highlight EMPO$^2$ as a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: EMPO^2 is a memory-augmented hybrid RL framework for LLM agents that improves exploration and generalization by combining on- and off-policy updates; it outperforms GRPO on ScienceWorld and WebShop and shows fast OOD adaptation with memory and no updates.
AI.motivation: Address the exploration bottleneck in reinforcement learning for large language model agents, especially in environments requiring discovery of novel states beyond pretrained knowledge.
AI.method: Propose Exploratory Memory-Augmented On- and Off-Policy Optimization (EMPO^2), a hybrid RL framework that uses memory to enhance exploration and combines on- and off-policy updates to maintain robustness with and without memory.
AI.result: Empirical results show 128.6% improvement over GRPO on ScienceWorld and 11.3% on WebShop. In out-of-distribution tests, EMPO^2 adapts to new tasks with only a few trials, using memory and no parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: EMPO^2 is a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents.

title: Uni-Animator: Towards Unified Visual Colorization
summary: We propose Uni-Animator, a novel Diffusion Transformer (DiT)-based framework for unified image and video sketch colorization. Existing sketch colorization methods struggle to unify image and video tasks, suffering from imprecise color transfer with single or multiple references, inadequate preservation of high-frequency physical details, and compromised temporal coherence with motion artifacts in large-motion scenes. To tackle imprecise color transfer, we introduce visual reference enhancement via instance patch embedding, enabling precise alignment and fusion of reference color information. To resolve insufficient physical detail preservation, we design physical detail reinforcement using physical features that effectively capture and retain high-frequency textures. To mitigate motion-induced temporal inconsistency, we propose sketch-based dynamic RoPE encoding that adaptively models motion-aware spatial-temporal dependencies. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that Uni-Animator achieves competitive performance on both image and video sketch colorization, matching that of task-specific methods while unlocking unified cross-domain capabilities with high detail fidelity and robust temporal consistency.

AI.tldr: Uni-Animator is a DiT-based unified framework for image and video sketch colorization that uses instance patch embedding for precise reference color alignment, physical feature-based detail reinforcement to preserve high-frequency textures, and sketch-based dynamic RoPE encoding to model motion-aware spatio-temporal dependencies, achieving competitive cross-domain performance with high detail fidelity and robust temporal coherence.
AI.motivation: Address the fragmentation between image and video sketch colorization methods and tackle challenges of imprecise color transfer with references, insufficient preservation of fine physical details, and temporal artifacts in large-motion video scenes by unifying both tasks in a single diffusion-transformer framework.
AI.method: Three core components integrated into a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) framework: (1) visual reference enhancement via instance patch embedding for accurate alignment of reference colors; (2) physical detail reinforcement using physical features to capture and retain high-frequency textures; (3) sketch-based dynamic RoPE encoding to adaptively model motion-aware spatial-temporal dependencies, enabling unified handling of images and videos.
AI.result: Experimental results show Uni-Animator achieves competitive performance on both image and video sketch colorization, matching task-specific methods while enabling unified cross-domain capabilities with high detail fidelity and robust temporal consistency.
AI.conclusion: Uni-Animator demonstrates that a unified DiT-based framework can effectively bridge image and video sketch colorization without sacrificing performance, delivering accurate color transfer, preserved textures, and stable temporal coherence across diverse motion scenarios.

title: Learning Disease-Sensitive Latent Interaction Graphs From Noisy Cardiac Flow Measurements
summary: Cardiac blood flow patterns contain rich information about disease severity and clinical interventions, yet current imaging and computational methods fail to capture underlying relational structures of coherent flow features. We propose a physics-informed, latent relational framework to model cardiac vortices as interacting nodes in a graph. Our model combines a neural relational inference architecture with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics, yielding a latent graph sensitive to disease severity and intervention level. We first apply this to computational fluid dynamics simulations of aortic coarctation. Learned latent graphs reveal that as the aortic radius narrows, vortex interactions become stronger and more frequent. This leads to a higher graph entropy, correlating monotonically with coarctation severity ($R^2=0.78$, Spearman $|ρ|=0.96$). We then extend this method to ultrasound datasets of left ventricles under varying levels of left ventricular assist device support. Again the latent graph representation captures the weakening of coherent vortical structures, thereby demonstrating cross-modal generalisation. Results show latent interaction graphs and entropy serve as robust and interpretable markers of cardiac disease and intervention.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed latent graph model captures cardiac vortex interactions, linking stronger, more frequent interactions and higher graph entropy to disease severity and LVAD support across CFD and ultrasound data.
AI.motivation: Current imaging methods inadequately capture relational structures of coherent cardiac flow features; interpretable markers of disease severity and intervention level are needed.
AI.method: A neural relational inference (NRI) framework is augmented with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics to model vortices as interacting graph nodes. The latent graph is learned from CFD simulations of aortic coarctation and ultrasound datasets of left ventricles under LVAD support, with entropy of the graph serving as a disease/intervention marker.
AI.result: For aortic coarctation: as the aortic radius narrows, vortex interactions strengthen and become more frequent, yielding higher graph entropy that correlates with severity (R^2=0.78, |Spearman ρ|=0.96). For LVAD scenarios: latent graphs capture weakening of coherent vortices; cross-modal generalization observed. Latent interaction graphs and entropy are robust, interpretable markers of disease and intervention.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers a physics-informed, interpretable latent graph representation of cardiac flow that generalizes across modalities and provides quantitative markers of disease severity and intervention level.

title: FairQuant: Fairness-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization for Medical Image Classification
summary: Compressing neural networks by quantizing model parameters offers useful trade-off between performance and efficiency. Methods like quantization-aware training and post-training quantization strive to maintain the downstream performance of compressed models compared to the full precision models. However, these techniques do not explicitly consider the impact on algorithmic fairness. In this work, we study fairness-aware mixed-precision quantization schemes for medical image classification under explicit bit budgets. We introduce FairQuant, a framework that combines group-aware importance analysis, budgeted mixed-precision allocation, and a learnable Bit-Aware Quantization (BAQ) mode that jointly optimizes weights and per-unit bit allocations under bitrate and fairness regularization. We evaluate the method on Fitzpatrick17k and ISIC2019 across ResNet18/50, DeiT-Tiny, and TinyViT. Results show that FairQuant configurations with average precision near 4-6 bits recover much of the Uniform 8-bit accuracy while improving worst-group performance relative to Uniform 4- and 8-bit baselines, with comparable fairness metrics under shared budgets.

AI.tldr: FairQuant introduces fairness-aware mixed-precision quantization for medical image classification, achieving efficient bit usage (4-6 bits) while improving worst-group performance under shared budgets.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of fairness considerations in neural network quantization. Medical imaging models must perform reliably across demographic/group subpopulations, even under aggressive compression and budget constraints.
AI.method: A framework (FairQuant) combining group-aware importance analysis, budgeted mixed-precision allocation, and a learnable Bit-Aware Quantization (BAQ) mode. Optimizes weights and per-unit bit allocations under bitrate and fairness regularization.
AI.result: Evaluations on Fitzpatrick17k and ISIC2019 using ResNet18/50, DeiT-Tiny, and TinyViT show that FairQuant configurations with average precision around 4-6 bits recover much of Full-precision performance and Uniform 8-bit accuracy, while improving worst-group performance compared to Uniform 4- and 8-bit baselines; fairness metrics remain comparable under shared budgets.
AI.conclusion: FairQuant enables fairness-aware, budget-constrained quantization for medical image classifiers, achieving efficient bit usage with preserved overall accuracy and enhanced worst-group fairness.

title: ColoDiff: Integrating Dynamic Consistency With Content Awareness for Colonoscopy Video Generation
summary: Colonoscopy video generation delivers dynamic, information-rich data critical for diagnosing intestinal diseases, particularly in data-scarce scenarios. High-quality video generation demands temporal consistency and precise control over clinical attributes, but faces challenges from irregular intestinal structures, diverse disease representations, and various imaging modalities. To this end, we propose ColoDiff, a diffusion-based framework that generates dynamic-consistent and content-aware colonoscopy videos, aiming to alleviate data shortage and assist clinical analysis. At the inter-frame level, our TimeStream module decouples temporal dependency from video sequences through a cross-frame tokenization mechanism, enabling intricate dynamic modeling despite irregular intestinal structures. At the intra-frame level, our Content-Aware module incorporates noise-injected embeddings and learnable prototypes to realize precise control over clinical attributes, breaking through the coarse guidance of diffusion models. Additionally, ColoDiff employs a non-Markovian sampling strategy that cuts steps by over 90% for real-time generation. ColoDiff is evaluated across three public datasets and one hospital database, based on both generation metrics and downstream tasks including disease diagnosis, modality discrimination, bowel preparation scoring, and lesion segmentation. Extensive experiments show ColoDiff generates videos with smooth transitions and rich dynamics. ColoDiff presents an effort in controllable colonoscopy video generation, revealing the potential of synthetic videos in complementing authentic representation and mitigating data scarcity in clinical settings.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based ColoDiff generates dynamic-consistent, content-aware colonoscopy videos to alleviate data scarcity; introduces TimeStream for inter-frame temporal modeling, Content-Aware intra-frame control via noise-injected embeddings and prototypes, and a non-Markovian sampler enabling real-time generation; validated on four datasets with downstream tasks showing potential clinical utility.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and the need for temporally coherent, controllable colonoscopy videos across irregular anatomy and multiple imaging modalities.
AI.method: TimeStream cross-frame tokenization decouples temporal dependencies; Content-Aware module uses noise-injected embeddings and learnable prototypes for precise clinical-attribute control; non-Markovian sampling to cut steps by >90% for real-time generation.
AI.result: ColoDiff yields smooth transitions and rich dynamics; demonstrates controllability and suitability across multiple imaging modalities; effective on generation metrics and downstream tasks across four datasets (three public, one hospital).
AI.conclusion: ColoDiff demonstrates the feasibility of controllable, diffusion-based colonoscopy video generation to mitigate data scarcity and support clinical analysis, with promise for data augmentation and broader clinical adoption; future work could target enhanced fidelity and attribute precision.

title: RhythmBERT: A Self-Supervised Language Model Based on Latent Representations of ECG Waveforms for Heart Disease Detection
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis is crucial for diagnosing heart disease, but most self-supervised learning methods treat ECG as a generic time series, overlooking physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. Existing contrastive methods utilize augmentations that distort morphology, whereas generative approaches employ fixed-window segmentation, which misaligns cardiac cycles. To address these limitations, we propose RhythmBERT, a generative ECG language model that considers ECG as a language paradigm by encoding P, QRS, and T segments into symbolic tokens via autoencoder-based latent representations. These discrete tokens capture rhythm semantics, while complementary continuous embeddings retain fine-grained morphology, enabling a unified view of waveform structure and rhythm. RhythmBERT is pretrained on approximately 800,000 unlabeled ECG recordings with a masked prediction objective, allowing it to learn contextual representations in a label-efficient manner. Evaluations show that despite using only a single lead, RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines. This generalization extends from prevalent conditions such as atrial fibrillation to clinically challenging cases such as subtle ST-T abnormalities and myocardial infarction. Our results suggest that considering ECG as structured language offers a scalable and physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

AI.tldr: RhythmBERT is a generative ECG language model that tokenizes P/QRS/T segments into discrete rhythm tokens from an autoencoder latent space, while preserving continuous morphology; pretrained on ~800k unlabeled ECGs with masked prediction; achieves competitive performance with a single lead versus 12-lead baselines and generalizes to atrial fibrillation, subtle ST-T changes, and myocardial infarction.
AI.motivation: Current self-supervised ECG methods largely treat ECG as a generic time series and rely on augmentations that distort morphology, while generative methods with fixed-window segmentation misalign cardiac cycles. This overlooks physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. A language-like representation could capture rhythm semantics and waveform structure more faithfully.
AI.method: Encode ECG segments corresponding to P, QRS, and T intervals into discrete tokens via an autoencoder-based latent representation, yielding rhythm-aware symbolic tokens; retain complementary continuous embeddings for fine-grained morphology. Use RhythmBERT with a masked prediction objective, trained on ~800k unlabeled ECG recordings (single-lead input).
AI.result: RhythmBERT achieves performance comparable or superior to strong 12-lead baselines despite using only a single lead; demonstrates generalization from atrial fibrillation to subtle ST-T abnormalities and myocardial infarction.
AI.conclusion: Framing ECG as structured language with hybrid discrete-continuous representations provides a scalable, physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

title: Physics-informed neural particle flow for the Bayesian update step
summary: The Bayesian update step poses significant computational challenges in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation. While log-homotopy particle flow filters offer an alternative to stochastic sampling, existing formulations usually yield stiff differential equations. Conversely, existing deep learning approximations typically treat the update as a black-box task or rely on asymptotic relaxation, neglecting the exact geometric structure of the finite-horizon probability transport. In this work, we propose a physics-informed neural particle flow, which is an amortized inference framework. To construct the flow, we couple the log-homotopy trajectory of the prior to posterior density function with the continuity equation describing the density evolution. This derivation yields a governing partial differential equation (PDE), referred to as the master PDE. By embedding this PDE as a physical constraint into the loss function, we train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field. This approach enables purely unsupervised training, eliminating the need for ground-truth posterior samples. We demonstrate that the neural parameterization acts as an implicit regularizer, mitigating the numerical stiffness inherent to analytic flows and reducing online computational complexity. Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario confirms better mode coverage and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed neural particle flow for Bayesian updates in high dimensions; unsupervised training via a master PDE, reducing stiffness and improving mode coverage compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Bayesian update in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation is computationally challenging. Existing log-homotopy flows are stiff; DL approximations treat updates as black boxes and neglect finite-horizon transport geometry.
AI.method: Couple log-homotopy trajectory with the posterior via the continuity equation to derive a master PDE; embed this PDE as a loss constraint; train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field (amortized, unsupervised).
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on multimodal benchmarks and nonlinear scenarios, with better mode coverage, robustness, and reduced online complexity due to implicit regularization.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed neural particle flow provides an effective, unsupervised amortized inference framework that mitigates stiffness, respects finite-horizon transport, and enhances Bayesian update performance.

title: EmbodMocap: In-the-Wild 4D Human-Scene Reconstruction for Embodied Agents
summary: Human behaviors in the real world naturally encode rich, long-term contextual information that can be leveraged to train embodied agents for perception, understanding, and acting. However, existing capture systems typically rely on costly studio setups and wearable devices, limiting the large-scale collection of scene-conditioned human motion data in the wild. To address this, we propose EmbodMocap, a portable and affordable data collection pipeline using two moving iPhones. Our key idea is to jointly calibrate dual RGB-D sequences to reconstruct both humans and scenes within a unified metric world coordinate frame. The proposed method allows metric-scale and scene-consistent capture in everyday environments without static cameras or markers, bridging human motion and scene geometry seamlessly. Compared with optical capture ground truth, we demonstrate that the dual-view setting exhibits a remarkable ability to mitigate depth ambiguity, achieving superior alignment and reconstruction performance over single iphone or monocular models. Based on the collected data, we empower three embodied AI tasks: monocular human-scene-reconstruction, where we fine-tune on feedforward models that output metric-scale, world-space aligned humans and scenes; physics-based character animation, where we prove our data could be used to scale human-object interaction skills and scene-aware motion tracking; and robot motion control, where we train a humanoid robot via sim-to-real RL to replicate human motions depicted in videos. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of our pipeline and its contributions towards advancing embodied AI research.

AI.tldr: A portable two-iPhone RGB-D capture pipeline (EmbodMocap) to recover metric-scale humans and scenes in the wild, enabling downstream embodied-AI tasks.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, in-the-wild, metric-scale human-scene motion data for embodied AI; studio setups and wearables are costly and limit large-scale data collection.
AI.method: Joint calibration of two moving RGB-D sequences to reconstruct humans and scenes within a unified metric world frame, without markers or fixed cameras, using dual-view to mitigate depth ambiguity.
AI.result: Dual-view setup yields superior alignment and reconstruction compared to single iPhone/monocular models; demonstrates metric-scale, scene-consistent capture and enables downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: EmbodMocap provides a practical, scalable pipeline that fuses human motion with scene geometry, supporting monocular human-scene reconstruction, physics-based animation, and sim-to-real robot control.

title: PRAC: Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression and Memory-Efficient Training
summary: Activations have become the primary memory bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. However, existing compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations, resulting in slow convergence or limited compression. To address this, we bridge the relationship between the algorithm's fast convergence and the requirements for subspace projection, and show that an effective compression should yield an unbiased estimate of the original activation with low variance. We propose Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression (PRAC), which novelly decomposes activations into two components: a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. By introducing a precise scaling factor, we prove that PRAC yields an unbiased gradient estimator with minimum variance under certain conditions. Extensive experiments on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks demonstrate that PRAC achieves up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.

AI.tldr: PRAC introduces a spectral-structure activation compression for LLM training by decomposing activations into a principal subspace (via SVD) and a random subspace from the orthogonal complement, yielding an unbiased, low-variance gradient estimator with substantial memory savings (up to 36%) and low overhead.
AI.motivation: Activation memory is the primary bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. Existing compression methods neglect the spectral structure of activations, leading to slow convergence or limited compression. A theoretically grounded, unbiased, low-variance compression that respects the activation spectrum is needed.
AI.method: Decompose activations into two components: a principal subspace captured by SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. Introduce a precise scaling factor to ensure the gradient estimator is unbiased and variance-minimizing under certain conditions; validate theoretically and empirically.
AI.result: Demonstrates up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost in both pre-training and fine-tuning tasks.
AI.conclusion: PRAC provides an assembly that leverages the spectral structure of activations to achieve memory-efficient training for LLMs with provable unbiasedness and low variance, validated by experiments.

title: Through BrokenEyes: How Eye Disorders Impact Face Detection?
summary: Vision disorders significantly impact millions of lives, altering how visual information is processed and perceived. In this work, a computational framework was developed using the BrokenEyes system to simulate five common eye disorders: Age-related macular degeneration, cataract, glaucoma, refractive errors, and diabetic retinopathy and analyze their effects on neural-like feature representations in deep learning models. Leveraging a combination of human and non-human datasets, models trained under normal and disorder-specific conditions revealed critical disruptions in feature maps, particularly for cataract and glaucoma, which align with known neural processing challenges in these conditions. Evaluation metrics such as activation energy and cosine similarity quantified the severity of these distortions, providing insights into the interplay between degraded visual inputs and learned representations.

AI.tldr: A computational framework with the BrokenEyes simulator models five common eye disorders to study their impact on neural-like representations in DL. It uses human and non-human data, finds disruptions in feature maps especially for cataract and glaucoma, and quantifies distortion with activation energy and cosine similarity.
AI.motivation: To understand how visual impairments alter learned representations in deep networks and relate DL processing to neural/clinical vision.
AI.method: Developed a BrokenEyes-based framework; simulate five disorders (AMD, cataract, glaucoma, refractive errors, diabetic retinopathy); train models under normal and disorder-specific conditions; evaluate feature representations with activation energy and cosine similarity; combine human and non-human datasets.
AI.result: Disruptions in feature maps, notably cataract and glaucoma; distortions align with known neural processing challenges; metrics quantify severity and reveal interaction between degraded inputs and learned representations.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables quantitative assessment of how ocular disorders affect DL representations, offering insights for robust perception models and potential clinical interpretation; suggests avenues to improve model resilience to degraded vision.

title: Learning Physical Operators using Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have emerged as promising surrogate models for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), but struggle to generalise beyond training distributions and are often constrained to a fixed temporal discretisation. This work introduces a physics-informed training framework that addresses these limitations by decomposing PDEs using operator splitting methods, training separate neural operators to learn individual non-linear physical operators while approximating linear operators with fixed finite-difference convolutions. This modular mixture-of-experts architecture enables generalisation to novel physical regimes by explicitly encoding the underlying operator structure. We formulate the modelling task as a neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) where these learned operators constitute the right-hand side, enabling continuous-in-time predictions through standard ODE solvers and implicitly enforcing PDE constraints. Demonstrated on incompressible and compressible Navier-Stokes equations, our approach achieves better convergence and superior performance when generalising to unseen physics. The method remains parameter-efficient, enabling temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons, and provides interpretable components whose behaviour can be verified against known physics.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed neural operator framework with operator splitting and mixture-of-experts enabling generalization to unseen physics, continuous-time predictions via neural ODEs, and interpretable components.
AI.motivation: Neural operators struggle with generalization and fixed timesteps; need modular physics-aware learning to capture operator structure and enable continuous-time PDE solving.
AI.method: Decompose PDEs via operator splitting; train separate neural operators for nonlinear operators; approximate linear parts with fixed finite-difference convolutions; a modular mixture-of-experts architecture; formulate as a neural ODE as RHS; continuous-time predictions with standard solvers; PDE constraints enforced implicitly; tested on incompressible and compressible Navier–Stokes.
AI.result: Improved convergence and better generalization to unseen physics; parameter-efficient; temporal extrapolation beyond training horizon; interpretable components verifiable against physics.
AI.conclusion: Addresses generalization and discretization limitations of neural operators; encodes operator structure in a modular framework; shows promise for fluid dynamics and PDE families; potential for broader physics-informed operator learning.

title: Plug-and-Play Diffusion Meets ADMM: Dual-Variable Coupling for Robust Medical Image Reconstruction
summary: Plug-and-Play diffusion prior (PnPDP) frameworks have emerged as a powerful paradigm for solving imaging inverse problems by treating pretrained generative models as modular priors. However, we identify a critical flaw in prevailing PnP solvers (e.g., based on HQS or Proximal Gradient): they function as memoryless operators, updating estimates solely based on instantaneous gradients. This lack of historical tracking inevitably leads to non-vanishing steady-state bias, where the reconstruction fails to strictly satisfy physical measurements under heavy corruption. To resolve this, we propose Dual-Coupled PnP Diffusion, which restores the classical dual variable to provide integral feedback, theoretically guaranteeing asymptotic convergence to the exact data manifold. However, this rigorous geometric coupling introduces a secondary challenge: the accumulated dual residuals exhibit spectrally colored, structured artifacts that violate the Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) assumption of diffusion priors, causing severe hallucinations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Spectral Homogenization (SH), a frequency-domain adaptation mechanism that modulates these structured residuals into statistically compliant pseudo-AWGN inputs. This effectively aligns the solver's rigorous optimization trajectory with the denoiser's valid statistical manifold. Extensive experiments on CT and MRI reconstruction demonstrate that our approach resolves the bias-hallucination trade-off, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity with significantly accelerated convergence.

AI.tldr: Introduces Dual-Coupled PnP Diffusion with Spectral Homogenization to fix non-vanishing bias and hallucinations in memoryless PnP solvers, ensuring asymptotic convergence to the data manifold and achieving state-of-the-art CT/MRI reconstruction with faster convergence.
AI.motivation: Prevailing Plug-and-Play diffusion priors with memoryless updates (HQS/PG) produce persistent bias and heavy hallucinations under corruption because they lack history. A dual-variable framework provides integral feedback to enforce data-consatisfying reconstructions.
AI.method: 1) Dual coupling to reintroduce a dual variable for integral data-consistency; 2) Spectral Homogenization to shape accumulated dual residuals into pseudo-AWGN in the frequency domain, preserving the denoiser's statistical manifold; 3) Validate on CT and MRI reconstruction.
AI.result: The approach reduces the bias-hallucination trade-off, delivers state-of-the-art fidelity, and accelerates convergence compared to standard PnP diffusion methods.
AI.conclusion: Combining dual-coupled optimization with spectral homogenization yields provable asymptotic convergence to the data manifold while maintaining plausible priors, enabling high-fidelity, fast CT/MRI reconstructions.

title: Regularized Online RLHF with Generalized Bilinear Preferences
summary: We consider the problem of contextual online RLHF with general preferences, where the goal is to identify the Nash Equilibrium. We adopt the Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) to capture potentially intransitive preferences via low-rank, skew-symmetric matrices. We investigate general preference learning with any strongly convex regularizer (where $η^{-1}$ is the regularization strength), generalizing beyond prior works limited to reverse KL-regularization. Central to our analysis is proving that the dual gap of the greedy policy is bounded by the square of the estimation error - a result derived solely from strong convexity and the skew-symmetricity of GBPM.Building on this insight and a feature diversity assumption, we establish two regret bounds via two simple algorithms: (1) Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, $e^{O(η)}$-free regret $\tilde{O}(ηd^4 (\log T)^2)$. (2) Explore-Then-Commit achieves $\mathrm{poly}(d)$-free regret $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{ηr T})$ by exploiting the low-rank structure; this is the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high-dimensions.

AI.tldr: This work introduces a Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) for contextual online RLHF with general, potentially intransitive preferences and proves that the dual gap of a greedy policy is bounded by the squared estimation error under strong convexity and skew-symmetry. It develops two simple algorithms with regret guarantees: Greedy Sampling (polylogarithmic, η-free regret) and Explore-Then-Commit (poly(d)-free regret ~ O~(sqrt(η r T)), leveraging low-rank structure; marking the first statistically efficient high-dimensional online RLHF guarantees.
AI.motivation: To identify Nash Equilibria in contextual online RLHF under general (possibly intransitive) preferences, extending beyond reverse KL regularization and enabling high-dimensional, efficient learning via a low-rank, skew-symmetric preference representation.
AI.method: Adopt GBPM with low-rank skew-symmetric matrices to capture general preferences. Prove a dual gap bound for the greedy policy using only strong convexity and skew-symmetry. Under a feature diversity assumption, analyze two algorithms: (1) Greedy Sampling with polylogarithmic regret, η-free; (2) Explore-Then-Commit with regret ~ tilde O(d^c sqrt(η r T)) leveraging low-rank structure.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the dual gap is bounded by the square of the estimation error. Provides two regret guarantees: Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, η-free regret tilde O(η d^4 (log T)^2); Explore-Then-Commit achieves tilde O(poly(d) sqrt(η r T)) regret, marking the first statistically efficient online RLHF guarantee in high dimensions.
AI.conclusion: GBPM with strong convexity and skew-symmetricity enables stable dual-gap behavior. Two simple, scalable algorithms yield provable, dimension-aware regret bounds, advancing theoretical guarantees for online RLHF with general preferences in high-dimensional settings.

title: Multidimensional Task Learning: A Unified Tensor Framework for Computer Vision Tasks
summary: This paper introduces Multidimensional Task Learning (MTL), a unified mathematical framework based on Generalized Einstein MLPs (GE-MLPs) that operate directly on tensors via the Einstein product. We argue that current computer vision task formulations are inherently constrained by matrix-based thinking: standard architectures rely on matrix-valued weights and vectorvalued biases, requiring structural flattening that restricts the space of naturally expressible tasks. GE-MLPs lift this constraint by operating with tensor-valued parameters, enabling explicit control over which dimensions are preserved or contracted without information loss. Through rigorous mathematical derivations, we demonstrate that classification, segmentation, and detection are special cases of MTL, differing only in their dimensional configuration within a formally defined task space. We further prove that this task space is strictly larger than what matrix-based formulations can natively express, enabling principled task configurations such as spatiotemporal or cross modal predictions that require destructive flattening under conventional approaches. This work provides a mathematical foundation for understanding, comparing, and designing computer vision tasks through the lens of tensor algebra.

AI.tldr: MTL generalizes neural tasks using GE-MLPs on tensors via the Einstein product, expanding the task space beyond matrix-based models and preserving multi-dimensional structure. Classification, segmentation, and detection are unified as task configurations within this framework; enables spatiotemporal and cross-modal predictions without flattening.
AI.motivation: Matrix-based CV models require flattening or vectorization, which constrains the space of expressible tasks. A tensor-centric framework aims to preserve structure and enable richer task formulations.
AI.method: Introduce Generalized Einstein MLPs (GE-MLPs) that operate on tensors with tensor-valued parameters using the Einstein product. Formally define a task space and show that common CV tasks are special cases distinguished by dimensional configurations; prove that the tensor-valued task space strictly contains the matrix-based one.
AI.result: A theoretical framework that (i) unifies CV tasks under a tensor-algebraic formulation, (ii) demonstrates that standard tasks are special cases of a broader task space, and (iii) asserts the possibility of designing tasks like spatiotemporal or cross-modal predictions without destructive flattening.
AI.conclusion: Tensor-based task design via GE-MLPs provides a principled mathematical foundation for understanding, comparing, and designing computer vision tasks. The framework expands the expressiveness of task configurations beyond traditional matrix-based architectures.

title: Bound to Disagree: Generalization Bounds via Certifiable Surrogates
summary: Generalization bounds for deep learning models are typically vacuous, not computable or restricted to specific model classes. In this paper, we tackle these issues by providing new disagreement-based certificates for the gap between the true risk of any two predictors. We then bound the true risk of the predictor of interest via a surrogate model that enjoys tight generalization guarantees, and evaluating our disagreement bound on an unlabeled dataset. We empirically demonstrate the tightness of the obtained certificates and showcase the versatility of the approach by training surrogate models leveraging three different frameworks: sample compression, model compression and PAC-Bayes theory. Importantly, such guarantees are achieved without modifying the target model, nor adapting the training procedure to the generalization framework.

AI.tldr: Disagreement-based generalization certificates bound the true risk gap between predictors without modifying the target model; uses a surrogate with strong generalization guarantees and unlabeled data; demonstrated across three surrogate frameworks (sample compression, model compression, PAC-Bayes) with empirical tightness.
AI.motivation: Generalization bounds for deep learning are often vacuous, non-computable, or restricted to specific models. There is a need for practical, broadly applicable certificates that do not alter training.
AI.method: Develop disagreement-based certificates for the gap between true risks of any two predictors; bound the risk of the predictor of interest via a surrogate model with tight generalization guarantees; evaluate the disagreement bound on an unlabeled dataset; instantiate surrogate models using three frameworks: sample compression, model compression, and PAC-Bayes.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrate that the certificates are tight; show the approach works across three surrogate frameworks; guarantees are achieved without modifying the target model or training procedure and can be evaluated on unlabeled data.
AI.conclusion: The work provides non-invasive, generalizable generalization certificates for deep learning, enabling tighter risk bounds via surrogate models and unlabeled data, with broad applicability across different surrogate paradigms.

title: DyGnROLE: Modeling Asymmetry in Dynamic Graphs with Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding
summary: Real-world dynamic graphs are often directed, with source and destination nodes exhibiting asymmetrical behavioral patterns and temporal dynamics. However, existing dynamic graph architectures largely rely on shared parameters for processing source and destination nodes, with limited or no systematic role-aware modeling. We propose DyGnROLE (Dynamic Graph Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding), a transformer-based architecture that explicitly disentangles source and destination representations. By using separate embedding vocabularies and role-semantic positional encodings, the model captures the distinct structural and temporal contexts unique to each role. Critical to the effectiveness of these specialized embeddings in low-label regimes is a self-supervised pretraining objective we introduce: Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP). The pretraining uses the full unlabeled interaction history to encode informative structural biases, enabling the model to learn role-specific representations without requiring annotated data. Evaluation on future edge classification demonstrates that DyGnROLE substantially outperforms a diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines, establishing role-aware modeling as an effective strategy for dynamic graph learning.

AI.tldr: A role-aware, transformer-based framework DyGnROLE for directed dynamic graphs that disentangles source/destination roles with separate embeddings and role-aware positional encodings, pretrained via Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP); achieves superior future edge classification.
AI.motivation: Directed dynamic graphs exhibit asymmetric behavior between source and destination nodes and complex temporal dynamics. Shared-parameter architectures fail to capture role-specific structural/temporal contexts, especially in low-label regimes. A role-aware approach with self-supervised pretraining can learn meaningful representations from unlabeled history.
AI.method: DyGnROLE uses separate embedding vocabularies for source and destination roles and role-semantic positional encodings within a transformer framework. It introduces Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP) as a self-supervised pretraining objective that leverages full interaction histories to learn role-specific representations without labels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on future edge classification shows DyGnROLE substantially outperforms a diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines, validating the effectiveness of role-aware modeling for dynamic graphs.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling source/destination roles and leveraging TCLP pretraining yields stronger, low-label-friendly dynamic graph representations, establishing role-aware modeling as a beneficial strategy for dynamic graph learning.

title: MovieTeller: Tool-augmented Movie Synopsis with ID Consistent Progressive Abstraction
summary: With the explosive growth of digital entertainment, automated video summarization has become indispensable for applications such as content indexing, personalized recommendation, and efficient media archiving. Automatic synopsis generation for long-form videos, such as movies and TV series, presents a significant challenge for existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs). While proficient at single-image captioning, these general-purpose models often exhibit critical failures in long-duration contexts, primarily a lack of ID-consistent character identification and a fractured narrative coherence. To overcome these limitations, we propose MovieTeller, a novel framework for generating movie synopses via tool-augmented progressive abstraction. Our core contribution is a training-free, tool-augmented, fact-grounded generation process. Instead of requiring costly model fine-tuning, our framework directly leverages off-the-shelf models in a plug-and-play manner. We first invoke a specialized face recognition model as an external "tool" to establish Factual Groundings--precise character identities and their corresponding bounding boxes. These groundings are then injected into the prompt to steer the VLM's reasoning, ensuring the generated scene descriptions are anchored to verifiable facts. Furthermore, our progressive abstraction pipeline decomposes the summarization of a full-length movie into a multi-stage process, effectively mitigating the context length limitations of current VLMs. Experiments demonstrate that our approach yields significant improvements in factual accuracy, character consistency, and overall narrative coherence compared to end-to-end baselines.

AI.tldr: A training-free, tool-augmented framework (MovieTeller) for long-form video summarization that grounds character identities via an external face-recognition tool and uses progressive abstraction to produce coherent, factually grounded movie synopses without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models struggle with long-form summaries due to lack of ID-consistency and narrative coherence; a method is needed to maintain factual grounding and handle long contexts without expensive fine-tuning.
AI.method: A plug-and-play pipeline: invoke an external face-recognition tool to obtain factual groundings (character identities and bounding boxes); inject these groundings into prompts to steer the VLM's reasoning; apply a progressive abstraction pipeline to decompose full-movie summarization into multi-stage steps, mitigating context-length limits.
AI.result: Experimental results show improvements in factual accuracy, character consistency, and narrative coherence compared with end-to-end baselines.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented, training-free abstraction is effective for long-form movie summarization, enabling reliable, coherent synopses without requiring model fine-tuning.

title: Prediction of Diffusion Coefficients in Mixtures with Tensor Completion
summary: Predicting diffusion coefficients in mixtures is crucial for many applications, as experimental data remain scarce, and machine learning (ML) offers promising alternatives to established semi-empirical models. Among ML models, matrix completion methods (MCMs) have proven effective in predicting thermophysical properties, including diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures. However, MCMs are restricted to single-temperature predictions, and their accuracy depends strongly on the availability of high-quality experimental data for each temperature of interest. In this work, we address this challenge by presenting a hybrid tensor completion method (TCM) for predicting temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary mixtures. The TCM employs a Tucker decomposition and is jointly trained on experimental data for diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary systems at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Predictions from the semi-empirical SEGWE model serve as prior knowledge within a Bayesian training framework. The TCM then extrapolates linearly to any temperature between 268 K and 378 K, achieving markedly improved prediction accuracy compared to established models across all studied temperatures. To further enhance predictive performance, the experimental database was expanded using active learning (AL) strategies for targeted acquisition of new diffusion data by pulsed-field gradient (PFG) NMR measurements. Diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in 19 solute + solvent systems were measured at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Incorporating these results yields a substantial improvement in the TCM's predictive accuracy. These findings highlight the potential of combining data-efficient ML methods with adaptive experimentation to advance predictive modeling of transport properties.

AI.tldr: A hybrid Tucker-based tensor completion method (TCM) predicts temperature-dependent infinite-dilution diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures by fusing experimental data at 298–333 K with SEGWE priors in a Bayesian framework; linear temperature extrapolation (268–378 K); further improved by active-learning–driven data expansion via PFG NMR, showing superior accuracy over traditional models.
AI.motivation: To overcome the temperature-limited capability of matrix completion methods and data scarcity for diffusion data, enabling accurate, temperature-spanning predictions with limited experiments.
AI.method: Joint Tucker-structured tensor completion trained on diffusion at 298, 313, 333 K; prior incorporated from SEGWE via Bayesian training; linear extrapolation to other temperatures; active learning to select new diffusion measurements; PFG NMR data for 19 solute–solvent systems to expand database.
AI.result: Significant accuracy improvements across studied temperatures; successful temperature extrapolation; added experimental data via AL further improves predictions.
AI.conclusion: Combines data-efficient ML with adaptive experiments to advance transport-property modeling; potential generalization to other transport coefficients and properties with limited data.

title: Large Multimodal Models as General In-Context Classifiers
comment: CVPR Findings 2026. Project website at https://circle-lmm.github.io/
summary: Which multimodal model should we use for classification? Previous studies suggest that the answer lies in CLIP-like contrastive Vision-Language Models (VLMs), due to their remarkable performance in zero-shot classification. In contrast, Large Multimodal Models (LMM) are more suitable for complex tasks. In this work, we argue that this answer overlooks an important capability of LMMs: in-context learning. We benchmark state-of-the-art LMMs on diverse datasets for closed-world classification and find that, although their zero-shot performance is lower than CLIP's, LMMs with a few in-context examples can match or even surpass contrastive VLMs with cache-based adapters, their "in-context" equivalent. We extend this analysis to the open-world setting, where the generative nature of LMMs makes them more suitable for the task. In this challenging scenario, LMMs struggle whenever provided with imperfect context information. To address this issue, we propose CIRCLE, a simple training-free method that assigns pseudo-labels to in-context examples, iteratively refining them with the available context itself. Through extensive experiments, we show that CIRCLE establishes a robust baseline for open-world classification, surpassing VLM counterparts and highlighting the potential of LMMs to serve as unified classifiers, and a flexible alternative to specialized models.

AI.tldr: LMMs with in-context learning can match or exceed CLIP-like VLMs for classification, especially in open-world settings when combined with a training-free pseudo-label refinement method (CIRCLE); they offer a unified, flexible alternative to specialized models.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether CLIP-like contrastive VLMs or Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) provide superior classification performance, with a focus on in-context learning in LMMs and the open-world setting where context quality varies.
AI.method: Benchmark state-of-the-art LMMs on diverse closed-world datasets with varying in-context examples; compare to CLIP-like VLMs; analyze open-world performance; propose CIRCLE, a training-free method that assigns pseudo-labels to in-context examples and iteratively refines them using available context; conduct extensive experiments.
AI.result: In closed-world, LMMs with a few in-context examples can match or surpass CLIP-like VLMs with cache-based adapters. In open-world, LMMs outperform VLMs when context is accurate, but struggle with imperfect context. CIRCLE provides a robust training-free baseline for open-world classification, surpassing VLM counterparts and highlighting LMMs as unified classifiers.
AI.conclusion: LMMs are a viable, flexible alternative to specialized models for both closed- and open-world classification. The CIRCLE method enhances their robustness in open-world settings, underscoring the potential of LMMs as unified classifiers.

title: Partial recovery of meter-scale surface weather
summary: Near-surface atmospheric conditions can differ sharply over tens to hundreds of meters due to land cover and topography, yet this variability is absent from current weather analyses and forecasts. It is unclear whether such meter-scale variability reflects irreducibly chaotic dynamics or contains a component predictable from surface characteristics and large-scale atmospheric forcing. Here we show that a substantial, physically coherent component of meter-scale near-surface weather is statistically recoverable from existing observations. By conditioning coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface station measurements and high-resolution Earth observation data, we infer spatially continuous fields of near-surface wind, temperature, and humidity at 10 m resolution across the contiguous United States. Relative to ERA5, the inferred fields reduce wind error by 29% and temperature and dewpoint error by 6%, while explaining substantially more spatial variance at fixed time steps. They also exhibit physically interpretable structure, including urban heat islands, evapotranspiration-driven humidity contrasts, and wind speed differences across land cover types. Our findings expand the frontier of weather modeling by demonstrating a computationally feasible approach to continental-scale meter-resolution inference. More broadly, they illustrate how conditioning coarse dynamical models on static fine-scale features can reveal previously unresolved components of the Earth system.

AI.tldr: Meter-scale near-surface weather variability can be statistically recovered at continental scale by conditioning coarse atmospheric state on surface measurements and high-res satellite data, yielding 10 m fields that outperform ERA5 in wind and temperature/dewpoint accuracy and reveal interpretable land-surface structure.
AI.motivation: Address sub-grid, meter-scale heterogeneity in near-surface conditions caused by land cover and topography, and determine whether it is chaotic or predictable from surface and large-scale forcing to improve weather analyses/forecasts.
AI.method: Condition coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface observations and high-resolution Earth observations to infer spatially continuous fields of wind, temperature, and humidity at 10 m across the contiguous US; compare against ERA5 for error reduction and variance explained; analyze physical structures.
AI.result: Inferred 10 m fields reduce wind error by 29% and temperature/dewpoint error by 6% relative to ERA5; explain more spatial variance at fixed time steps; show urban heat islands, humidity contrasts driven by evapotranspiration, and land-cover dependent wind differences.
AI.conclusion: A computationally feasible approach to continental-scale meter-resolution inference is demonstrated, illustrating how conditioning coarse dynamical models on fine-scale static features reveals previously unresolved components of the Earth system.

title: Skarimva: Skeleton-based Action Recognition is a Multi-view Application
summary: Human action recognition plays an important role when developing intelligent interactions between humans and machines. While there is a lot of active research on improving the machine learning algorithms for skeleton-based action recognition, not much attention has been given to the quality of the input skeleton data itself. This work demonstrates that by making use of multiple camera views to triangulate more accurate 3D~skeletons, the performance of state-of-the-art action recognition models can be improved significantly. This suggests that the quality of the input data is currently a limiting factor for the performance of these models. Based on these results, it is argued that the cost-benefit ratio of using multiple cameras is very favorable in most practical use-cases, therefore future research in skeleton-based action recognition should consider multi-view applications as the standard setup.

AI.tldr: Multi-view triangulation improves 3D skeleton quality, boosting skeleton-based action recognition; data quality is a bottleneck, advocating multi-view setups as standard.
AI.motivation: Argues input data quality limits current models; better 3D skeletons via triangulation lead to significant performance gains, motivating multi-view data collection.
AI.method: Use multiple camera views to triangulate more accurate 3D skeletons and feed into state-of-the-art action recognition models.
AI.result: Significant performance improvements of state-of-the-art models when using higher-quality 3D skeletons from multi-view triangulation.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view setups are cost-effective in most practical uses; future work should adopt multi-view skeleton data as the standard in skeleton-based action recognition.

title: Benchmarking Temporal Web3 Intelligence: Lessons from the FinSurvival 2025 Challenge
summary: Temporal Web analytics increasingly relies on large-scale, longitudinal data to understand how users, content, and systems evolve over time. A rapidly growing frontier is the \emph{Temporal Web3}: decentralized platforms whose behavior is recorded as immutable, time-stamped event streams. Despite the richness of this data, the field lacks shared, reproducible benchmarks that capture real-world temporal dynamics, specifically censoring and non-stationarity, across extended horizons. This absence slows methodological progress and limits the transfer of techniques between Web3 and broader Web domains. In this paper, we present the \textit{FinSurvival Challenge 2025} as a case study in benchmarking \emph{temporal Web3 intelligence}. Using 21.8 million transaction records from the Aave v3 protocol, the challenge operationalized 16 survival prediction tasks to model user behavior transitions.We detail the benchmark design and the winning solutions, highlighting how domain-aware temporal feature construction significantly outperformed generic modeling approaches. Furthermore, we distill lessons for next-generation temporal benchmarks, arguing that Web3 systems provide a high-fidelity sandbox for studying temporal challenges, such as churn, risk, and evolution that are fundamental to the wider Web.

AI.tldr: A FinSurvival Challenge 2025 benchmark on 21.8M Aave v3 transactions, with 16 survival tasks. Domain-aware temporal features beat generic models, highlighting the value of temporal Web3 benchmarks for churn, risk, and evolution theory in Web3 and beyond.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of shared, reproducible benchmarks that capture real-world temporal dynamics (censoring, non-stationarity) in Web3 data, enabling methodological progress and transfer to broader Web domains.
AI.method: Design and run 16 survival-prediction tasks on a large-scale Web3 transaction dataset (Aave v3). Develop domain-aware temporal features, compare against generic models, and analyze results to derive benchmark design lessons. Include reporting of winning solutions.
AI.result: Domain-aware temporal feature construction significantly outperforms generic modeling approaches across tasks. The benchmark demonstrates feasibility, provides productive baselines, and yields insights on temporal dynamics, offering a blueprint for next-generation temporal benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Web3 data provide a high-fidelity sandbox for studying temporal challenges (churn, risk, evolution). The FinSurvival Challenge 2025 offers a pathway to reproducible, domain-appropriate benchmarks that can transfer to broader Web analytics.

title: Spatio-Temporal Token Pruning for Efficient High-Resolution GUI Agents
summary: Pure-vision GUI agents provide universal interaction capabilities but suffer from severe efficiency bottlenecks due to the massive spatiotemporal redundancy inherent in high-resolution screenshots and historical trajectories. We identify two critical misalignments in existing compression paradigms: the temporal mismatch, where uniform history encoding diverges from the agent's "fading memory" attention pattern, and the spatial topology conflict, where unstructured pruning compromises the grid integrity required for precise coordinate grounding, inducing spatial hallucinations. To address these challenges, we introduce GUIPruner, a training-free framework tailored for high-resolution GUI navigation.
