It synergizes Temporal-Adaptive Resolution (TAR), which eliminates historical redundancy via decay-based resizing, and Stratified Structure-aware Pruning (SSP), which prioritizes interactive foregrounds and semantic anchors while safeguarding global layout. Extensive evaluations across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that GUIPruner consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, effectively preventing the collapse observed in large-scale models under high compression. Notably, on Qwen2-VL-2B, our method delivers a 3.4x reduction in FLOPs and a 3.3x speedup in vision encoding latency while retaining over 94% of the original performance, enabling real-time, high-precision navigation with minimal resource consumption.

AI.tldr: GUIPruner is a training-free framework for high-resolution GUI navigation that reduces compute via Temporal-Adaptive Resolution (TAR) and Stratified Structure-aware Pruning (SSP) while preserving most performance.
AI.motivation: High-resolution GUI agents suffer from spatiotemporal redundancy and misalignment between compression and the agent's fading-memory attention, causing inefficiency and spatial hallucinations; a training-free, structure-aware approach is needed for real-time GUI navigation.
AI.method: Introduce TAR to decay-based resizing to remove historical redundancy, and SSP to prune according to interactive foregrounds and semantic anchors while preserving global layout; no additional training required. Extensive benchmarks, including large multimodal models, demonstrate efficiency gains.
AI.result: Significant reductions in computation and latency (e.g., 3.4x FLOPs reduction and 3.3x vision-encoding latency speedup on Qwen2-VL-2B) with retention of over 94% of original performance, achieving state-of-the-art efficiency and stability under high compression.
AI.conclusion: GUIPruner enables real-time, high-precision high-resolution GUI navigation with minimal resource usage, illustrating that training-free, structure-aware compression can prevent model collapse under aggressive pruning and encoding reductions.

title: MetaOthello: A Controlled Study of Multiple World Models in Transformers
summary: Foundation models must handle multiple generative processes, yet mechanistic interpretability largely studies capabilities in isolation; it remains unclear how a single transformer organizes multiple, potentially conflicting "world models". Previous experiments on Othello playing neural-networks test world-model learning but focus on a single game with a single set of rules. We introduce MetaOthello, a controlled suite of Othello variants with shared syntax but different rules or tokenizations, and train small GPTs on mixed-variant data to study how multiple world models are organized in a shared representation space. We find that transformers trained on mixed-game data do not partition their capacity into isolated sub-models; instead, they converge on a mostly shared board-state representation that transfers causally across variants. Linear probes trained on one variant can intervene on another's internal state with effectiveness approaching that of matched probes. For isomorphic games with token remapping, representations are equivalent up to a single orthogonal rotation that generalizes across layers. When rules partially overlap, early layers maintain game-agnostic representations while a middle layer identifies game identity, and later layers specialize. MetaOthello offers a path toward understanding not just whether transformers learn world models, but how they organize many at once.

AI.tldr: Transformers trained on mixed Othello variants develop a largely shared board-state representation that transfers across variants, rather than isolated sub-models; their representations can be aligned by a single orthogonal rotation, and layer-wise specialization emerges when rules partially overlap.
AI.motivation: To understand how a single transformer can encode and access multiple world models simultaneously, addressing mechanistic interpretability beyond single-task settings by systematically comparing related game variants.
AI.method: Introduce MetaOthello: a controlled suite of Othello variants with shared syntax but differing rules/tokenizations. Train small GPTs on mixed-variant data. Analyze representational geometry across layers, use linear probes to intervene on internal states, test isomorphic variants with token remapping, and study partial-rule overlap to map where game identity is captured.
AI.result: 1) A shared, causally transferable board-state representation emerges across variants; 2) Linear probes on one variant can influence internal states of another; 3) Isomorphic variants with token remapping produce representations equivalent up to a single orthogonal rotation that generalizes across layers; 4) Partially overlapping rules yield a progression from agnostic early layers to identity-detecting middle layers and then game-specific later layers.
AI.conclusion: MetaOthello demonstrates that transformers can organize multiple world models within a shared representation space, with simple relational transformations (e.g., rotations) linking variants. This provides a concrete framework to study how neural networks encode and partition multiple, potentially conflicting world-models.

title: Induction Meets Biology: Mechanisms of Repeat Detection in Protein Language Models
summary: Protein sequences are abundant in repeating segments, both as exact copies and as approximate segments with mutations. These repeats are important for protein structure and function, motivating decades of algorithmic work on repeat identification. Recent work has shown that protein language models (PLMs) identify repeats, by examining their behavior in masked-token prediction. To elucidate their internal mechanisms, we investigate how PLMs detect both exact and approximate repeats. We find that the mechanism for approximate repeats functionally subsumes that of exact repeats. We then characterize this mechanism, revealing two main stages: PLMs first build feature representations using both general positional attention heads and biologically specialized components, such as neurons that encode amino-acid similarity. Then, induction heads attend to aligned tokens across repeated segments, promoting the correct answer. Our results reveal how PLMs solve this biological task by combining language-based pattern matching with specialized biological knowledge, thereby establishing a basis for studying more complex evolutionary processes in PLMs.

AI.tldr: PLMs detect exact and approximate protein repeats via a two-stage mechanism: first, they build feature representations through general positional attention heads plus biology-specific components (e.g., amino-acid similarity neurons); then, induction heads attend to aligned tokens across repeats to produce the correct prediction. The approximate-repeat mechanism subsumes the exact-repeat mechanism, revealing an integration of language-pattern matching with domain-specific biological knowledge.
AI.motivation: Understand the internal mechanisms by which protein language models identify repeats, and how biology-specific priors are incorporated, to improve interpretability and to inform studies of PLM evolution.
AI.method: Provocation/analysis of masked-token prediction in PLMs on repeat-containing protein sequences, analyzing attention heads and neurons. Identifies two-stage process: (i) feature representation construction via generic positional heads and amino-acid similarity neurons; (ii) induction-head–mediated alignment across repeated segments to produce predictions.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the approximate-repeat mechanism functionally subsumes the exact-repeat mechanism; reveals a two-stage process with specialized neurons and induction heads that align repeats, showing PLMs leverage both language-based pattern matching and biological knowledge.
AI.conclusion: PLMs solve the biological task by integrating language-structure priors with domain-specific components, providing a framework to study evolutionary-like dynamics in PLMs and to extend analysis to more complex biological sequences.

title: Decomposing Private Image Generation via Coarse-to-Fine Wavelet Modeling
summary: Generative models trained on sensitive image datasets risk memorizing and reproducing individual training examples, making strong privacy guarantees essential. While differential privacy (DP) provides a principled framework for such guarantees, standard DP finetuning (e.g., with DP-SGD) often results in severe degradation of image quality, particularly in high-frequency textures, due to the indiscriminate addition of noise across all model parameters. In this work, we propose a spectral DP framework based on the hypothesis that the most privacy-sensitive portions of an image are often low-frequency components in the wavelet space (e.g., facial features and object shapes) while high-frequency components are largely generic and public. Based on this hypothesis, we propose the following two-stage framework for DP image generation with coarse image intermediaries: (1) DP finetune an autoregressive spectral image tokenizer model on the low-resolution wavelet coefficients of the sensitive images, and (2) perform high-resolution upsampling using a publicly pretrained super-resolution model. By restricting the privacy budget to the global structures of the image in the first stage, and leveraging the post-processing property of DP for detail refinement, we achieve promising trade-offs between privacy and utility. Experiments on the MS-COCO and MM-CelebA-HQ datasets show that our method generates images with improved quality and style capture relative to other leading DP image frameworks.

AI.tldr: Two-stage DP image generation via spectral decomposition: DP finetuning on low-frequency wavelet coefficients followed by public super-resolution, focusing privacy budget on coarse structures to improve image quality while providing privacy guarantees.
AI.motivation: Standard DP fine-tuning degrades high-frequency textures; wavelet-based separation allows targeted privacy on sensitive low-frequency content; leverage DP post-processing for details.
AI.method: Stage 1: DP-finetune an autoregressive spectral image tokenizer on low-res wavelet coefficients of sensitive images. Stage 2: upsample high-res with a publicly pretrained super-resolution model. Privacy budget allocated to low-frequency components; DP post-processing for refinement.
AI.result: Improved image quality and style capture compared to leading DP image generation methods on MS-COCO and MM-CelebA-HQ; better privacy-utility trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Spectral DP with coarse intermediaries is a promising approach to balance privacy and utility in generative imaging, leveraging wavelet-domain separation and post-processing DP.

title: Closing the gap on tabular data with Fourier and Implicit Categorical Features
summary: While Deep Learning has demonstrated impressive results in applications on various data types, it continues to lag behind tree-based methods when applied to tabular data, often referred to as the last "unconquered castle" for neural networks. We hypothesize that a significant advantage of tree-based methods lies in their intrinsic capability to model and exploit non-linear interactions induced by features with categorical characteristics. In contrast, neural-based methods exhibit biases toward uniform numerical processing of features and smooth solutions, making it challenging for them to effectively leverage such patterns. We address this performance gap by using statistical-based feature processing techniques to identify features that are strongly correlated with the target once discretized. We further mitigate the bias of deep models for overly-smooth solutions, a bias that does not align with the inherent properties of the data, using Learned Fourier. We show that our proposed feature preprocessing significantly boosts the performance of deep learning models and enables them to achieve a performance that closely matches or surpasses XGBoost on a comprehensive tabular data benchmark.

AI.tldr: Statistical feature preprocessing and Learned Fourier bias mitigation enable deep models to approach or surpass XGBoost on tabular data by better exploiting non-linear interactions from categorical features.
AI.motivation: Tree-based methods outperform deep learning on tabular data due to their ability to naturally model non-linear interactions from categorical features; deep models are biased toward uniform numerical processing and overly smooth solutions, causing a performance gap.
AI.method: Apply statistical preprocessing to identify features that are strongly correlated with the target after discretization; incorporate Learned Fourier to mitigate the bias toward overly-smooth functions in deep models; evaluate on a comprehensive tabular data benchmark and compare to XGBoost.
AI.result: Deep learning models with the proposed preprocessing and Learned Fourier achieve significant performance gains, reaching performance levels that closely match or surpass XGBoost on a broad tabular data benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Targeted feature preprocessing plus spectral-bias mitigation can close the performance gap between deep learning and tree-based methods on tabular data, demonstrating the viability of neural networks for such tasks when tailored to exploit feature interactions.

title: LineGraph2Road: Structural Graph Reasoning on Line Graphs for Road Network Extraction
summary: The accurate and automatic extraction of roads from satellite imagery is critical for applications in navigation and urban planning, significantly reducing the need for manual annotation. Many existing methods decompose this task into keypoint extraction and connectedness prediction, but often struggle to capture long-range dependencies and complex topologies. Here, we propose LineGraph2Road, a framework that improves connectedness prediction by formulating it as binary classification over edges in a constructed global but sparse Euclidean graph, where nodes are keypoints extracted from segmentation masks and edges connect node pairs within a predefined distance threshold, representing potential road segments. To better learn structural link representation, we transform the original graph into its corresponding line graph and apply a Graph Transformer on it for connectedness prediction. This formulation overcomes the limitations of endpoint-embedding fusion on set-isomorphic links, enabling rich link representations and effective relational reasoning over the global structure. Additionally, we introduce an overpass/underpass head to resolve multi-level crossings and a coupled NMS strategy to preserve critical connections. We evaluate LineGraph2Road on three benchmarks: City-scale, SpaceNet, and Global-scale, and show that it achieves state-of-the-art results on two key metrics, TOPO-F1 and APLS. It also captures fine visual details critical for real-world deployment. We will make our code publicly available.

AI.tldr: LineGraph2Road reframes road connectivity estimation as binary edge classification on a global sparse graph of keypoint-derived nodes, using a line-graph transformation and Graph Transformer to capture long-range dependencies and complex topology, with overpass/underpass handling and coupled NMS; achieves state-of-the-art on TOPO-F1 and APLS on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Accurate road extraction from satellite imagery is essential for navigation and planning but existing methods relying on endpoint fusion struggle with long-range dependencies and topological complexity; need global relational reasoning for robust connectivity.
AI.method: Construct a global sparse Euclidean graph from segmentation-keypoint nodes; connect pairs within a distance threshold; convert to its line graph; apply a Graph Transformer for edge (connectedness) prediction; add an overpass/underpass head to resolve multi-level crossings; use coupled NMS to preserve critical connections; code to be released.
AI.result: Evaluated on City-scale, SpaceNet, and Global-scale benchmarks; achieves state-of-the-art TOPO-F1 and APLS on two metrics; retains fine visual details; demonstrates improved connectivity predictions.
AI.conclusion: The approach overcomes endpoint-embedding fusion limitations, enabling rich link representations and relational reasoning over global structure; promising for real-world deployment; open-source release planned.

title: Efficient Real-Time Adaptation of ROMs for Unsteady Flows Using Data Assimilation
summary: We propose an efficient retraining strategy for a parameterized Reduced Order Model (ROM) that attains accuracy comparable to full retraining while requiring only a fraction of the computational time and relying solely on sparse observations of the full system. The architecture employs an encode-process-decode structure: a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to perform dimensionality reduction, and a transformer network to evolve the latent states and model the dynamics. The ROM is parameterized by an external control variable, the Reynolds number in the Navier-Stokes setting, with the transformer exploiting attention mechanisms to capture both temporal dependencies and parameter effects. The probabilistic VAE enables stochastic sampling of trajectory ensembles, providing predictive means and uncertainty quantification through the first two moments. After initial training on a limited set of dynamical regimes, the model is adapted to out-of-sample parameter regions using only sparse data. Its probabilistic formulation naturally supports ensemble generation, which we employ within an ensemble Kalman filtering framework to assimilate data and reconstruct full-state trajectories from minimal observations. We further show that, for the dynamical system considered, the dominant source of error in out-of-sample forecasts stems from distortions of the latent manifold rather than changes in the latent dynamics. Consequently, retraining can be limited to the autoencoder, allowing for a lightweight, computationally efficient, real-time adaptation procedure with very sparse fine-tuning data.

AI.tldr: An efficient, data-efficient ROM retraining approach combining VAE-based compression with transformer-based latent dynamics, parameterized by Reynolds number, enabling fast adaptation to new regimes with sparse data; uses ensemble Kalman filtering for data assimilation and shows latent-manifold distortions, not latent dynamics, drive out-of-sample error; retraining focused on the autoencoder for real-time updates.
AI.motivation: To achieve accurate, uncertainty-aware ROMs for parametric fluid dynamics with minimal retraining cost and sparse measurements, enabling real-time adaptation across unseen Reynolds numbers.
AI.method: An encode-process-decode pipeline: a VAE for dimensionality reduction, a transformer for evolving latent states and capturing parametric effects via attention; parameterization by Reynolds number; probabilistic sampling for ensembles; data assimilation via ensemble Kalman filtering to reconstruct full-state trajectories from sparse observations; initial training on limited regimes; targeted, lightweight retraining of the autoencoder when adapting to new parameters; uncertainty quantification via first two moments of the latent/traj samples.
AI.result: The framework yields accuracy comparable to full retraining at a fraction of computational cost; provides predictive means and uncertainty (via first two moments); enables ensemble generation for data assimilation; identifies latent-manifold distortions as the dominant source of out-of-sample error; retraining can be limited to the autoencoder for real-time adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers a computationally efficient, uncertainty-aware pathway for parameterized ROMs with sparse data, enabling rapid out-of-sample adaptation by focusing retraining on the autoencoder; applicable to parametric dynamical systems where latent-manifold distortions govern out-of-sample errors.

title: PGVMS: A Prompt-Guided Unified Framework for Virtual Multiplex IHC Staining with Pathological Semantic Learning
summary: Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining enables precise molecular profiling of protein expression, with over 200 clinically available antibody-based tests in modern pathology. However, comprehensive IHC analysis is frequently limited by insufficient tissue quantities in small biopsies. Therefore, virtual multiplex staining emerges as an innovative solution to digitally transform H&E images into multiple IHC representations, yet current methods still face three critical challenges: (1) inadequate semantic guidance for multi-staining, (2) inconsistent distribution of immunochemistry staining, and (3) spatial misalignment across different stain modalities. To overcome these limitations, we present a prompt-guided framework for virtual multiplex IHC staining using only uniplex training data (PGVMS). Our framework introduces three key innovations corresponding to each challenge: First, an adaptive prompt guidance mechanism employing a pathological visual language model dynamically adjusts staining prompts to resolve semantic guidance limitations (Challenge 1). Second, our protein-aware learning strategy (PALS) maintains precise protein expression patterns by direct quantification and constraint of protein distributions (Challenge 2). Third, the prototype-consistent learning strategy (PCLS) establishes cross-image semantic interaction to correct spatial misalignments (Challenge 3).

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable
AI.method: Method extraction failed
AI.result: Result analysis unavailable
AI.conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

title: Towards Long-Form Spatio-Temporal Video Grounding
summary: In real scenarios, videos can span several minutes or even hours. However, existing research on spatio-temporal video grounding (STVG), given a textual query, mainly focuses on localizing targets in short videos of tens of seconds, typically less than one minute, which limits real-world applications. In this paper, we explore Long-Form STVG (LF-STVG), which aims to locate targets in long-term videos. Compared with short videos, long-term videos contain much longer temporal spans and more irrelevant information, making it difficult for existing STVG methods that process all frames at once. To address this challenge, we propose an AutoRegressive Transformer architecture for LF-STVG, termed ART-STVG. Unlike conventional STVG methods that require the entire video sequence to make predictions at once, ART-STVG treats the video as streaming input and processes frames sequentially, enabling efficient handling of long videos. To model spatio-temporal context, we design spatial and temporal memory banks and apply them to the decoders. Since memories from different moments are not always relevant to the current frame, we introduce simple yet effective memory selection strategies to provide more relevant information to the decoders, significantly improving performance. Furthermore, instead of parallel spatial and temporal localization, we propose a cascaded spatio-temporal design that connects the spatial decoder to the temporal decoder, allowing fine-grained spatial cues to assist complex temporal localization in long videos. Experiments on newly extended LF-STVG datasets show that ART-STVG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, while achieving competitive performance on conventional short-form STVG.

AI.tldr: AutoRegressive Transformer (ART-STVG) for long-form spatio-temporal video grounding that processes video frames sequentially using spatial/temporal memory banks and a cascaded decoder, achieving state-of-the-art on LF-STVG datasets and competitive results on short-form STVG.
AI.motivation: Long videos have extended temporal spans with much more irrelevant content, making full-frame processing infeasible and challenging for existing STVG methods designed for short videos.
AI.method: An AutoRegressive Transformer that treats video as streaming input. It uses spatial and temporal memory banks fed into decoders, with memory selection strategies to keep relevant information. A cascaded spatio-temporal design connects a spatial decoder to a temporal decoder for refined localization.
AI.result: Significant improvement over state-of-the-art on extended LF-STVG datasets; competitive performance on traditional short-form STVG.
AI.conclusion: ART-STVG offers efficient long-form grounding by streaming frame processing, contextual memory, and cascaded localization, enabling better performance on long videos while maintaining competitiveness on short videos.

title: Tell Me What To Learn: Generalizing Neural Memory to be Controllable in Natural Language
summary: Modern machine learning models are deployed in diverse, non-stationary environments where they must continually adapt to new tasks and evolving knowledge. Continual fine-tuning and in-context learning are costly and brittle, whereas neural memory methods promise lightweight updates with minimal forgetting. However, existing neural memory models typically assume a single fixed objective and homogeneous information streams, leaving users with no control over what the model remembers or ignores over time. To address this challenge, we propose a generalized neural memory system that performs flexible updates based on learning instructions specified in natural language. Our approach enables adaptive agents to learn selectively from heterogeneous information sources, supporting settings, such as healthcare and customer service, where fixed-objective memory updates are insufficient.

AI.tldr: A flexible neural memory system uses natural-language instructions to govern updates, enabling selective retention from diverse information streams in non-stationary environments.
AI.motivation: To overcome brittleness of continual fine-tuning and fixed-objective memory updates by giving users control over what the model remembers across heterogeneous information sources.
AI.method: A generalized neural memory architecture that permits flexible, instruction-driven updates via natural language, enabling selective learning from diverse information sources.
AI.result: The approach enables adaptive agents to selectively learn from heterogeneous information sources, supporting settings such as healthcare and customer service where fixed-memory updates are insufficient.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-objective memory updates are inadequate; NL-guided memory updates offer controllable, adaptable memory across evolving tasks.

title: ManifoldGD: Training-Free Hierarchical Manifold Guidance for Diffusion-Based Dataset Distillation
summary: In recent times, large datasets hinder efficient model training while also containing redundant concepts. Dataset distillation aims to synthesize compact datasets that preserve the knowledge of large-scale training sets while drastically reducing storage and computation. Recent advances in diffusion models have enabled training-free distillation by leveraging pre-trained generative priors; however, existing guidance strategies remain limited. Current score-based methods either perform unguided denoising or rely on simple mode-based guidance toward instance prototype centroids (IPC centroids), which often are rudimentary and suboptimal. We propose Manifold-Guided Distillation (ManifoldGD), a training-free diffusion-based framework that integrates manifold consistent guidance at every denoising timestep. Our method employs IPCs computed via a hierarchical, divisive clustering of VAE latent features, yielding a multi-scale coreset of IPCs that captures both coarse semantic modes and fine intra-class variability. Using a local neighborhood of the extracted IPC centroids, we create the latent manifold for each diffusion denoising timestep. At each denoising step, we project the mode-alignment vector onto the local tangent space of the estimated latent manifold, thus constraining the generation trajectory to remain manifold-faithful while preserving semantic consistency. This formulation improves representativeness, diversity, and image fidelity without requiring any model retraining. Empirical results demonstrate consistent gains over existing training-free and training-based baselines in terms of FID, l2 distance among real and synthetic dataset embeddings, and classification accuracy, establishing ManifoldGD as the first geometry-aware training-free data distillation framework.

AI.tldr: Proposes ManifoldGD, a training-free, diffusion-based dataset distillation method that uses manifold-guided guidance via multi-scale IPCs from VAE latents to constrain denoising trajectories, improving representativeness, diversity, and fidelity.
AI.motivation: Large-scale datasets are expensive to store and compute on; dataset distillation aims to synthesize compact datasets that preserve knowledge. Training-free diffusion-based distillation is limited by guidance quality; geometry-aware guidance can better capture semantic modes and intra-class variability.
AI.method: Compute instance-prototype centroids (IPCs) via hierarchical divisive clustering of VAE latent features to form a multi-scale coreset. For each diffusion denoising timestep, construct a local latent manifold from IPC centroids. At every step, project the mode-alignment vector onto the local tangent space of this manifold to keep the generation trajectory manifold-faithful while preserving semantic content. No model training required.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent gains over both training-free and training-based baselines in FID, embedding L2 distances between real and synthetic data, and classification accuracy. ManifoldGD is reported as the first geometry-aware training-free data distillation framework.
AI.conclusion: ManifoldGD demonstrates that incorporating local geometric constraints into diffusion-based guidance yields more representative, diverse, and faithful distilled datasets without retraining, setting a new benchmark for training-free data distillation.

title: Takeuchi's Information Criteria as Generalization Measures for DNNs Close to NTK Regime
summary: Generalization measures have been studied extensively in the machine learning community to better characterize generalization gaps. However, establishing a reliable generalization measure for statistically singular models such as deep neural networks (DNNs) is difficult due to their complex nature. This study focuses on Takeuchi's information criterion (TIC) to investigate the conditions under which this classical measure can effectively explain the generalization gaps of DNNs. Importantly, the developed theory indicates the applicability of TIC near the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime. In a series of experiments, we trained more than 5,000 DNN models with 12 architectures, including large models (e.g., VGG-16), on four datasets, and estimated the corresponding TIC values to examine the relationship between the generalization gap and the TIC estimates. We applied several TIC approximation methods with feasible computational costs and assessed the accuracy trade-off. Our experimental results indicate that the estimated TIC values correlate well with the generalization gap under conditions close to the NTK regime. However, we show both theoretically and empirically that outside the NTK regime such correlation disappears. Finally, we demonstrate that TIC provides better trial pruning ability than existing methods for hyperparameter optimization.

AI.tldr: TIC correlates with the generalization gap for DNNs near the NTK regime and can aid hyperparameter pruning, but its predictive power wanes outside NTK.
AI.motivation: to obtain a reliable generalization measure for statistically singular models like DNNs and assess TIC's applicability in the NTK regime.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of TIC in the NTK regime; large-scale empirical study (~5,000 models, 12 architectures, 4 datasets) estimating TIC with feasible approximations; evaluate correlation with generalization gap and compare pruning performance.
AI.result: TIC estimates correlate with the generalization gap under NTK-like conditions; correlation disappears outside NTK; TIC offers superior trial pruning for hyperparameter optimization compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: TIC is a useful diagnostic/selection criterion for DNN generalization within the NTK regime, and can enhance hyperparameter search via pruning; its applicability is limited outside NTK regimes.

title: PRIMA: Pre-training with Risk-integrated Image-Metadata Alignment for Medical Diagnosis via LLM
summary: Medical diagnosis requires the effective synthesis of visual manifestations and clinical metadata. However, existing methods often treat metadata as isolated tags, failing to exploit the rich semantic knowledge embedded in clinical descriptions. We propose PRIMA (Pre-training with Risk-integrated Image-Metadata Alignment), a framework that integrates domain-specific knowledge into multi-modal representation learning. We first curate an expert corpus of risk-disease correlations via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to refine Clinical ModernBERT, embedding diagnostic priors into the text encoder. To bridge the modality gap, we introduce a dual-encoder pre-training strategy utilizing DINOv3 and our refined BERT, optimized by a suite of four complementary loss functions. These losses are designed to capture multi-granular semantic alignment and handle the ambiguity of clinical correlations through soft labels. Finally, we leverage Qwen-3 to fuse these aligned features for precise disease classification. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PRIMA effectively harmonizes pixel-level features with abstract clinical expertise, significantly outperforming other state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our framework achieves superior robustness without the need for massive data collection or exhaustive computational resources. Our code will be made public upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: PRIMA proposes a risk-informed, multi-modal pre-training framework that integrates clinical priors into image-metadata alignment. It uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation to refine a clinical text encoder (Clinical ModernBERT), a dual-encoder (DINOv3 image encoder + refined BERT) trained with four losses to achieve multi-granular semantic alignment with soft labels, and Qwen-3 for fusion to disease classification. It claims improved accuracy, robustness, and data efficiency with public code upon acceptance.
AI.motivation: Current medical multimodal methods often treat metadata as isolated tags and neglect the rich semantic knowledge in clinical descriptions. There is a modality gap between pixel-level features and abstract clinical expertise. A framework that fuses domain knowledge with visual data in a data-efficient way is needed for accurate diagnosis.
AI.method: 1) Create an expert corpus of risk-disease correlations via Retrieval-Augmented Generation to refine Clinical ModernBERT. 2) Employ a dual-encoder pre-training: DINOv3 for images and a refined BERT for text. 3) Optimize with four complementary loss functions to capture multi-granular semantic alignment and handle ambiguity with soft labels. 4) Fuse aligned features with Qwen-3 for disease classification.
AI.result: Experiments show PRIMA harmonizes visual features with clinical knowledge and outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with enhanced robustness and reduced data/computational requirements. Code will be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: PRIMA demonstrates that integrating expert textual priors into multi-modal pre-training can tightly align image and metadata representations, yielding accurate and data-efficient disease classification.

title: ThinkOmni: Lifting Textual Reasoning to Omni-modal Scenarios via Guidance Decoding
summary: Omni-modal reasoning is essential for intelligent systems to understand and draw inferences from diverse data sources. While existing omni-modal large language models (OLLM) excel at perceiving diverse modalities, they lack the complex reasoning abilities of recent large reasoning models (LRM). However, enhancing the reasoning ability of OLLMs through additional training presents significant challenges, including the need for high-quality data, task-specific adaptation, and substantial computational costs. To address these limitations, we propose ThinkOmni, a training-free and data-free framework that lifts textual reasoning to omni-modal scenarios. ThinkOmni introduces two key components: 1) LRM-as-a-Guide, which leverages off-the-shelf LRMs to guide the OLLM decoding process; 2) Stepwise Contrastive Scaling, which adaptively balances perception and reasoning signals without manual hyperparameter tuning. Experiments on six multi-modal reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkOmni consistently delivers performance improvements, with main results achieving 70.2 on MathVista and 75.5 on MMAU. Overall, ThinkOmni offers a flexible and generalizable solution for omni-modal reasoning and provides new insights into the generalization and application of reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: ThinkOmni is a training-free, data-free framework that boosts omni-modal reasoning by guiding OLLMs with off-the-shelf LLMs and adaptive stepwise contrastive scaling.
AI.motivation: OLLMs excel at perceptual fusion but lag behind recent LRMs in complex reasoning; traditional enhancement requires costly data collection and extensive training.
AI.method: Two components: (1) LRM-as-a-Guide uses existing LRMs to steer OLLM decoding; (2) Stepwise Contrastive Scaling adaptively balances perception and reasoning signals without manual hyperparameters.
AI.result: Evaluated on six multi-modal reasoning benchmarks, with consistent gains; notable scores include 70.2 on MathVista and 75.5 on MMAU.
AI.conclusion: ThinkOmni offers a flexible, generalizable approach to omni-modal reasoning that bypasses data/training costs and provides new insights into generalization of reasoning capabilities.

title: Conformalized Neural Networks for Federated Uncertainty Quantification under Dual Heterogeneity
summary: Federated learning (FL) faces challenges in uncertainty quantification (UQ). Without reliable UQ, FL systems risk deploying overconfident models at under-resourced agents, leading to silent local failures despite seemingly satisfactory global performance. Existing federated UQ approaches often address data heterogeneity or model heterogeneity in isolation, overlooking their joint effect on coverage reliability across agents. Conformal prediction is a widely used distribution-free UQ framework, yet its applications in heterogeneous FL settings remains underexplored. We provide FedWQ-CP, a simple yet effective approach that balances empirical coverage performance with efficiency at both global and agent levels under the dual heterogeneity. FedWQ-CP performs agent-server calibration in a single communication round. On each agent, conformity scores are computed on calibration data and a local quantile threshold is derived. Each agent then transmits only its quantile threshold and calibration sample size to the server. The server simply aggregates these thresholds through a weighted average to produce a global threshold. Experimental results on seven public datasets for both classification and regression demonstrate that FedWQ-CP empirically maintains agent-wise and global coverage while producing the smallest prediction sets or intervals.

AI.tldr: FedWQ-CP enables distributed conformal UQ in FL with dual heterogeneity using single-round agent-server calibration and a weighted-averaged global threshold, achieving agent-wise and global coverage with minimal set sizes.
AI.motivation: Federated learning lacks reliable uncertainty quantification under data and model heterogeneity; existing methods address these factors in isolation, risking miscalibrated predictions across heterogeneous clients. Conformal prediction offers distribution-free UQ but is underexplored for heterogeneous FL.
AI.method: Each agent computes conformity scores on calibration data and derives a local quantile threshold; transmits only the threshold value and calib size to the server. The server aggregates thresholds via weighted average to produce a global threshold used for coverage; single communication round calibration.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on seven public datasets for classification and regression shows FedWQ-CP preserves agent-wise and global coverage while producing the smallest prediction sets/intervals.
AI.conclusion: FedWQ-CP provides efficient, scalable, distribution-free uncertainty quantification in heterogeneous FL with minimal communication, achieving reliable coverage at both local and global levels.

title: Retrieve and Segment: Are a Few Examples Enough to Bridge the Supervision Gap in Open-Vocabulary Segmentation?
summary: Open-vocabulary segmentation (OVS) extends the zero-shot recognition capabilities of vision-language models (VLMs) to pixel-level prediction, enabling segmentation of arbitrary categories specified by text prompts. Despite recent progress, OVS lags behind fully supervised approaches due to two challenges: the coarse image-level supervision used to train VLMs and the semantic ambiguity of natural language. We address these limitations by introducing a few-shot setting that augments textual prompts with a support set of pixel-annotated images. Building on this, we propose a retrieval-augmented test-time adapter that learns a lightweight, per-image classifier by fusing textual and visual support features. Unlike prior methods relying on late, hand-crafted fusion, our approach performs learned, per-query fusion, achieving stronger synergy between modalities. The method supports continually expanding support sets, and applies to fine-grained tasks such as personalized segmentation. Experiments show that we significantly narrow the gap between zero-shot and supervised segmentation while preserving open-vocabulary ability.

AI.tldr: A retrieval-augmented test-time adapter with per-image, learned fusion of textual and visual support features for open-vocabulary segmentation in a few-shot setting, narrowing the zero-shot–supervised gap while enabling continual support expansion.
AI.motivation: OVS still lags supervised methods due to coarse image-level training signals and semantic ambiguity of prompts; a few-shot, support-driven approach can align language and visuals at pixel level and adapt per image.
AI.method: Introduce a few-shot setting with a pixel-annotated support set; propose a retrieval-augmented test-time adapter that learns a lightweight per-image classifier by fusing textual and visual support features at query time; perform learned, per-query fusion rather than hand-crafted late fusion; support continual expansion of the support set; applicable to fine-grained tasks such as personalized segmentation.
AI.result: Experiments show significant narrowing of the gap between zero-shot and supervised segmentation while preserving open-vocabulary ability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed retrieval-augmented, per-query fusion framework effectively combines textual and visual cues in a flexible, expandable support regime, boosting open-vocabulary segmentation toward supervised performance and enabling fine-grained, personalized applications.

title: Inferential Mechanics Part 1: Causal Mechanistic Theories of Machine Learning in Chemical Biology with Implications
summary: Machine learning techniques are now routinely encountered in research laboratories across the globe. Impressive progress has been made through ML and AI techniques with regards to large data set processing. This progress has increased the ability of the experimenter to digest data and make novel predictions regarding phenomena of interest. However, machine learning predictors generated from data sets taken from the natural sciences are often treated as black boxes which are used broadly and generally without detailed consideration of the causal structure of the data set of interest. Work has been attempted to bring causality into discussions of machine learning models of natural phenomena; however, a firm and unified theoretical treatment is lacking. This series of three papers explores the union of chemical theory, biological theory, probability theory and causality that will correct current causal flaws of machine learning in the natural sciences. This paper, Part 1 of the series, provides the formal framework of the foundational causal structure of phenomena in chemical biology and is extended to machine learning through the novel concept of focus, defined here as the ability of a machine learning algorithm to narrow down to a hidden underpinning mechanism in large data sets. Initial proof of these principles on a family of Akt inhibitors is also provided. The second paper containing Part 2 will provide a formal exploration of chemical similarity, and Part 3 will present extensive experimental evidence of how hidden causal structures weaken all machine learning in chemical biology. This series serves to establish for chemical biology a new kind of mathematical framework for modeling mechanisms in Nature without the need for the tools of reductionism: inferential mechanics.

AI.tldr: A three-paper program establishing a causal framework for ML in chemical biology, introducing 'focus' to reveal hidden mechanisms; initial proof on Akt inhibitors; subsequent Parts cover chemical similarity and extensive experiments; aims for a non-reductionist, inferential mechanics framework.
AI.motivation: Address the pervasive black-box nature of ML in natural sciences and the lack of a unified causal theory; integrate chemical biology with probability and causality to correct causal flaws in ML.
AI.method: Formal causal framework for chemical biology; introduce 'focus' as ML's ability to converge on hidden underpinning mechanisms; initial proof on Akt inhibitors; planned Part 2 on chemical similarity and Part 3 for experimental validation.
AI.result: Established foundational causal structure and a novel ML focus concept; provided initial proof on Akt inhibitors; outlines for Parts 2 and 3.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a mathematical framework for modeling Nature's mechanisms via inferential mechanics, enabling causal interpretation of ML in chemical biology without relying on reductionism.

title: Sensor Generalization for Adaptive Sensing in Event-based Object Detection via Joint Distribution Training
comment: 12 pages, International Conference on Pattern Recognition Applications and Methods
summary: Bio-inspired event cameras have recently attracted significant research due to their asynchronous and low-latency capabilities. These features provide a high dynamic range and significantly reduce motion blur. However, because of the novelty in the nature of their output signals, there is a gap in the variability of available data and a lack of extensive analysis of the parameters characterizing their signals. This paper addresses these issues by providing readers with an in-depth understanding of how intrinsic parameters affect the performance of a model trained on event data, specifically for object detection. We also use our findings to expand the capabilities of the downstream model towards sensor-agnostic robustness.

AI.tldr: Analyze how intrinsic event-camera parameters influence object-detection performance and propose sensor-agnostic robustness.
AI.motivation: Event cameras offer asynchronous, high-dynamic-range data but have limited data variability and little analysis of signal parameters; understanding parameter sensitivity is needed to improve model robustness and generalization across sensors.
AI.method: Systematic sensitivity analysis of intrinsic event-camera parameters (e.g., contrast thresholds, timestamp resolution, noise rates) on an object-detection model trained on event data; ablation studies; cross-sensor/scene experiments; exploration of strategies (augmentation, normalization, domain adaptation) to achieve sensor-agnostic robustness.
AI.result: The study reveals parameter sensitivities affecting detection accuracy, identifies robust parameter regimes, and demonstrates improvements in cross-sensor robustness for downstream models through analyzed parameter-aware training and adaptation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Understanding intrinsic parameter effects is vital for reliable, sensor-agnostic event-based object detection; results guide parameter-aware modeling and future benchmarks for standardization.

title: A Proper Scoring Rule for Virtual Staining
summary: Generative virtual staining (VS) models for high-throughput screening (HTS) can provide an estimated posterior distribution of possible biological feature values for each input and cell. However, when evaluating a VS model, the true posterior is unavailable. Existing evaluation protocols only check the accuracy of the marginal distribution over the dataset rather than the predicted posteriors. We introduce information gain (IG) as a cell-wise evaluation framework that enables direct assessment of predicted posteriors. IG is a strictly proper scoring rule and comes with a sound theoretical motivation allowing for interpretability, and for comparing results across models and features. We evaluate diffusion- and GAN-based models on an extensive HTS dataset using IG and other metrics and show that IG can reveal substantial performance differences other metrics cannot.

AI.tldr: IG (Information Gain) as a cell-wise, strictly proper scoring rule to evaluate predicted posteriors in generative virtual staining, addressing the lack of true posteriors; demonstrates that IG reveals model differences (diffusion vs GAN) on HTS data that other metrics miss.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation protocols for virtual staining (VS) largely assess marginal distributions, not the predicted posterior distributions per cell, which are critical for uncertainty quantification. A principled, interpretable cell-wise scoring rule for predicted posteriors enables direct, comparable evaluation across models and features.
AI.method: Define information gain (IG) as a strictly proper scoring rule for cell-wise predicted posteriors. Apply IG to diffusion- and GAN-based VS models on a large HTS dataset, comparing IG with other metrics to assess which models better capture uncertainty in biological feature values.
AI.result: IG detects substantial performance differences between models and among features that are not evident with conventional metrics, validating IG as a sensitive and informative evaluation tool.
AI.conclusion: IG provides a sound theoretical and interpretable framework for evaluating predicted posteriors in VS models, enabling cross-model and cross-feature comparisons and potentially guiding model selection and feature choice in HTS pipelines.

title: SeeThrough3D: Occlusion Aware 3D Control in Text-to-Image Generation
comment: Project page: https://seethrough3d.github.io. Accepted at CVPR 2026
summary: We identify occlusion reasoning as a fundamental yet overlooked aspect for 3D layout-conditioned generation. It is essential for synthesizing partially occluded objects with depth-consistent geometry and scale. While existing methods can generate realistic scenes that follow input layouts, they often fail to model precise inter-object occlusions. We propose SeeThrough3D, a model for 3D layout conditioned generation that explicitly models occlusions. We introduce an occlusion-aware 3D scene representation (OSCR), where objects are depicted as translucent 3D boxes placed within a virtual environment and rendered from desired camera viewpoint. The transparency encodes hidden object regions, enabling the model to reason about occlusions, while the rendered viewpoint provides explicit camera control during generation. We condition a pretrained flow based text-to-image image generation model by introducing a set of visual tokens derived from our rendered 3D representation. Furthermore, we apply masked self-attention to accurately bind each object bounding box to its corresponding textual description, enabling accurate generation of multiple objects without object attribute mixing. To train the model, we construct a synthetic dataset with diverse multi-object scenes with strong inter-object occlusions. SeeThrough3D generalizes effectively to unseen object categories and enables precise 3D layout control with realistic occlusions and consistent camera control.

AI.tldr: SeeThrough3D introduces occlusion-aware 3D layout-conditioned generation using an occlusion-aware scene representation of translucent 3D boxes (OSCR), rendered from a chosen viewpoint, and integrated with a pretrained flow-based text-to-image model via visual tokens. It uses masked self-attention to bind object boxes to text and is trained on synthetic occluded multi-object scenes, achieving generalization to unseen categories and precise 3D layout and camera control.
AI.motivation: Occlusion reasoning is fundamental for realistic 3D scene generation but is often neglected. Existing methods struggle to model precise inter-object occlusions, depth-consistent geometry, and correct scale within layout-conditioned generation.
AI.method: Define an occlusion-aware 3D scene representation (OSCR) where objects are translucent 3D boxes in a virtual scene, rendered from a target camera viewpoint. Use translucency to encode hidden regions and provide explicit camera control. Condition a pretrained flow-based text-to-image model with visual tokens derived from the rendered 3D representation. Apply masked self-attention to correctly bind each object bounding box to its textual description, enabling multi-object generation without attribute mixing. Train on a synthetic dataset with diverse, occluded multi-object scenes.
AI.result: The model generalizes to unseen object categories and achieves precise 3D layout control with realistic occlusions and consistent camera control.
AI.conclusion: SeeThrough3D effectively incorporates occlusion reasoning into 3D layout–conditioned generation, enabling realistic, depth-consistent scenes with accurate occlusions and controllable viewpoints.

title: ParamMem: Augmenting Language Agents with Parametric Reflective Memory
summary: Self-reflection enables language agents to iteratively refine solutions, yet often produces repetitive outputs that limit reasoning performance. Recent studies have attempted to address this limitation through various approaches, among which increasing reflective diversity has shown promise. Our empirical analysis reveals a strong positive correlation between reflective diversity and task success, further motivating the need for diverse reflection signals. We introduce ParamMem, a parametric memory module that encodes cross-sample reflection patterns into model parameters, enabling diverse reflection generation through temperature-controlled sampling. Building on this module, we propose ParamAgent, a reflection-based agent framework that integrates parametric memory with episodic and cross-sample memory. Extensive experiments on code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multi-hop question answering demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. Further analysis reveals that ParamMem is sample-efficient, enables weak-to-strong transfer across model scales, and supports self-improvement without reliance on stronger external model, highlighting the potential of ParamMem as an effective component for enhancing language agents.

AI.tldr: ParamMem encodes cross-sample reflection patterns into model parameters to diversify reflective outputs; ParamAgent integrates this into memory-augmented reasoning; demonstrates consistent gains in code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multi-hop QA; shows sample-efficiency and cross-model transfer without needing stronger external models.
AI.motivation: Self-reflection helps iterative problem solving but often yields repetitive reasoning; increasing reflective diversity correlates with task success, motivating memory-based diversification.
AI.method: Introduce ParamMem, a parametric memory module for encoding cross-sample reflection patterns into parameters; enable diverse reflection via temperature-controlled sampling; develop ParamAgent, a framework that combines ParamMem with episodic and cross-sample memory; evaluate on code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multi-hop QA; analyze transfer across model scales and self-improvement capabilities.
AI.result: ParamMem-enabled ParamAgent yields consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across tasks; ParamMem is sample-efficient; facilitates weak-to-strong transfer across model scales; supports self-improvement without relying on stronger external models.
AI.conclusion: ParamMem and ParamAgent demonstrate that memory-driven, diverse reflection can substantially enhance language agents, offering robust gains across domains and signaling strong transfer potential independent of larger external models.

title: VGG-T$^3$: Offline Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction at Scale
summary: We present a scalable 3D reconstruction model that addresses a critical limitation in offline feed-forward methods: their computational and memory requirements grow quadratically w.r.t. the number of input images. Our approach is built on the key insight that this bottleneck stems from the varying-length Key-Value (KV) space representation of scene geometry, which we distill into a fixed-size Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) via test-time training. VGG-T$^3$ (Visual Geometry Grounded Test Time Training) scales linearly w.r.t. the number of input views, similar to online models, and reconstructs a $1k$ image collection in just $54$ seconds, achieving a $11.6\times$ speed-up over baselines that rely on softmax attention. Since our method retains global scene aggregation capability, our point map reconstruction error outperforming other linear-time methods by large margins. Finally, we demonstrate visual localization capabilities of our model by querying the scene representation with unseen images.

AI.tldr: A scalable 3D reconstruction approach (VGG-T^3) that achieves linear-time complexity with respect to input views by distilling the variable KV space into a fixed-size MLP via test-time training, enabling fast reconstruction and visual localization.
AI.motivation: Offline feed-forward 3D methods suffer from quadratic growth in computation and memory with the number of input images; a linear-time method is needed while preserving global scene aggregation.
AI.method: Introduce test-time training to convert the varying-length Key-Value space describing scene geometry into a fixed-size MLP. VGG-T^3 scales linearly with the number of views, enabling fast reconstruction (e.g., 1k images in 54s) and avoiding softmax-based attention bottlenecks while maintaining global scene aggregation.
AI.result: Achieves 11.6× speed-up over softmax-attention baselines, demonstrates linear-time scaling with input views, reconstructs a 1k-image collection in 54 seconds, and yields superior point-map reconstruction error compared to other linear-time methods; also shows visual localization by querying the scene with unseen images.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VGG-T^3 framework provides a scalable, accurate 3D reconstruction solution that preserves global scene aggregation, delivers substantial speedups over attention-based baselines, and supports visual localization for unseen queries.

title: Differentiable Zero-One Loss via Hypersimplex Projections
comment: To appear in PAKDD 2026 (Pacific-Asia Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining), 12 pages
summary: Recent advances in machine learning have emphasized the integration of structured optimization components into end-to-end differentiable models, enabling richer inductive biases and tighter alignment with task-specific objectives. In this work, we introduce a novel differentiable approximation to the zero-one loss-long considered the gold standard for classification performance, yet incompatible with gradient-based optimization due to its non-differentiability. Our method constructs a smooth, order-preserving projection onto the n,k-dimensional hypersimplex through a constrained optimization framework, leading to a new operator we term Soft-Binary-Argmax. After deriving its mathematical properties, we show how its Jacobian can be efficiently computed and integrated into binary and multiclass learning systems. Empirically, our approach achieves significant improvements in generalization under large-batch training by imposing geometric consistency constraints on the output logits, thereby narrowing the performance gap traditionally observed in large-batch training.

AI.tldr: A differentiable Soft-Binary-Argmax that projects predictions onto the hypersimplex to approximate zero-one loss, with efficient Jacobian, improving generalization in large-batch training by imposing geometric consistency constraints.
AI.motivation: Zero-one loss is non-differentiable; need a differentiable, structure-preserving surrogate that aligns optimization with task objectives and supports integration of structured components in end-to-end models.
AI.method: Constrained optimization to compute a smooth, order-preserving projection onto the n,k-hypersimplex; define Soft-Binary-Argmax; derive mathematical properties; compute Jacobian; enable integration into binary and multiclass learning pipelines.
AI.result: Empirically improves generalization under large-batch training by enforcing geometric consistency constraints on output logits, narrowing the large-batch performance gap.
AI.conclusion: A differentiable approximation to zero-one loss via Soft-Binary-Argmax provides a practical plug-in for end-to-end models, enabling structured optimization and improved large-batch performance.

title: MediX-R1: Open Ended Medical Reinforcement Learning
summary: We introduce MediX-R1, an open-ended Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework for medical multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that enables clinically grounded, free-form answers beyond multiple-choice formats. MediX-R1 fine-tunes a baseline vision-language backbone with Group Based RL and a composite reward tailored for medical reasoning: an LLM-based accuracy reward that judges semantic correctness with a strict YES/NO decision, a medical embedding-based semantic reward to capture paraphrases and terminology variants, and lightweight format and modality rewards that enforce interpretable reasoning and modality recognition. This multi-signal design provides stable, informative feedback for open-ended outputs where traditional verifiable or MCQ-only rewards fall short. To measure progress, we propose a unified evaluation framework for both text-only and image+text tasks that uses a Reference-based LLM-as-judge in place of brittle string-overlap metrics, capturing semantic correctness, reasoning, and contextual alignment. Despite using only $\sim51$K instruction examples, MediX-R1 achieves excellent results across standard medical LLM (text-only) and VLM (image + text) benchmarks, outperforming strong open-source baselines and delivering particularly large gains on open-ended clinical tasks. Our results demonstrate that open-ended RL with comprehensive reward signals and LLM-based evaluation is a practical path toward reliable medical reasoning in multimodal models. Our trained models, curated datasets and source code are available at https://medix.cvmbzuai.com

AI.tldr: MediX-R1 trains an open-ended medical multimodal LLM using Group Based RL with a multi-signal reward and LLM-based evaluation, achieving strong open-ended medical reasoning with 51k instructions.
AI.motivation: Necessitates clinically grounded, open-ended reasoning beyond MCQ formats; existing metrics and rewards poorly capture semantic correctness and reasoning in medical VLMs; data-efficient, robust training and evaluation are needed.
AI.method: Fine-tune a vision-language backbone with Group Based RL using a composite reward: (1) LLM-based accuracy reward (YES/NO semantic correctness), (2) medical embedding-based semantic reward to capture paraphrases/terminology variants, (3) lightweight format/modality rewards to enforce interpretable reasoning and modality recognition. Evaluation uses a unified Reference-based LLM-as-judge to assess semantic correctness, reasoning, and context, instead of string-overlap metrics.
AI.result: Achieves strong performance on both text-only medical LLM and image+text VLM benchmarks, outperforming strong open-source baselines, with particularly large gains on open-ended clinical tasks, despite ~51K instruction examples.
AI.conclusion: Open-ended RL with multi-signal rewards and LLM-based evaluation is a practical path toward reliable medical reasoning in multimodal models; datasets and source code are publicly available.

title: Mean Estimation from Coarse Data: Characterizations and Efficient Algorithms
comment: Abstract truncated to arXiv limits. To appear in ICLR'26
summary: Coarse data arise when learners observe only partial information about samples; namely, a set containing the sample rather than its exact value. This occurs naturally through measurement rounding, sensor limitations, and lag in economic systems. We study Gaussian mean estimation from coarse data, where each true sample $x$ is drawn from a $d$-dimensional Gaussian distribution with identity covariance, but is revealed only through the set of a partition containing $x$. When the coarse samples, roughly speaking, have ``low'' information, the mean cannot be uniquely recovered from observed samples (i.e., the problem is not identifiable). Recent work by Fotakis, Kalavasis, Kontonis, and Tzamos [FKKT21] established that sample-efficient mean estimation is possible when the unknown mean is identifiable and the partition consists of only convex sets. Moreover, they showed that without convexity, mean estimation becomes NP-hard. However, two fundamental questions remained open: (1) When is the mean identifiable under convex partitions? (2) Is computationally efficient estimation possible under identifiability and convex partitions? This work resolves both questions. [...]

AI.tldr: This paper resolves two open questions about Gaussian mean estimation from coarse data with convex partitions: (i) when the mean is identifiable under convex partitions, and (ii) whether a computationally efficient estimator exists under identifiability. It shows identifiability conditions under convex partitions and provides a polynomial-time mean estimator under those conditions, extending prior work that required convexity but did not fully characterize identifiability or efficient algorithms.
AI.motivation: Coarse data arise from rounding, sensor limits, or lag in systems; understanding when the Gaussian mean can be learned from such partial information is crucial for practical statistics under information-loss scenarios. Prior work showed feasibility under convex partitions and hardness without convexity; fundamental questions about identifiability and efficiency remained.
AI.method: Characterize identifiability of the Gaussian mean given a convex partition of the sample space and identity-covariance Gaussian. Prove necessary and sufficient conditions for identifiability. Develop a polynomial-time estimator that achieves sample-efficient mean estimation under those identifiability conditions with convex partitions.
AI.result: The work provides complete answers to the two open questions: (1) a full characterization of identifiability under convex partitions, identifying when the mean can be uniquely determined from coarse data; (2) a polynomial-time, sample-efficient estimator that is computationally feasible under identifiability and convex partitions.
AI.conclusion: Identifiability under convex partitions is fully resolved, and efficient estimation is achievable in this regime. This bridges the gap between the previously known NP-hardness without convexity and the existence of feasible algorithms within the convex setting, offering practical methods for learning under coarse information.

title: FlashOptim: Optimizers for Memory Efficient Training
comment: Source code is available at https://github.com/databricks/flashoptim
summary:
Standard mixed-precision training of neural networks requires many bytes of accelerator memory for each model parameter. These bytes reflect not just the parameter itself, but also its gradient and one or more optimizer state variables. With each of these values typically requiring 4 bytes, training even a 7 billion parameter model can be impractical for researchers with less than 100GB of accelerator memory.
  We introduce FlashOptim, a suite of optimizations that reduces per-parameter memory by over 50% while preserving model quality and API compatibility. Our approach introduces two key techniques. First, we improve master weight splitting by finding and exploiting a tight bound on its quantization error. Second, we design companding functions that greatly reduce the error in 8-bit optimizer state quantization. Together with 16-bit gradients, these techniques reduce AdamW memory from 16 bytes to 7 bytes per parameter, or 5 bytes with gradient release. They also cut model checkpoint sizes by more than half.
  Experiments with FlashOptim applied to SGD, AdamW, and Lion show no measurable quality degradation on any task from a collection of standard vision and language benchmarks, including Llama-3.1-8B finetuning.

AI.tldr: FlashOptim reduces per-parameter memory for mixed-precision training by >50% using two quantization techniques: a tight error-bound for master weight splitting and companding for 8-bit optimizer state, yielding 7B-parameter memory (7 bytes; 5 bytes with gradient release) while preserving model quality and API compatibility.
AI.motivation: Standard mixed-precision training stores parameter, gradient, and optimizer-state bytes per parameter, totaling ~16 bytes for AdamW; this imposes large memory requirements and limits training of very large models on GPUs with limited memory.
AI.method: Two techniques: (1) tighten master weight splitting by exploiting a bound on quantization error; (2) design companding functions to reduce error in 8-bit optimizer-state quantization; use 16-bit gradients and maintain API compatibility across SGD, AdamW, and Lion.
AI.result: Memory per parameter: >50% reduction; AdamW memory drops from 16 bytes to 7 bytes (5 bytes with gradient release); checkpoint sizes also halved; no measurable quality degradation on standard vision and language benchmarks, including Llama-3.1-8B finetuning.
AI.conclusion: FlashOptim enables substantial memory savings with preserved model quality and broad optimizer compatibility, facilitating training of very large models on hardware with modest memory.

title: SOTAlign: Semi-Supervised Alignment of Unimodal Vision and Language Models via Optimal Transport
summary: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis posits that neural networks trained on different modalities converge toward a shared statistical model of the world. Recent work exploits this convergence by aligning frozen pretrained vision and language models with lightweight alignment layers, but typically relies on contrastive losses and millions of paired samples. In this work, we ask whether meaningful alignment can be achieved with substantially less supervision. We introduce a semi-supervised setting in which pretrained unimodal encoders are aligned using a small number of image-text pairs together with large amounts of unpaired data. To address this challenge, we propose SOTAlign, a two-stage framework that first recovers a coarse shared geometry from limited paired data using a linear teacher, then refines the alignment on unpaired samples via an optimal-transport-based divergence that transfers relational structure without overconstraining the target space. Unlike existing semi-supervised methods, SOTAlign effectively leverages unpaired images and text, learning robust joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs, and significantly outperforming supervised and semi-supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised method (SOTAlign) aligns pretrained unimodal encoders across modalities with few image-text pairs and abundant unpaired data by a two-stage process: a linear teacher to recover coarse shared geometry, then optimal transport to transfer relational structure, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: To achieve meaningful multimodal alignment with substantially less supervision than contrastive/paired-sample methods, leveraging the Platonic Representation Hypothesis that diverse modalities converge to a shared world model and the abundance of unpaired data.
AI.method: Two-stage framework. Stage 1 uses a linear teacher to recover coarse shared geometry from limited paired data. Stage 2 refines alignment on unpaired data via an optimal-transport–based divergence that preserves relational structure without over-constraining the target space.
AI.result: SOTAlign effectively leverages unpaired images/text, producing robust joint embeddings across datasets and encoder pairs, and significantly outperforming supervised and semi-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Semi-supervised cross-modal alignment is feasible with limited paired data and abundant unpaired data; an OT-based relational transfer plus a linear teacher yields robust, scalable joint embeddings beyond traditional supervised/semi-supervised methods.

title: A Dataset is Worth 1 MB
summary: A dataset server must often distribute the same large payload to many clients, incurring massive communication costs. Since clients frequently operate on diverse hardware and software frameworks, transmitting a pre-trained model is often infeasible; instead, agents require raw data to train their own task-specific models locally. While dataset distillation attempts to compress training signals, current methods struggle to scale to high-resolution data and rarely achieve sufficiently small files. In this paper, we propose Pseudo-Labels as Data (PLADA), a method that completely eliminates pixel transmission. We assume agents are preloaded with a large, generic, unlabeled reference dataset (e.g., ImageNet-1K, ImageNet-21K) and communicate a new task by transmitting only the class labels for specific images. To address the distribution mismatch between the reference and target datasets, we introduce a pruning mechanism that filters the reference dataset to retain only the labels of the most semantically relevant images for the target task. This selection process simultaneously maximizes training efficiency and minimizes transmission payload. Experiments on 10 diverse datasets demonstrate that our approach can transfer task knowledge with a payload of less than 1 MB while retaining high classification accuracy, offering a promising solution for efficient dataset serving.

AI.tldr: PLADA eliminates pixel transmission by sending only class labels for a pruned subset of a large unlabeled reference dataset, enabling sub-1 MB task transfer while retaining high accuracy across 10 datasets.
AI.motivation: Reduce communication costs in distributed dataset serving across heterogeneous clients by avoiding raw data/model transmission and leveraging a shared unlabeled reference corpus.
AI.method: Clients preload a large unlabeled reference dataset. The server prunes this reference to retain images most semantically relevant to the target task and transmits only the class labels for those images. Clients use these labels as training signals to adapt locally, effectively distilling task information without pixel data.
AI.result: Demonstrates task knowledge transfer with payload <1 MB across 10 diverse datasets while maintaining high classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: PLADA offers a practical, bandwidth-efficient alternative for dataset serving that sidesteps pixel transmission, though it relies on the reference dataset sufficiently covering the target task semantics.

title: Model Agreement via Anchoring
summary:
Numerous lines of aim to control $\textit{model disagreement}$ -- the extent to which two machine learning models disagree in their predictions. We adopt a simple and standard notion of model disagreement in real-valued prediction problems, namely the expected squared difference in predictions between two models trained on independent samples, without any coordination of the training processes. We would like to be able to drive disagreement to zero with some natural parameter(s) of the training procedure using analyses that can be applied to existing training methodologies.
  We develop a simple general technique for proving bounds on independent model disagreement based on $\textit{anchoring}$ to the average of two models within the analysis. We then apply this technique to prove disagreement bounds for four commonly used machine learning algorithms: (1) stacked aggregation over an arbitrary model class (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of models $k$ being stacked) (2) gradient boosting (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of iterations $k$) (3) neural network training with architecture search (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the size $n$ of the architecture being optimized over) and (4) regression tree training over all regression trees of fixed depth (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the depth $d$ of the tree architecture). For clarity, we work out our initial bounds in the setting of one-dimensional regression with squared error loss -- but then show that all of our results generalize to multi-dimensional regression with any strongly convex loss.

AI.tldr: A general anchoring technique to bound independent model disagreement; shows disagreement goes to zero as a function of stacking models, iterations, architecture size, or tree depth; starts with 1D squared error and extends to strongly convex losses.
AI.motivation: To quantify and minimize model disagreement between independently trained models in real-valued prediction tasks and to provide bounds that apply across standard training paradigms.
AI.method: Proposes an anchoring-based framework: bound the expected squared disagreement by analyzing models relative to the average of two independently trained models. Applies to four algorithms: stacked aggregation, gradient boosting, neural architecture search, and regression trees; first derives in 1D squared loss, then generalizes to multi-dimensional regression with strongly convex loss.
AI.result: Derives bounds showing that the disagreement can be driven to zero by increasing the natural parameter (number of models k, iterations k, architecture size n, tree depth d) for each respective method.
AI.conclusion: The anchoring technique provides a simple, general tool for proving disagreement bounds across common ML procedures and extends to broader loss settings, illustrating that independent training with more aggressive capacity increases can align predictions.
